Additionally, there are requirements on auditoriums, labs, and libraries. Licensing is not time-bound, and universities need to get licensing for each specialty. Licensing also defines the language(s) of instruction to be used, types of programs (full-time and/or part-time) universities can offer, and the maximum number of students they can enroll.

The interest rates on student loans were decreased from 12 percent to 9 percent, and out of this 9 percent rate, 6 percent is to be paid by the student while 3 percent is to be paid by the government. All regular bachelor’s and master’s degree students are eligible for student loans. Since March 2019, clinical residency students are also eligible to receive student loans.

A Standards and Assessment Agency (US$ 3.7 million) - this institution will provide
information to the education community and the public at-large on student achievement and
overall system performance with the aim of learning about the system's strengths and
weaknesses, promoting accountability in public education and measuring compatibility
between educational standards in BiH and the rest of Europe. The Agency will be a shared
inter-Entity institution supplying high quality professional services in the field of student
assessments.

An Education Management Information System (US$ 2.1 million) - this component will
finance development of a BiH-wide EMIS that will enable improved sector management and
compatibility of information among BiH's decentralized education systems. It will also test
the viability of a per student budgeting model designed to promote increased efficiency and
equity in education spending.

reform_title: The Second Emergency Education Rehabilitation Project
reform_description: EMIS modules for education statistics and capitation funding will be piloted under the Second Emergency Education Rehabilitation Project.

A Higher Education Reform (US$ 3.5 million) -- this component is designed to stimulate
professional coordination, innovation, strategic planning and efficient governance at both
sector and institutional levels in higher education in BiH.

reform_title: Creation of a Council of Higher Education and its secretariat
reform_description:
Following creation of a Council of
Higher Education, a secretariat will be established under the Council with terms of reference
to focus on two objectives: I) increased strategic planning and institutional consolidation and
2) improved qualification and accreditation systems.

To provide incentives for strategic
planning, a Higher Education Fund will be established in the amount of $2.0 million.

The project aims to increase cooperation and coordination in education through the creation of
key intermediary institutions and shared management mechanisms, as follows:
* development of a new capacity for standardized student assessment throughout BiH through creation of a shared Standards and Assessment Agency
* development of a shared approach to promoting strategic planning, management consolidation and accreditation issues through creation of a Council of Higher Education
* development of a shared core Education Management Information System across the cantons and RS
* allocation of non-salary incentives for innovations to schools through ajoint (Federation-RS) Quality Fund Board
* development of a capacity to generate and use household level data on education and welfare, along with other issues in the social sectors

the project will support reform at
two levels:
* consolidation of university management in the office of the university (as opposed to individual faculties) and development of new procedures of higher education program accreditation through the Higher Education Fund

the project will support reform at
two levels:
* creating a market mechanism in which schools or groups of schools contract for services, including in-service teacher training, from their choice of service provider through the Quality Fund

reform_title: Nomination of a BiH-wide EMIS Committee
reform_description:
The Federation Minister and Deputy Minister
of Education, Science, Culture and Sports and the RS Minister of Education have nominated a
BiH-wide EMIS Committee for which terms of reference are included in the Project Operational
Manual (POM). The Committee will oversee the results of the two modules to be piloted in the
cantons and play a coordination and consensus building role in the design and implementation of
the roll out of EMIS modules throughout BiH.

The Project will support the establishment of an intermediary
coordination capacity in a newly created Council of Higher Education, and its Technical
Secretariat.
A BiH-wide Board has been nominated to oversee the design and implementation of the Quality Fund

an EMIS Committee has been established to coordinate preparation of a common core
management information mechanism across BiH.

The goal of this component is to mobilize the professional capacity of teachers to
improve the teaching and learning processes in schools, and as a result improve the quality of
education being offered to primary school children in BiH. The component has three specific
objectives: (a) to support improvements in teaching and learning methods used in schools, (b)
to enhance the motivation of teachers and encourage them to engage in in-service training
activities, and (c) to strengthen teacher training institutions, improve the quality of course
offerings and improve their efficiency.

The First Component is the School Grants that support Educational Improvement
Projects (EIP). These are short-term projects (one to two years) funded by the Quality Fund on
a competitive basis. EIPs are designed by teams of teachers in a primary school following
pre-established procedures, which will be detailed in an EIP preparation manual (developed
with Italian Grant Fund financing).
The First Component is the School Grants that support Educational Improvement Projects (EIP).

The EMIS Component contains the following three sub-components:
* the development and implementation of an initial set of education management information system software modules (ISEM);
* the strengthening of the system platforms of all the cantonal Ministries of Education in the Federation and the Ministry of Education of Republic of Srpska, (these will be referred to as the Ministries); and
* the development and delivery of professional management training in information systems for school principals, school accountants and the staff from the Ministries.

reform_title: Project sub-component (development of the initial set of EMIS modules)

reform_description:
The third sub-component is the provision of professional training to the principal and
school accountant of each school in information management. There will be three phases of training: the first phase will be associated with the piloting of the first set of
modules, the second and third phases with the countrywide roll-out of the EMIS.

the project will support: (a) the creation of a Higher Education Council and its
Technical Secretariat; (b) the establishment of the basic principles to develop a standards and
accreditation mechanism; and (c) the establishment of a Higher Education Fund to support
individual universities' capacity for institutional diagnosis, policy and strategy design, and
financial management.

reform_title: RS Ministry of Education Education Strategy
reform_description: The Republika Srpska Ministry of Education produced an Education Strategy setting out broad priorities for improving educational quality across the entity.

reform_title: Establishment of the Standards and Assessment Agency
reform_description: Creation of a new state-level inter-entity institution responsible for defining educational performance standards and conducting standardized student assessments in literacy and mathematics across Bosnia and Herzegovina. The agency was established as a shared professional body accountable to entity prime ministers with a governing board representing ministries of education universities teachers and commerce.

reform_title: Establishment of the Council of Higher Education
reform_description: Creation of a new BiH-wide intermediary coordinating body for higher education responsible for strategic planning institutional consolidation accreditation standards and mutual recognition of degrees across all constituent groups. A technical secretariat was established under the council to manage a Higher Education Fund supporting university institutional development plans.

Development and rollout of a BiH-wide education management information system providing compatible standardized data collection across all cantons and Republika Srpska. The system included software modules for student personnel and financial data a capitation-based funding pilot and management training for school principals and canton-level administrators.

Establishment of a joint Federation-Republika Srpska Quality Fund governed by a shared inter-entity board providing competitive school grants teacher training grants and merit-based scholarships and institutional strengthening grants to teacher training institutions across BiH primary schools with the aim of improving teaching quality and innovation.
Introduction of a new mechanism for textbook selection in the Federation (piloted under the Emergency Education Reconstruction Projects) that established a privatized textbook publishing system.
Adoption of an education strategy by the Republika Srpska Ministry of Education establishing broad priorities for improving educational quality within the Entity.

reform_title: Establishment of the Standards and Assessment Agency
reform_description: Creation of a shared, inter-Entity educational institution to define performance standards, conduct standardized student assessments, and provide data on system performance across Bosnia and Herzegovina.

reform_title: Establishment of the Council of Higher Education
reform_description: Formation of a Council of Higher Education and an associated Higher Education Fund to coordinate higher education, consolidate university management, and develop common institutional standards for accreditation and degree recognition.

reform_title: Establishment of the Education Management Information System (EMIS)
reform_description: Implementation of a common, compatible education management information system across the country to track education inputs, outputs, and outcomes for evidence-based policymaking.

Creation of a decentralized education financing mechanism that provides competitive school-level and institutional grants to improve teaching methods, learning processes, and empower local management of resources.
Implementation of a major EU-Phare donor-financed investment project aimed at developing new curricula for vocational education programs.

Entity-level education strategy setting broad priorities for improving education quality in Republika Srpska, though the report notes it did not fully address socioeconomic, financial, institutional, and capacity constraints.

Creation of a joint Federation-Republika Srpska Quality Fund to finance competitive school grants, teacher training grants and scholarships, and grants to teacher training institutions to improve teaching and learning in primary education.

Establishment of a shared State-level Standards and Assessment Agency to define student performance standards, assess learning outcomes, publish system performance information, and harmonize assessment practices across Bosnia and Herzegovina.

Development of a shared core Education Management Information System across the cantons and Republika Srpska to collect and report education data, improve sector management, and support compatible information across decentralized education systems.

reform_title: Council of Higher Education and Higher Education Fund
reform_description: Creation of a Bosnia and Herzegovina-wide Council of Higher Education, technical secretariat, and Higher Education Fund to coordinate strategic planning, university management consolidation, accreditation procedures, qualification recognition, and higher education institutional reform.

reform_title: Tuzla Canton Per-Student Block Grant Pilot
reform_description: Pilot of per-student block grants for primary and secondary education in Tuzla Canton, designed to test a capitation-based budgeting mechanism that could be extended to other cantons and Republika Srpska.
Pilot mechanism for textbook selection in the Federation that resulted in privatized textbook publishing and improved textbook quality in classrooms.
In 2008, a national law (Law No. 11738/2008) established the minimum wage for teachers, which represented a significant and equitable raise for public school teachers in Brazil.
National legislation officially established that 30 per cent of teachers’ work hours should be devoted to these activities, as enacted in the Teacher Wages Law approved in 2008.
Law No. 11738/2008 mandates that 32.5 per cent of teachers’ working hours should be devoted to activities outside the classrooms.
Law No. 11738/2008 approved in 2008 mandated a minimum wage for teachers, which represents a significant and equitable raise in salary among teachers in Brazil.

Brazil has recently defined its education goals for a period of ten years through its National Education Plan (2014-2024). The plan sets out 20 goals and targets along with broad strategies, including teacher policies.
In addition, the National Education Plan, approved in 2014, determined the development and implementation of minimum standards of quality for all Brazilian schools (CAQi).

The National Education Plan (2014-2024) establishes a strategy to evaluate entry-level professionals. This strategy can be followed by states and municipalities, and it consists of assembling a team of experienced professionals who, in turn, supervise teachers to decide whether to offer them tenure after the probationary period.

Brazil has set new goals and targets to improve teaching quality under the National Education Plan (2014-2024). Among the initiatives are the establishment of a career plan, including professional development activities to be implemented by states and municipalities.

reform_title: 2015 Standards for Pre-service and Inservice Teacher Education
reform_description: The 2015 Standards for Pre-service and Inservice Teacher Education increased the length of programmes from 3 to 4 years and the time devoted to practical training in schools, as well as a set of general principles for the design of teacher initial education programmes. This new legislation also established the knowledge and skills to be developed by the curriculum of teacher training institutions (CNE, 2015).

reform_title: National Program for Training and Certification of School Principals i
reform_description: The federal government did recently launch the National Program for Training and Certification of School Principals in partnership with higher education institutions. Although participation in this programme is not compulsory, it does offer principals the opportunity to develop the necessary skills to succeed in their job (Ministry of Education, 2015).

reform_title: The Education Guidelines and Foundations Act (LDB)
reform_description: The Education Guidelines and Foundations Act (LDB) establishes that the curricula taught in Brazilian schools must follow the national common core and be composed of Portuguese language, mathematics, natural and social sciences, art, physical education and a modern foreign language. The law also stresses that indigenous and African-Brazilian history and culture should be taught in schools. The LDB established a framework to develop the national common core standards for what students must know and be able to do after completing each subject in each grade

reform_title: The Education Guidelines and Foundations Act (LDB)
reform_description: The LDB and other regulations define the tasks teachers are expected to carry out in schools at the national level

Also, the National Plan for Education established that the average salary of teachers should be equivalent to the average salary of other professionals with university level degrees in Brazil by 2024.

reform_title: The minimum quality school standards’ legislation
reform_description: The minimum quality school standards’ legislation established the importance of school infrastructure standards in providing students with an effective learning experience.

reform_title: mandatory annual minimum per pupil expenditure
reform_description: Brazil did establish a mandatory annual minimum per pupil expenditure, which was calculated not on the basis of the estimated resources necessary to achieve a quality education but rather on the funds available in a given year.

In Brazil, the National Education Plan establishes that all teachers should have subject-knowledge training at a higher education level and that 50 per cent of the teachers should receive training at the graduate level.
The National Education Plan established the need for developing more programmes to effectively prepare principals and school leaders

reform_title: National Program for Training and Certification of School Principals i
reform_description: The newly created National Program for Training and Certification of School Principals will aim to develop national standards for school principals and a certification programme for school leaders in order to guide state and municipal governments in their selection of principals

A national low-stakes student learning assessment system testing students in grades 3 5 9 and 12 in public and private schools measuring language and mathematics achievement. Results combined with retention rates form the Basic Education Development Index (IDEB) and school results are made public.

reform_title: Education Guidelines and Foundations Act (LDB) – National Curriculum Framework
reform_description: The LDB established the national common core curriculum framework for Brazilian schools requiring instruction in Portuguese language mathematics natural and social sciences art physical education and a modern foreign language. It also defined teacher tasks beyond teaching including curriculum design grading assessments collaborating on school plans and participating in school evaluations and professional development.

The incorporation of student test scores from SAEB into a school quality index (IDEB) combining language and mathematics scores with retention rates to produce a publicly reported indicator of school quality used to inform education policy at local and federal levels.

reform_title: Institutional Program for Scholarships for Initiation in Teaching (PIBID)
reform_description: A federal government scholarship programme for undergraduate students enrolled in teacher education programmes providing financial support for participation in public school teaching projects to develop pedagogical skills and increase practical classroom experience.

reform_title: Law No. 11738/2008 – National Teacher Minimum Wage Law
reform_description: A national law establishing a minimum wage for public school teachers in Brazil representing a significant and equitable salary increase. The law also mandated that 32.5 percent of teachers' working hours be devoted to activities outside the classroom including planning and collaborative work.

reform_title: Constitutional Amendment on Compulsory School Enrollment (Ages 4–17)
reform_description: A constitutional amendment made it mandatory for children aged 4 to 17 years to be enrolled in school expanding the scope of compulsory basic education in Brazil.

A ten-year national education plan setting 20 goals and targets including teacher policy goals such as requiring all teachers to have subject-knowledge training at higher education level targeting 50 percent of teachers to receive graduate-level training establishing minimum quality standards for schools (CAQi) and mandating that average teacher salaries reach parity with other university-educated professionals by 2024.

A nationally implemented federal literacy programme to train teachers from Grades 1–3 launched in 2014 and financed partly by the Ministry of Education with implementation carried out at state and municipal levels.

reform_title: 2015 Standards for Pre-service and In-service Teacher Education (CNE Resolution 02/2015)
reform_description: New national legislation increasing the minimum length of initial teacher education programmes from 3 to 4 years increasing time devoted to practical training in schools and establishing national curricular guidelines including the knowledge and skills to be developed by teacher training institutions.

reform_title: National Program for Training and Certification of School Principals
reform_description: A federal programme launched by the Ministry of Education in partnership with higher education institutions to develop national standards for school principals and offer voluntary training and certification for school leaders to guide state and municipal governments in principal selection.
Establishment of a national student learning assessment testing 3rd, 5th, 9th, and 12th grade students in public and private schools to measure student achievement and evaluate school quality.

A reform of the National Education Law that defined official teacher tasks, established a framework for national common core standards, and required all basic education teachers to have a university degree.
Creation of a school quality index that incorporates students' language and mathematics test scores from national assessments along with retention rates.

A national law that established a minimum wage for teachers and mandated that 30 to 32.5 percent of teachers' working hours be devoted to activities outside the classroom, such as planning and collaborative work.
A Constitutional Amendment making it mandatory for children aged 4 to 17 years to be enrolled at school.

A ten-year national plan establishing education goals and targets, determining the implementation of minimum quality standards for schools, setting goals for teacher salaries, and outlining strategies for teacher evaluation and career plans.
A nationally implemented professional development program aimed at training teachers for Grades 1 through 3 in literacy.

reform_title: 2015 Standards for Pre-service and In-service Teacher Education
reform_description: New legislation that increased the length of teacher education programs to four years, expanded the time devoted to practical training in schools, and established general principles and required competencies for teacher training curricula.

reform_title: National Program for Training and Certification of School Principals
reform_description: A federal program launched in partnership with higher education institutions to train school principals and develop national standards and a certification program for school leaders.

reform_title: Institutional Program for Scholarships for Initiation in Teaching (PIBID)
reform_description: A federal government scholarship program for undergraduate students enrolled in teacher education programs, providing incentives for them to participate in public school projects to develop pedagogical skills.
National low-stakes student assessment system testing students in selected grades in public and private schools to measure student achievement and evaluate school quality.

National education law establishing a common curricular framework for Brazilian schools, defining official teacher duties beyond classroom teaching, and requiring basic education teachers to hold a university degree.
National school quality index combining students’ language and mathematics assessment scores with retention rates, with school results made public.

National law establishing a minimum wage for public school teachers and requiring a portion of teachers’ working hours to be devoted to activities outside the classroom, including planning and collaborative work.

reform_title: Constitutional Amendment on Compulsory Schooling Ages 4–17
reform_description: Constitutional change making school enrollment mandatory for children and youth aged 4 to 17, with application starting in 2016.

Ten-year national education plan setting 20 goals and broad strategies for Brazil’s education system, including teacher policy goals, salary targets, professional development, principal preparation, minimum quality standards, and school accountability policies.

reform_title: Minimum Standards of Quality for Schools (CAQi)
reform_description: Policy requiring the development and implementation of minimum quality standards for Brazilian schools, including standards related to school infrastructure and working conditions.
National literacy professional development programme launched to train teachers in Grades 1–3 and implemented nationally.

reform_title: 2015 Standards for Pre-service and In-service Teacher Education
reform_description: National teacher education standards increasing teacher preparation programmes from three to four years, requiring 3,200 hours of preparation, expanding school-based practical training, and defining curriculum principles and required teacher knowledge and skills.

reform_title: National Program for Training and Certification of School Principals
reform_description: Federal programme launched with higher education institutions to develop national standards and a certification programme for school principals and guide state and municipal selection of school leaders.

reform_title: Institutional Program for Scholarships for Initiation in Teaching (PIBID)
reform_description: Federal scholarship programme for undergraduate teacher education students to participate in public school teaching projects and develop pedagogical skills.

The Constitution Act of 1867 (section 93) stipulates that "[I]n and for each Province the Legislature may exclusively make Laws in relation to Education […]". Thus, ministries and departments of education, through education or school acts and attendant regulations, typically control: the nature of the school curriculum; the level to which schools are financed; the training and certification required of teachers; the methods of assessment and the standards for student testing; the structures to sustain school governance and management; the establishment of school boards; and the design and distribution of curriculum materials.

Another national body serving a wide array of clients is the Canadian Education Association (CEA), established in 1891. CEA functions as a clearinghouse for ideas and issues for educators and educational policy-makers at all levels of the school system across the country, regularly publishes newsletters, periodicals, and special reports, and features a well-resourced library.

reform_title: Establishment of the Council of Ministers of Education, Canada, (CMEC)
reform_description: A national body, the Council of Ministers of Education, Canada, (CMEC) was established in 1967 by the ministers of education to promote discussion on matters of mutual interest and further collaboration and consultation among the provinces and territories; to liaise with the federal government; and to represent Canadian education internationally.

reform_title: Relaxed their prescriptive lists of texts in classrooms
reform_description: Beginning in the 1970s, some provinces relaxed their prescriptive lists of texts for use in the classroom. Local educational authorities are sufficiently empowered to modify basic curricula to reflect and acknowledge the presence of diverse local cultures.
In the 1970s and 1980s, legislation was passed in many jurisdictions with the intention of ensuring that children with special needs had access to suitable education programmes and services.
During the 1980s and 1990s, governments merged departments in order to reduce the administrative costs of education

The Community Schools Programme has been in operation since the early 1980s. The programme provides enhanced support services to 17 schools in underprivileged urban neighbourhoods. Approximately 40% of students in community schools are of Aboriginal origin. The programme encourages parents and the community to take part in decisions made in the schools and delivers supplementary programmes and services to students, including nutrition programmes, cultural activities, and opportunities to use local community resources.

reform_title: The Charter of Rights and Freedoms (1982)
reform_description: In addition to the plenary power given to the provinces over education, the Constitution Act and the Charter of Rights and Freedoms (1982) contain specific rights to denominational education and to minority-language education, with assurances also made that all Canadian citizens must receive reasonable protection and equal benefits from the law regardless to race, religion, gender, and national origin.

reform_title: Quebec education legislation covering adult education
reform_description: Adult education has been covered by Quebec education legislation since 1988, in recognition of the considerable expansion of this sector over the past thirty years. Specific services are offered to adults in the secondary, college, and university sectors. Under the legislation, adult education services can be offered in the secondary sector by two networks, one formal (school boards and private institutions) and one informal (independent popular education and literacy groups and private training organizations, including internal corporate programs).

The CMEC launched in 1989 the School Achievement Indicators Programme (SAIP), in which a consortium of provinces worked together to produce and implement, for the first time, a national assessment instrument for elementary and secondary reading, writing, and mathematics.
In 1989, CMEC also formed the joint venture with Statistics Canada, called the Canadian Education Statistics Council (CESC), to provide and disseminate comparable educational statistics.
These changes, centred around five features (i.e. accountability, high quality education, accessibility, mobility and responsiveness to learners’ needs), have been initiated over the 1990s.

reform_title: Recentralization of functions previously deledated to school boards
reform_description: During the 1990s and early in 2000, certain provinces re-centralized a number of functions which until that time had been delegated to school boards. Re centralization occurred by means of collaboration between, or amalgamation of, ministries and school boards, and resulted in a reduction in the number of school boards and trustees. In almost all jurisdictions where restructuring took place, school councils were set up to increase parents’ participation in decision making in schools.

An action plan launched in 1993 is a strategy designed to enhance the well being of children, young people, and families. This cooperative process is designed to develop a joint method for addressing issues related to children. Schools, professional staff in public health, social workers, and other specialists work together to deliver integrated services to children, young people, and their families.

reform_title: Comprehensive strategy to improve the quality and relevance of education
reform_description: In 1994, the provincial government announced a comprehensive strategy to improve the quality and relevance of education. This strategy called for the review of all provincial curricula and consolidation of the basic academic skills. It also recommended teaching students computer skills and other important skills.

reform_title: New organization for high school education in New Brunswick
reform_description: In 1995, New Brunswick embarked on a new organization for high school education that focuses on a four-year programme called the High School Programme. The high school experience will normally be of four years duration including Grades IX-XII. Grades IX-X offer an opportunity for consolidation of, and growth in, necessary skills and knowledge across a broad common curriculum. The Grades IX-X programme is outcome-directed and students may proceed to Grades XI-XII courses when subject outcomes have been achieved. The Grades XI-XII programme leads to a New Brunswick High School Diploma. There are compulsory courses and an opportunity for students to choose electives that reflect personal interests, post secondary intentions and career aspirations.

reform_title: Implementation of a provincial multimedia learning strategy
reform_description: In May 1995, the Department announced the implementation of a provincial multimedia learning strategy, which includes a roll-out of a multimedia learning network and the issues of grants for installation of cables in Saskatchewan schools and development of educational multimedia software.

reform_title: Creation of language-based school boards in Ontario
reform_description: Ontario created language-based school boards, both denominational and non-denominational, in 1996.

reform_title: Abolishment and replacement of school boards
reform_description: In New Brunswick, the provincial government abolished school boards in 1996 and replaced them with education councils made up of parents.
The new Education Act that took effect in July 1996 gives greater responsibility to communities
The new Education Act, passed in January 1996, places greater emphasis on students in the classroom.

reform_title: The Education Quality and Accountability Office (EQAO)
reform_description: Established in 1996 as an arm’s length agency, the Education Quality and Accountability Office (EQAO) is responsible for ensuring greater accountability and quality in the elementary and secondary education systems in Ontario through collection, evaluation, and reporting of information on educational assessment and quality. A significant part of the EQAO’s mandate involves provincial testing of pupils’ academic achievement and reporting the results of the testing to the Minister of Education and Training and the general public.

reform_title: Initiation of the Pan-Canadian Education Research Agenda (PCERA)
reform_description: The Canadian Education Statistics Council (CESC), a partnership between Statistics Canada and CMEC, initiated the Pan-Canadian Education Research Agenda (PCERA) in 1997 to bring inter-provincial/territorial research issues that are important to ministers of education and training to the attention of the research community in Canada, and to promote open discussion on these issues with different partners in education.

reform_title: Getting all schools in Canada connected to the Internet
reform_description: Emergence of information and communication technologies (ICT) at all levels: Canada has set itself the challenge of taking action to get all schools in Canada connected to the Internet. This objective was achieved in 1999.

reform_title: Compulsory credits and test in Ontario
reform_description: In Ontario since September 1999, students must complete 30 credits during the four-year secondary school programme—18 compulsory and 12 optional. They must also pass the Ontario Secondary School Literacy Test in order to graduate.

reform_title: Compulsory credits and test in Ontario
reform_description: In Ontario, since September 1999, students must complete 30 credits during the four-year secondary school programme—18 compulsory and 12 optional. They must also pass the Ontario Secondary School Literacy Test in order to graduate.

Created in 1999, Nunavut has set the priority of training a new generation of young Inuit in administration and policies to manage the Territory, and of introducing curriculum in the Inuktikut language for the Inuit as well as the non-Inuit population, especially in the workplace.
In May 2001, the government proposed reinstating school boards, while providing an enhanced role for parents and reducing the number of boards.
Since June 2001, the student transcripts indicate language orientation (English first language, French first language, and French immersion), and also show the programme.

The implementation of major curriculum changes has been realized for the Senior High School Programme in the 2003-04 year. These changes were a result of partnering with the other Atlantic Provinces (Atlantic Provinces Education Foundation—APEF) in developing a common curriculum.

The federal government and provincial and territorial governments (with the exception of Quebec) have adopted an Early Childhood Development Initiative that aims to: promote health during pregnancy, at birth and throughout early childhood; improve support for parents and families; strengthen early childhood development, learning and child care; strengthen community support.
The Quebec government has set up its own early childhood development programmes.

As outlined in their new action plan, the ministries and departments of education in the provinces and territories will work in collaboration with local Aboriginal representatives, education stakeholders, and the federal government to identify and share best practices in Aboriginal education, including teacher training and recruitment, and to establish and/or share education indicators specific to Aboriginal student outcomes.

In addition to the plenary power given to the provinces over education, the Constitution Act and the Charter of Rights and Freedoms (1982) contain specific rights to denominational education and to minority-language education, with assurances also made that all Canadian citizens must receive reasonable protection and equal benefits from the law regardless to race, religion, gender, and national origin.

The Government of Canada passed two legislative acts, the Northwest Territories Act and the Yukon Act, giving the two territories responsibility for education services, for which the federal government assumes funding.

The Government of Canada passed two legislative acts, the Northwest Territories Act and the Yukon Act, giving the two territories responsibility for education services, for which the federal government assumes funding.

reform_title: Constitutional amendments won by Quebec and Newfoundland and Labrador
reform_description: The governments of Quebec and Newfoundland and Labrador have won constitutional amendments enabling them to create public, language-based, non-denominational school boards.

Since 1963 the federal role, particularly in post-secondary education, has been advanced through the office of the Department of Secretary of State (and since 1993, through the Department of Human Resources Development Canada— HRDC). These departments fund dozens of programmes directly or indirectly, especially in the fields of technical and vocational education; make federal transfers to the provinces for health and education; finance university research, student assistance, and federal post-secondary institutions; and support for the Official Languages Act (funding for the promotion of official-language education is provided by the Department of Canadian Heritage).

reform_title: The James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement
reform_description: Registered Indians and Inuit in northern Quebec receive educational services from Quebec under the James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement.

reform_title: Healthy Children in the Northwest Territories
reform_description: Early childhood development programmes have received attention from every administration in the late 1990s and the beginning of the new century. Most have adopted or enriched and strengthened early childhood health and learning programmes. These programmes are recognized under such names as: Healthy Children in the Northwest Territories; School Meals, Inner City Schools and Kids at Risk in British Columbia; Early Years Challenge Fund in Ontario; and Integrated School-Linked Services in Saskatchewan.

reform_title: School Meals, Inner City Schools and Kids at Risk in British Columbia
reform_description: Early childhood development programmes have received attention from every administration in the late 1990s and the beginning of the new century. Most have adopted or enriched and strengthened early childhood health and learning programmes. These programmes are recognized under such names as: Healthy Children in the Northwest Territories; School Meals, Inner City Schools and Kids at Risk in British Columbia; Early Years Challenge Fund in Ontario; and Integrated School-Linked Services in Saskatchewan.

reform_title: Early Years Challenge Fund in Ontario
reform_description: Early childhood development programmes have received attention from every administration in the late 1990s and the beginning of the new century. Most have adopted or enriched and strengthened early childhood health and learning programmes. These programmes are recognized under such names as: Healthy Children in the Northwest Territories; School Meals, Inner City Schools and Kids at Risk in British Columbia; Early Years Challenge Fund in Ontario; and Integrated School-Linked Services in Saskatchewan.

Early childhood development programmes have received attention from every administration in the late 1990s and the beginning of the new century. Most have adopted or enriched and strengthened early childhood health and learning programmes. These programmes are recognized under such names as: Healthy Children in the Northwest Territories; School Meals, Inner City Schools and Kids at Risk in British Columbia; Early Years Challenge Fund in Ontario; and Integrated School-Linked Services in Saskatchewan.

reform_title: Integration of universal day care into the public school system
reform_description: Quebec, as part of a four-pronged programme to support children and families, has announced that it will move to integrate universal day care into the public school system. This will be done by creating early-childhood centres, which will provide stimulating environments for preschoolers and encourage the development of local services that respond to the needs of families.

reform_title: Overhaul of the education and training system in Manitoba
reform_description: In recent years, the education and training system in Manitoba has been completely overhauled. Key components of education and training renewal in Manitoba include: renewing the curriculum from kindergarten to secondary IV (Grade XII) to provide quality education adapted to current needs and focused on the future; and using learning technology to facilitate cooperation and provide students with more options.

reform_title: Introduction of standard testing method in Manitoba
reform_description: Manitoba is also striving with other provinces to develop its common learning outcomes within a single education framework, and has introduced a standard testing method in language arts, mathematics, science and social studies in Grades III, VI, IX and XII;

reform_title: The restructuring of education in New Brunswick
reform_description: The restructuring of education in New Brunswick has targeted the following objectives: increasing parents’ participation in the education of their children; rationalizing administrative methods in education; and ensuring that shrinking resources are directed primarily toward the classroom.

reform_title: Introduction of new programmes and courses
reform_description: While constant efforts are made to ensure that courses delivered meet the province’s social and economic realities and needs, the Department of Education has also recently placed great emphasis on introducing new programmes and courses with an emphasis on science, technology, technical and economic education, and cooperative education. These initiatives include improving student learning outcomes, introducing special programmes for academically gifted students, and improving professional development courses on curriculum and teaching.

Curriculum reform is based on the following approaches: all communities in the Territories now have access to a full range of secondary curriculum; an electronic network links all Northwest Territories communities and is being used to launch a series of applications designed to facilitate curriculum delivery.

The new Education Act also provides for the creation of a board for the education of Afro-Canadians and a board for the education of the Mi’kmaq, with the role of guiding the Minister on programs and services to be delivered to these communities, so that they have the benefit of a supportive learning environment.

The Ministry of Education has developed a totally new provincial curriculum. Provincial standards have been set for teaching time and classroom size, and to improve the quality of education for exceptional children in Ontario schools. The education system is becoming more accountable through the administration of tests to students throughout the province, the appraisal of teaching staff, and the use of report cards that are easier to understand. Steps have also been taken to improve guidance of students for course selection and their future career plans. The new curriculum requires students in all elementary classes to study technology. Elementary classes learn to read and write earlier and also learn mathematics skills earlier. The reforms have been made with the vision of providing students in Ontario the latest knowledge and skills to make their full contribution to a highly competitive global economy and to become responsible citizens in their respective communities. The new curriculum’s main focus is on preparing students for success in the labour market or in learning programs at college and university.

reform_title: Development of curriculum policy documents for each discipline
reform_description: The Ministry has developed curriculum policy documents for each discipline. These documents contain information on the course that can be offered by a school. For every course offered at the secondary level, the new curriculum outlines clear and detailed curriculum expectations.

A three-year programme has been organized for the intermediate grades. At the senior high level, the curriculum has been organized to provide a three-year period of study. Examinations are prepared and marked locally. No provincial examinations are used. Achievement scores are reported in percentage or letter grades. Since the province does not maintain a provincial registry of students records, schools are required to maintain adequate records and issue transcripts.

reform_title: Renewal of the curriculum and education system in Prince Edward Island
reform_description: Recently, the curriculum and education system have been renewed in Prince Edward Island. The Department of Education has taken many actions to improve programme and student assessment. It has also focused on upgrading teachers’ knowledge and skills in student assessment, establishing committees to develop shared and clear methods for reporting on curriculum and student performance, and developing students’ information technology skills.

Alternative education programmes have been implemented to meet the needs of students for whom traditional teaching is not effective and who have dropped out of school or are at risk of dropping out. The alternative education project attempts to provide an alternate short-term solution to conventional classroom instruction, with a long-term objective of returning these young people to school.

Other initiatives in Prince Edward Island have focused on an outcome-based curriculum, innovative in-service teacher training programmes, partnerships with the private sector and other organizations, and training for teachers and administrative officials.

reform_title: Measure adopted in Prince Edward Island
reform_description: Measures have also been adopted to strengthen accountability, such as the publication of tangible results in annual reports, a three-year plan describing the Department’s policies and orientations, regional performance indicators, standards applicable to programs and services.

reform_title: Initiatives adopted in Prince Edward Island
reform_description: Prince Edward Island has also adopted initiatives linked to lifelong learning; viable, high quality post-secondary and adult education programmes adapted to the needs of its population; an education and literacy strategy for adults and students; a strategy for work force training programmes; and upgraded career guidance courses.

In conjunction with the main education partners, the Department of Education has developed the Saskatchewan Indicators Programme, which strives to: promote an excellent learning environment for all students and providing information on achievement of Saskatchewan’s education goals; provide support for the decision making process, planning, and policy development at all levels of the education system; and generate informed dialogue on education
Until the introduction of the School Achievement Indicators Programme (SAIP), coordinated by CMEC, the country was without a national assessment programme.

A number of jurisdictions introduced large-scale testing in attempts to measure systems, sub-components of the system (e.g. boards or schools), or individual students. Such accountability measures have swept across all jurisdictions.

reform_title: Establishment of the Post-Secondary Education Quality Assessment Board
reform_description: In April 2001, the Ontario government established the Post-Secondary Education Quality Assessment Board to assess proposals for new degree programmes to be offered by colleges and new institutions wishing to grant degrees in Ontario, and to advise the Minister of Training, Colleges and Universities in this area.
For example, student loan limits provided under the Canada Student Loan Programme were increased by 57% in 1994.

reform_title: Guides and resources to support special-needs students and their teachers
reform_description: Every jurisdiction has developed or adapted numerous guides and resources to support the learning needs of special-needs students and the information needs of the teachers.

reform_title: Approval of full funding for a fundamentalist Christian school
reform_description: In Alberta, the city of Edmonton public board recently approved full funding for a fundamentalist Christian school.

In 1991, independent school legislation was introduced in Saskatchewan. The new legal framework balances the educational interests of children enrolled in independent schools, their parents, and the public.

reform_title: Creation of a network linking public schools in Nova Scotia
reform_description: Nova Scotia, for example, has created a huge network linking public schools, municipal libraries, colleges, museums and the Department of Education’s administrative offices

The Yukon Education Student Network (YESnet) is a network set up by the Department of Education to enable all Yukon students to access the Internet and the rich, diverse databases it contains, and give students an opportunity to communicate worldwide.

reform_title: Set up of a teleconferencing network
reform_description: In Ontario, the twelve French language school boards have set up a teleconferencing network linking all French language secondary schools and all school board administrative offices across the province.

The Pan-Canadian Education Indicators Program (PCEIP) is an ongoing initiative of the Canadian Education Statistics Council (CESC), a partnership between Statistics Canada and CMEC, to provide a set of statistical measures on education systems in Canada.

reform_title: Constitution Act – Provincial Jurisdiction over Education
reform_description: The Constitution Act established that each province has exclusive legislative authority over education, forming the legal basis for Canada's decentralized education governance structure with thirteen distinct provincial and territorial education systems.

reform_title: Northwest Territories Act and Yukon Act – Territorial Education Responsibility
reform_description: The federal government passed the Northwest Territories Act and the Yukon Act giving the two territories responsibility for education services with federal funding.

reform_title: Council of Ministers of Education Canada (CMEC) Establishment
reform_description: A national body was established by provincial and territorial ministers of education to promote discussion on matters of mutual interest, further collaboration and consultation among provinces and territories, liaise with the federal government, and represent Canadian education internationally.

reform_title: Special Education Legislation in Provincial Jurisdictions
reform_description: In the 1970s and 1980s, legislation was passed in many provincial jurisdictions to ensure that children with special needs had access to suitable education programmes and services, supplemented by a large regulatory framework.

The Atlantic Provinces Special Education Authority was established to provide educational services, programmes, and opportunities for students who are blind, visually impaired, deaf, hard of hearing, and deaf-blind across Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Prince Edward Island, and Newfoundland and Labrador.

Saskatchewan established the Community Schools Programme providing enhanced support services to schools in underprivileged urban neighbourhoods, encouraging parental and community participation in school decision-making and delivering supplementary programmes including nutrition and cultural activities.

reform_title: Charter of Rights and Freedoms – Education Rights
reform_description: The Charter of Rights and Freedoms established specific rights to denominational education and minority-language education at the elementary and secondary levels for French- and English-speaking linguistic minorities across Canada.

Quebec enacted education legislation covering adult education, establishing a formal network of adult education services in the secondary, college, and university sectors governed by an adult general training educational services framework.

The Council of Ministers of Education Canada (CMEC) launched a national assessment programme in which a consortium of provinces worked together to produce and implement a national assessment instrument for elementary and secondary reading, writing, and mathematics.
CMEC formed a joint venture with Statistics Canada called the Canadian Education Statistics Council to provide and disseminate comparable educational statistics across Canada.

The federal government and provincial and territorial governments (except Quebec) adopted a joint Early Childhood Development Initiative aimed at promoting health during pregnancy and early childhood; improving support for parents and families; strengthening early childhood development, learning and child care; and strengthening community support.

During the 1990s and early 2000s, certain provinces re-centralized functions previously delegated to school boards including programming, funding, curriculum development, performance appraisal, and evaluation of teaching staff, resulting in a reduction in the number of school boards.

reform_title: Quebec and Newfoundland Constitutional Amendments for Non-Denominational School Boards
reform_description: The governments of Quebec and Newfoundland and Labrador won constitutional amendments enabling them to create public, language-based, non-denominational school boards, replacing the previous denominational school board structure.

reform_title: Western Canadian Protocol for Curriculum Development
reform_description: An intergovernmental collaborative protocol was established among the four western provinces and two territories for the common development of curriculum in mathematics and languages.

reform_title: Atlantic Provinces Common Core Curriculum Collaboration
reform_description: The Atlantic provinces collaborated on the development and implementation of a common core curriculum from kindergarten to Grade XII in languages, mathematics, science, and social studies through the Atlantic Provinces Education Foundation (APEF).

Ontario, the Atlantic provinces, and the western provinces and territories (nine provinces and two territories) collaborated on the development of a common framework of goals for science education from kindergarten to Grade XII.

The Ontario Ministry of Education developed a new provincial curriculum with provincial standards for teaching time and classroom size, competency-based expectations for each course, and a focus on preparing students for the labour market and post-secondary education.

The British Columbia government launched a new accreditation programme for primary and secondary schools and developed a system of performance indicators for post-secondary education to strengthen public accountability.

Manitoba undertook a comprehensive overhaul of its education and training system, including renewing the curriculum from kindergarten to Grade XII, introducing standard testing in language arts, mathematics, science and social studies at Grades III, VI, IX and XII, and tabling legislation to encourage greater parental and community participation in public schools.

Manitoba introduced provincial standards tests at Grade III, VI, Senior I, and Senior IV levels, with final marks for compulsory core subjects calculated by combining standards test marks with classroom-based evaluation.

Alberta established the Alberta Initiative for School Improvement to support improved student learning by encouraging teachers, parents, and the community to collaborate on innovative and creative teaching methods, including pilot projects to reduce class sizes and introduce early literacy programmes.

reform_title: Alberta Aboriginal Language and Culture Curriculum
reform_description: In collaboration with the Western provinces, Alberta completed a common Aboriginal Language and Culture curriculum to better meet the needs of Aboriginal students.

reform_title: Northwest Territories Aboriginal Curriculum Programmes (Innuqatigiit and Dene Kede)
reform_description: The Northwest Territories implemented Aboriginal programmes in all schools through Innuqatigiit (kindergarten to Grade XII) and Dene Kede (kindergarten to Grade IX) to reflect Dene and Inuit cultural perspectives in the curriculum.

The Northwest Territories introduced a Teacher Induction Programme to help new and beginning teachers become competent professionals and develop understanding of local school, community, and cultures, aiming to improve teacher performance, recruitment, and retention.

Saskatchewan's Department of Education developed the Saskatchewan Indicators Programme to promote an excellent learning environment, provide information on achievement of education goals, support decision-making and policy development, and generate informed dialogue on education.

reform_title: Quebec Early Childhood Centres and Universal Day Care Integration
reform_description: Quebec announced a programme to integrate universal day care into the public school system by creating early-childhood centres providing stimulating environments for preschoolers and encouraging development of local family services.

Quebec undertook a comprehensive education reform at the elementary and secondary levels focused on developing students' intellectual activities, civic responsibility, and occupational skills, with revised curriculum content including languages, technology, science, mathematics, social world, arts, and personal development, and cross-curricular skills.
New Brunswick moved from an optional to a mandatory kindergarten programme in recognition of the importance of early childhood education.
Quebec introduced full-day kindergarten programmes for 5-year-olds in all school boards.

reform_title: British Columbia Ministry for Children and Families
reform_description: British Columbia established the Ministry for Children and Families, merging various aspects of five ministries to focus on the needs of children and youth from birth to age 19.

reform_title: Saskatchewan Pre-Kindergarten Programme for Community Schools
reform_description: Saskatchewan Education developed the Pre-kindergarten Programme for Community Schools to provide holistic early intervention for 3- and 4-year-olds at risk of school failure, working in partnership with boards of education and community groups.

reform_title: Ontario Better Beginnings Better Futures Programme
reform_description: Ontario launched the long-term Better Beginnings, Better Futures initiative funded by multiple provincial ministries and federal departments, focusing on children up to age 8, their families, and neighbourhoods through holistic, community-based programming.

New Brunswick introduced Early Childhood Initiatives addressing the needs of children and families from the prenatal through the kindergarten period, combining services of Public Health, Family and Community Social Services, Mental Health Commission, Department of Income Assistance, and Education to improve school readiness.

Yukon and western and northern Canadian jurisdictions established the Western and Northern Canadian Protocol to support the development of common curriculum frameworks for Western and Northern Canada.
The federal government established the Canada Millennium Scholarship Foundation to award bursaries and scholarships to post-secondary students across Canada to improve access and reduce student debt.
The federal government established the Canada Education Savings Grant programme to supplement parental savings for post-secondary education.
The Yukon Department of Education established the Yukon Education Student Network to enable all Yukon students to access the Internet and communicate worldwide.

reform_title: Ontario French Language School Board Teleconferencing Network
reform_description: Ontario's twelve French language school boards established a teleconferencing network linking all French language secondary schools and school board administrative offices across the province.

Saskatchewan introduced independent school legislation establishing a new legal framework balancing the educational interests of children enrolled in independent schools, their parents, and the public.

reform_title: British Columbia 1994 Comprehensive Education Quality Strategy
reform_description: The British Columbia provincial government announced a comprehensive strategy to improve the quality and relevance of education, calling for review of all provincial curricula, consolidation of basic academic skills, and teaching of computer skills.
The federal government increased student loan limits under the Canada Student Loans Programme by 57% to improve access to post-secondary education and address rising educational costs.

New Brunswick introduced a new four-year high school programme (Grades IX–XII) with an outcome-directed curriculum, provincial examinations in Mathematics and English at Grade XI, and a Computer Literacy Skills requirement for graduation.

British Columbia introduced a credit-based graduation programme requiring students in Grades XI and XII to complete at least 52 credits, with province-wide examinations in 17 Grade XII academic subjects counting for 40% of the final grade.

Saskatchewan announced the implementation of a provincial multimedia learning strategy including a roll-out of a multimedia learning network and grants for installation of cables in schools and development of educational multimedia software.
Ontario created language-based school boards, both denominational and non-denominational, restructuring the governance of education along linguistic rather than purely denominational lines.

reform_title: New Brunswick Abolition of School Boards and Creation of Education Councils
reform_description: The New Brunswick provincial government abolished school boards and replaced them with education councils made up of parents to increase parental participation in education governance.

reform_title: Ontario Education Quality and Accountability Office (EQAO)
reform_description: Ontario established the Education Quality and Accountability Office as an arm's length agency responsible for ensuring greater accountability and quality in elementary and secondary education through provincial testing and reporting of student achievement.
A new Education Act took effect in the Northwest Territories giving greater responsibility to communities and establishing a framework for curriculum delivery including distance education programmes.

Nova Scotia passed a new Education Act placing greater emphasis on students in the classroom, providing for the development of new curriculum at elementary and secondary levels, reform of cooperative education and school-work transition programmes, and the creation of advisory boards for the education of Afro-Canadians and the Mi'kmaq.
The Northwest Territories launched the Healthy Children initiative targeting children from birth to age 6 and their families as part of early childhood education priorities.

The Canadian Education Statistics Council initiated the Pan-Canadian Education Research Agenda to bring inter-provincial and territorial research issues to the attention of the research community and promote policy-relevant research and communication among education stakeholders.
A new territory of Nunavut was created with its own department of education responsible for delivering education services to the Inuit population.

reform_title: Ontario Secondary School Literacy Test and Credit Requirements
reform_description: Ontario introduced a requirement that secondary school students complete 30 credits (18 compulsory and 12 optional) during a four-year programme and pass the Ontario Secondary School Literacy Test in order to graduate.

reform_title: Ontario Post-Secondary Education Quality Assessment Board
reform_description: The Ontario government established the Post-Secondary Education Quality Assessment Board to assess proposals for new degree programmes to be offered by colleges and new institutions wishing to grant degrees in Ontario.

reform_title: Newfoundland and Labrador Senior High School Curriculum Reform (APEF)
reform_description: Newfoundland and Labrador implemented major curriculum changes for the Senior High School Programme in partnership with the other Atlantic Provinces through the Atlantic Provinces Education Foundation (APEF) to develop a common curriculum.

reform_title: Alberta Commission on Learning – Teacher Professional Development Requirements
reform_description: Following the 2003 Alberta Commission on Learning recommendations, the Alberta government moved forward on implementing requirements that all teachers have targeted annual professional development plans linked to their schools' improvement plans, with comprehensive professional development plans for every school jurisdiction and school.
British Columbia introduced a revised graduation programme requiring students to complete at least 80 credits in Grades X–XII with updated required courses replacing the Foundation Studies framework.

reform_title: Establishment of the Council of Ministers of Education, Canada (CMEC)
reform_description: Creation of a national intergovernmental body by the ministers of education to promote discussion, further collaboration among provinces and territories, liaise with the federal government, and represent Canadian education internationally.
Introduction of a collaborative pan-Canadian assessment instrument for elementary and secondary reading, writing, and mathematics, implemented by a consortium of provinces.

reform_title: Establishment of the Canadian Education Statistics Council (CESC)
reform_description: Creation of a joint venture between the Council of Ministers of Education and Statistics Canada to provide and disseminate comparable educational statistics across the country.
Introduction of a provincial legal framework in Saskatchewan to regulate and balance the educational interests of students enrolled in independent schools, their parents, and the public.

reform_title: Action Plan for Children, Young People, and Families
reform_description: Launch of a provincial strategy in Saskatchewan designed to deliver integrated services across schools, public health, and social work to support the well-being of youth and families.
Reorganization of high school education in New Brunswick into a four-year program focusing on skill consolidation in lower grades and a credit-based system leading to a diploma in upper grades.

reform_title: Abolition of School Boards and Creation of Education Councils
reform_description: A provincial governance reform in New Brunswick that abolished traditional school boards and replaced them with parent-led education councils.
A provincial governance reform in Ontario that established language-based school boards, including both denominational and non-denominational structures.

reform_title: Establishment of the Education Quality and Accountability Office (EQAO)
reform_description: Creation of an independent agency in Ontario responsible for ensuring accountability and quality in elementary and secondary education through provincial testing and reporting.

reform_title: Creation of the Nunavut Department of Education
reform_description: Establishment of a dedicated department of education for the newly created territory of Nunavut to manage the delivery of education services.

reform_title: Secondary School Graduation Requirements and Literacy Test
reform_description: An Ontario provincial reform requiring students to complete 30 credits and pass the newly introduced Ontario Secondary School Literacy Test to graduate from the four-year secondary school program.

Constitutional allocation of exclusive authority over education to provincial legislatures, establishing provincial control over curriculum, finance, teacher certification, assessment, school governance, school boards, and curriculum materials.

reform_title: Charter protections for denominational and minority-language education
reform_description: Constitutional recognition of denominational education rights and minority-language education rights at the elementary and secondary levels.

reform_title: Northwest Territories Act and Yukon Act education transfer
reform_description: Federal legislation giving the Northwest Territories and Yukon responsibility for education services, with federal funding support.
Creation of Nunavut as a territory with its own education administration responsible for managing education services.

reform_title: Council of Ministers of Education, Canada
reform_description: Establishment of CMEC as an intergovernmental body for education policy discussion, collaboration, federal liaison, and international representation.
Launch of a pan-Canadian student assessment programme to implement national assessment instruments in reading, writing, mathematics, and later science.
Creation of a CMEC-Statistics Canada joint body to produce and disseminate comparable education statistics.

Reorganization of education administration through department mergers, recentralization of programming, funding, and teacher evaluation functions, and reduction in the number of school boards or districts.
Adoption by most provinces and territories of accountability strategies requiring education departments, local administrations, and schools to report on spending and learning outcomes.
Overhaul of curricula across jurisdictions to emphasize core subjects, languages, mathematics, science, technology, globalization, competitiveness, and productivity.
Federal, provincial, and territorial adoption of an early childhood initiative to strengthen pregnancy and early-childhood health, family support, early learning, child care, and community support.
Establishment by Quebec of its own early childhood development programmes outside the federal-provincial-territorial Early Childhood Development Initiative.
Adoption by all education administrations of strategies and programmes to assess student performance and report outcomes to students, parents, and taxpayers.
Government action to connect all Canadian schools to the Internet, achieving the objective in 1999 and setting a further objective of high-speed regular access for students.
Changes in vocational and technical education, apprenticeship, vocational guidance, co-operative education, and postsecondary-industry partnerships to support transitions from study to employment.

reform_title: Aboriginal education planning and curriculum changes
reform_description: Provincial and territorial implementation of changes to include Aboriginal communities and parents in educational planning and revise curriculum and teaching practices to reflect Aboriginal history, culture, and traditions.
Expansion of federal, provincial, and territorial student assistance programmes through debt and interest relief, grants, bursaries, scholarships, and funding mechanisms for diverse student needs.

reform_title: Quebec and Newfoundland and Labrador language-based non-denominational school boards
reform_description: Constitutional amendments enabling Quebec and Newfoundland and Labrador to create public language-based non-denominational school boards.
Creation in Ontario of language-based school boards, both denominational and non-denominational.
Abolition of school boards in New Brunswick and replacement with parent-based education councils.
Intergovernmental collaboration among the western provinces and territories to develop common curriculum in mathematics and languages.
Collaboration among Atlantic provinces to develop and implement a common core curriculum from kindergarten to Grade XII in languages, mathematics, science, and social studies.
Collaboration among Ontario, Atlantic provinces, western provinces, and territories to develop a common framework of science education goals from kindergarten to Grade XII.
Change from optional to mandatory kindergarten in New Brunswick.
Provision of full-day kindergarten programmes for 5-year-olds in all Quebec school boards.

reform_title: British Columbia Ministry for Children and Families
reform_description: Creation of a ministry merging child- and youth-related functions from five ministries to coordinate services for children and youth from birth to age 19.

reform_title: Saskatchewan Pre-kindergarten Programme for Community Schools
reform_description: Development of a pre-kindergarten programme for 3- and 4-year-olds at risk of school failure, implemented with boards of education and community groups.
Creation of a secretariat to initiate coordinated and integrated services for children, youth, and families, with early childhood targeted for concerted action.
Quebec programme to integrate universal day care into the public school system through early-childhood centres.
Interdepartmental initiative covering the prenatal through kindergarten period to improve development of children at risk of delayed school readiness.

reform_title: Alberta curriculum and education system renewal
reform_description: Renewal of Alberta curriculum and the education system with emphasis on performance reporting, fiscal accountability, open planning, parental responsibility, choice, flexibility, and high standards.
Provincial initiative supporting improved student learning through collaborative school improvement projects involving teachers, parents, and communities.

reform_title: Common Aboriginal Language and Culture curriculum
reform_description: Completion by Alberta in collaboration with western provinces of a common Aboriginal Language and Culture curriculum.
Introduction of a graduation programme with Foundation Studies, credit requirements, provincial curriculum, and province-wide Grade XII examinations.
Introduction of a new credit-based graduation programme for Grades X-XII requiring at least 80 credits and Required Courses.
Creation of a School Completion Certificate for students completing goals and objectives set out in an individualized Student Learning Plan.

reform_title: British Columbia school accreditation and postsecondary indicators
reform_description: Launch of a new accreditation programme for primary and secondary schools and development of postsecondary performance indicators.
Provincial strategy to improve education quality and relevance through curriculum review, consolidation of basic academic skills, and computer-skills instruction.
Introduction of provincial standards tests in Grades III, VI, Senior I, and Senior IV, with results combined with classroom and school-based evaluation.
Overhaul of Manitoba’s education and training system through K–Senior IV curriculum renewal and use of learning technology to expand cooperation and student options.
Development of an Aboriginal education strategy to address the needs of Aboriginal people.
Reorganization of high school education into a four-year Grades IX-XII High School Programme with outcome-directed Grades IX-X, compulsory and elective courses, and a provincial diploma.

reform_title: New Brunswick restructuring and learning outcomes reform
reform_description: Restructuring of education to increase parent participation, rationalize administration, target classroom resources, specify learning outcomes, develop standards, and assess students in Grades III and VIII and high school.

reform_title: Newfoundland and Labrador Senior High School curriculum changes
reform_description: Implementation of major Senior High School Programme curriculum changes developed with other Atlantic provinces through the Atlantic Provinces Education Foundation common curriculum.
New Education Act giving greater responsibility to communities for education.
Implementation of distance education programmes to provide students access to a full range of quality courses.
Implementation of Aboriginal programmes in all Northwest Territories schools through Innuqatigiit for kindergarten to Grade XII and Dene Kede for kindergarten to Grade IX.
Early childhood initiative targeting children from birth to age 6 and their families.

reform_title: Northwest Territories representative teacher education programme
reform_description: Development of a basic education programme to provide all communities with teachers representative of the population by 2004.
Education Act placing greater emphasis on students in the classroom and supporting curriculum, personalized programming, and school-to-work reforms.

reform_title: Nova Scotia Afro-Canadian and Mi’kmaq education boards
reform_description: Creation of boards for the education of Afro-Canadians and Mi’kmaq to guide the Minister on programmes and services for these communities.

reform_title: Nunavut Inuktitut curriculum and secondary curriculum development
reform_description: Nunavut reform to introduce curriculum in the Inuktitut language and complete secondary curriculum while expanding continuing education for the Inuit population.

reform_title: Ontario Education Quality and Accountability Office
reform_description: Establishment of EQAO as an arm’s-length agency responsible for assessment, quality reporting, and accountability in elementary and secondary education.

reform_title: Ontario new provincial curriculum and accountability reform
reform_description: Development of a new provincial curriculum with clear expectations, standards for teaching time and class size, provincial testing, teacher appraisal, and clearer report cards.

reform_title: Prince Edward Island curriculum and assessment renewal
