reform_description: Renewal of curriculum and the education system through programme and student assessment reforms, teacher upgrading in assessment, reporting methods, and information technology skills.

reform_title: Prince Edward Island alternative education programmes
reform_description: Implementation of alternative education programmes for students not served effectively by conventional instruction and those who have dropped out or are at risk of dropping out.

reform_title: Prince Edward Island outcome-based curriculum and accountability reforms
reform_description: Adoption of outcome-based curriculum, innovative in-service teacher training, partnerships, annual results reporting, three-year departmental planning, performance indicators, and programme and service standards.

reform_title: Prince Edward Island adult literacy and workforce training strategies
reform_description: Adoption of lifelong learning initiatives, adult and student literacy strategy, workforce training strategy, and upgraded career guidance courses.

reform_title: Quebec elementary and secondary education reform
reform_description: Quebec reform of school missions, curriculum content, organization of learning, essential knowledge and skills, cultural content, assessment requirements, and cross-curricular skills.

reform_title: Saskatchewan children and families action plan
reform_description: Cooperative action plan to enhance the well-being of children, young people, and families through integrated services involving schools, public health staff, social workers, and specialists.
Provincial multimedia learning strategy including rollout of a multimedia learning network, school cabling grants, and educational multimedia software development.
Development of an indicators programme to report on achievement of education goals, support system-level decision-making and policy development, and inform public dialogue.
Completion of a full-cycle curriculum review in compulsory kindergarten-to-Grade XII subjects and establishment of an ongoing electronic curriculum renewal process.

reform_title: Western and Northern Canadian Protocol curriculum frameworks
reform_description: Yukon participation in WNCP to develop common curriculum frameworks for Western and Northern Canada and adapt British Columbia programmes to Yukon needs.

reform_title: Yukon elementary and secondary curriculum reform
reform_description: Yukon curriculum reform emphasizing training and postsecondary education, First Nations cultural and linguistic obligations, innovative programmes, early intervention, literacy, distance education, Internet access, school safety, and school board participation.

reform_title: Yukon non-categorical special education funding model
reform_description: Adoption of a non-categorical model for allocating special education resources to support inclusive education for students with special needs.
Legislation passed in many jurisdictions to ensure children with special needs have access to suitable education programmes and services.
Interprovincial authority providing educational services, programmes, and support for students who are blind, visually impaired, deaf, hard of hearing, or deaf-blind across Atlantic provinces.
Independent school legislation establishing a legal framework balancing the interests of children in independent schools, parents, and the public.
Legislation bringing adult education under Quebec education law and establishing formal and informal adult education service networks in the secondary sector.
Institution of achievement indicators in Ontario colleges to measure student and employer satisfaction.

reform_title: Ontario Post-Secondary Education Quality Assessment Board
reform_description: Establishment of a board to assess proposals for new college degree programmes and new degree-granting institutions and advise the Minister.

reform_title: Canada Student Loan Programme limit increase and repayment assistance changes
reform_description: Student financial assistance reforms including a 57 percent increase in federal student loan limits and changes such as income-contingent repayment exploration and expanded interest relief.

reform_title: Portugal’s Education Territories for Priority Intervention
reform_description: Portugal’s Education Territories for Priority Intervention (Territorios Educativos de Interventio Prioritoria, 1996, updated in 2006, 2012) targets areas with high shares of disadvantaged students and above-average early school-leaving rates

reform_title: Portugal’s Education Territories for Priority Intervention (2006 Update)
reform_description: Portugal’s Education Territories for Priority Intervention (Territorios Educativos de Interventio Prioritoria, 1996, updated in 2006, 2012) targets areas with high shares of disadvantaged students and above-average early school-leaving rates

reform_title: Ireland’s National Strategy to improve Literacy and Numeracy (2011-20)
reform_description: Ireland’s National Strategy to improve Literacy and Numeracy (2011-20) established performance targets (and intermediate benchmarks) for literacy and numeracy at primary and secondary levels to be met by 2020, in consultation with various stakeholders. The Strategy mobilised six arms of action, each with discreet targets and measures: training teachers; developing school leaders; engaging parents and communities; clarifying curricula expectations; mobilising assessment tools; and targeting supports to needs.
Ireland also introduced a school self-evaluation process explicitly focused on literacy and numeracy in its first cycle (2012-15).

reform_title: Portugal’s Education Territories for Priority Intervention (2012 Update)
reform_description: Portugal’s Education Territories for Priority Intervention (Territorios Educativos de Interventio Prioritoria, 1996, updated in 2006, 2012) targets areas with high shares of disadvantaged students and above-average early school-leaving rates

The Unistats platform (2012) incorporated data on student satisfaction and labour market outcomes for recent graduates from already existing surveys, as well as course-level pedagogical and logistical information provided by institutions.

reform_title: School Active Search (Busca Ativa Escolar, 2017) strategy
reform_description: Early intervention at subnational level could complement the School Active Search (Busca Ativa Escolar, 2017) strategy efforts in Brazil.

reform_title: Latvia’s Tackling Early School Leaving project (2017-22)
reform_description: Latvia’s Tackling Early School Leaving project (2017-22) targets at-risk students in general and vocational education through prevention and intervention across three levels: students, institutions and system.

reform_title: Portugal's Students' Profile at the End of Compulsory Schooling
reform_description: Portugal's Students' Profile at the End of Compulsory Schooling (Perfil dos Alunos ? Sa?da da Escolaridade Obrigat?ria, 2017) established a new framework for teaching, learning and assessment delineating what young people are expected to achieve by age 18 for each discipline at all levels of compulsory schooling.

National Literacy Policy (Politica Nacional de Alfabetiza, 2019) established a clear, shared vision focused on learning outcomes, with detailed related planned actions.  The Strategy also promoted policy continuity as targets extended across multiple administrations.

Germany's Good Daycare Facilities Act (Gute-KiTa-Gesetz, 2019) formally steers efforts to promote ECEC quality and equity within a federal system across ten priority areas. To support action, the federal government established a dedicated fund of EUR 5.5 billion and made individual agreements with the Lender, legally enabling them to receive and invest funds according to local needs, realities and priorities.
The United Kingdom's Discover Uni platform (2019), supports prospective higher education students in deciding whether, where, and what to study.

reform_title: Online Learning for Teachers (Aprendo En L?nea Docente, 2020)
reform_description: a dedicated Online Learning for Teachers (Aprendo En L?nea Docente, 2020) portal houses more than 20 000 pedagogical resources, including learning guides and teaching strategies linked to the prioritised objectives, videos, webinars guidelines, resources for students and assessment tools.

reform_title: Comprehensive Assessment of Learning (Diagnostico Integral de Aprendizajes)
reform_description: Chile implemented the Comprehensive Assessment of Learning (Diagnostico Integral de Aprendizajes, DIA, 2020).

reform_title: The updated National Plan for Early Childhood (Plano Nacional pela Primeira Infancia, 2020).
reform_description: The updated National Plan for Early Childhood (Plano Nacional pela Primeira Infancia, 2020).
A national system for evaluating higher education institutions and programmes overseen by CONAES providing quality assurance and accountability for the tertiary education sector.

reform_title: Fundeb (Fundo de Manutenção e Desenvolvimento da Educação Básica e de Valorização dos Profissionais da Educação)
reform_description: A national redistributive education funding mechanism pooling state and municipal tax revenues and redistributing them to reduce funding disparities across Brazil's decentralised education system building on objectives established with Fundef in 1996.

A national school quality index based on SAEB assessment results and student transition indicators providing performance scores at school municipal state and national levels from primary to upper secondary education to inform improvement planning and targets.

reform_title: Extension of Compulsory Education to Age 4
reform_description: Brazil extended compulsory education to begin at age 4 making pre-school education mandatory and thereby expanding ECEC participation particularly among younger children.

reform_title: Teachers' Legal Right to Continuous Professional Development
reform_description: A legal provision establishing teachers' right to continuous professional development in Brazil formalising CPD as an entitlement for the teaching profession.

reform_title: National Education Plan (Plano Nacional de Educação - PNE)
reform_description: A national education plan establishing goals and targets for the Brazilian education system including expanding ECEC enrolment to 50% for 0-3 year-olds by 2024 increasing VET participation to 25% of upper secondary students and spending 10% of GDP on education by 2024.

reform_title: New More Education Programme (Programa Novo Mais Educação)
reform_description: A federal programme providing extended learning time and extra-curricular activities particularly targeting disadvantaged students building on full-time education initiatives at federal and subnational levels.

A national curriculum reform establishing universal minimum learning requirements for all students across Brazil regardless of background. The BNCC was adopted for early childhood and primary education in 2017 and extended to upper secondary in 2018 setting common learning standards across the country.

reform_title: New Upper Secondary (Novo Ensino Médio - NEM)
reform_description: A reform of upper secondary education aimed at increasing flexibility of student pathways and transparency around what students can learn at this level. It introduced a new organisation of learning at upper secondary level and targets the integrated vocational model to increase participation and reduce drop-out.

reform_title: School Active Search Strategy (Busca Ativa Escolar)
reform_description: A national strategy aimed at identifying and re-engaging students who have dropped out or are at risk of dropping out of school operating at subnational level to reduce early school leaving.

reform_title: National Quality Parameters of Early Childhood Education (Parâmetros nacionais de qualidade da educação infantil)
reform_description: National quality standards for early childhood education establishing parameters for ECEC provision across municipalities to raise quality and consistency in the sector.

reform_title: National Literacy Policy (Política Nacional de Alfabetização)
reform_description: A national policy focused on improving literacy outcomes across Brazil establishing a framework for literacy improvement with planned actions and monitoring mechanisms.

reform_title: National Curriculum Guidelines for Initial Teacher Education (ITE)
reform_description: National guidelines establishing standards for initial teacher education programmes across Brazil to raise quality and ensure adequate practical components for all trainee teachers.

reform_title: Common National Base for Initial Teacher Education (BNC-Formação)
reform_description: A national framework establishing common competency standards for initial teacher education programmes across Brazil to standardise and raise the quality of teacher preparation.
A national programme aimed at strengthening vocational education and training pathways and improving alignment between education provision and labour market needs.

reform_title: New Regulations for Private VET Providers
reform_description: New national regulations governing private vocational education and training providers to enhance quality assurance and oversight of the VET sector.

reform_title: National System for Evaluation of Elementary Education (SAEB)
reform_description: A national system for evaluating student learning outcomes in elementary education administering biannual standardised assessments in Portuguese and mathematics at key year levels to monitor system performance and inform policy.

An updated national redistributive education funding mechanism building on the original Fundeb (2007) aimed at more effectively redistributing education funding across states and municipalities to foster greater equality in basic education financing.

reform_title: National Plan for Early Childhood (Plano Nacional pela Primeira Infância)
reform_description: An updated national plan guiding early childhood policy across Brazil establishing priority areas for ECEC development and providing a framework for subnational action on early childhood education.

reform_title: Monitoring and Evaluation Plan of the VET Offer
reform_description: A national plan introducing monitoring and evaluation mechanisms for vocational education and training provision to enhance quality assurance across the VET sector.

reform_title: Common National Matrix of Competences for School Leaders (Matriz Nacional Comum de Competências do Diretor Escolar)
reform_description: A national framework under development to clarify key competencies and responsibilities for school leaders across Brazil aiming to professionalise the school leader role and improve selection and training processes.

reform_title: Curriculum Guidelines for Pre-School Education (Orientações Curriculares para a Educação Pré-escolar)
reform_description: Updated pedagogical guidelines that act as a reference for curriculum development and organizational principles to improve alignment with the first cycle of basic education and encourage collaboration during transitions to primary education.

reform_title: National Common Curricular Base (Base Nacional Comum Curricular / BNCC)
reform_description: A national curricular reform that establishes universal minimum learning requirements across the education system to support equity regardless of student background.

reform_title: New Upper Secondary (Novo Ensino Médio / NEM)
reform_description: A reform of the upper secondary education level implemented to enhance the flexibility and transparency of learning pathways for students.

An institution established to bring together federal, provincial, and territorial governments to improve the timeliness, reliability, and accessibility of labor market information by conducting research and producing centralized data dashboards.
A policy initiative that aims to nurture deeper collaborations between education actors by encouraging schools to collaborate in local, cross-level clusters.

A reform offering guidance to balance curricular areas and ensure progression across the school year by identifying essential learning objectives for all school levels and subjects, alongside a differentiated plan for vocational training.
Education financing mechanism establishing redistributive objectives for basic education funding.
National redistributive education funding mechanism for basic education and teacher valorisation, building on Fundef objectives.

reform_title: Extension of compulsory education to age 4
reform_description: Extended compulsory education to begin at age 4 and continue through age 17, broadening mandatory participation in education.

reform_title: Teachers’ legal right to continuous professional development
reform_description: Teacher policy establishing a legal right for teachers to participate in continuous professional development.

reform_title: Education earmark from oil and natural gas revenues
reform_description: Funding reform requiring 75% of revenue from oil and natural gas exploration to go to education.

National education plan establishing system-wide goals across education, including ECEC enrolment, VET participation, CPD participation, principal appointment on technical merit and performance, stakeholder engagement, and education spending targets.
Federal programme to expand educational opportunities through additional learning time and activities, particularly for vulnerable students.
National curriculum framework establishing universal minimum learning requirements and guiding local curricula, teaching, learning and assessment across basic education.
Upper secondary reform introducing a new organisation of learning and greater flexibility in student pathways, to be implemented alongside the BNCC and aligned with national assessments.
National strategy to identify and support children and adolescents who are out of school or at risk of dropping out.
Adjustment of the federal education spending floor using 2017 education expenditure as the minimum value until 2036 with inflation adjustments.

reform_title: National Quality Parameters of Early Childhood Education
reform_description: National quality parameters for early childhood education intended to guide quality and consistency in municipal ECEC provision.
National policy focused on improving literacy outcomes through a shared vision, planned actions, and alignment efforts.
VET policy initiative to strengthen vocational education and training and better align provision with labour market information and skills needs.

reform_title: National Curriculum Guidelines and Common National Base for Initial Teacher Education
reform_description: National teacher education reform establishing curriculum guidelines and a common base for initial teacher education to raise standards and improve provider accountability.

reform_title: New regulations for private VET providers
reform_description: Regulatory reform for private vocational education and training providers intended to strengthen quality assurance in VET provision.

reform_title: Updated National Plan for Early Childhood
reform_description: Updated national plan for early childhood setting priorities for early childhood policy, including education-related provision and monitoring.

reform_title: Monitoring and Evaluation Plan of the VET offer
reform_description: Plan introduced to monitor and evaluate vocational education and training provision and strengthen quality assurance in the sector.
Revised Fundeb mechanism intended to redistribute education funding more effectively and foster greater equality across Brazil’s decentralised education system.

reform_title: Launching of a ten-year education system
reform_description: After 1979, the Ministry of Education, in consideration of the country's urgent needs, launched a ten-year education system (4+3+3)

reform_title: Expansion to an eleven-year education system
reform_description:
After 1979, the Ministry of Education, in consideration of the country's urgent needs,
launched a ten-year education system (4+3+3) and then expanded it to an eleven-year
education system from 1986 to 1996.

reform_title: Article 65 of the Constitution (1993, amended in 1999)
reform_description:
Article 65 of the Constitution (1993, amended in 1999) specifies that: "The State
shall protect and upgrade citizens’ rights to quality education at all levels and shall
take necessary steps for quality education to reach all citizens."

reform_title: Article 66 of the Constitution (1993, amended in 1999)
reform_description:
"The State shall establish a comprehensive and standardized educational system throughout
the country that shall guarantee the principles of educational freedom and quality to
ensure that all citizens have equal opportunity to earn a living." (Article 66).

reform_title: Article 67 of the Constitution (1993, amended in 1999)
reform_description:
"The State shall adopt an educational programme according to the principle of modern
pedagogy including technology and foreign languages. The State shall control public
and private schools and classrooms at all levels." (Article 67).

reform_title:
The plan for Rebuilding Quality
Education and Training (1994)
reform_description:
the plan for Rebuilding Quality
Education and Training (1994)

the Ministry of
Education, Youth and Sports (MoEYS) formulated the Education Investment Plan
1995-2000, which detailed priority strategies, programmes and investment
requirements.

reform_title: Introduction of a new twelve-year education system
reform_description:
The curriculum was reformed, new textbooks
were developed and new skills were provided to teachers to prepare the groundwork
for the introduction of a new twelve-year education system (6+3+3) in the 1996/97
school year.

reform_title: The first Socio-Economic Development Plan (SEDP I)
reform_description:
The Government prepared the first Socio-
Economic Development Plan (SEDP I) 1996-2000 that set out broad education
development policies, strategies and targets.

Among the main results achieved in the 1996-2000 period, the following
deserve particular mention:
• Establishment of an institutional framework (the school cluster system) to
strengthen the quality of education and to improve the efficiency of planning,
implementation and monitoring; participatory bottom-up planning exercises
were initiated in the six UNICEF supported provinces in coordination with the
MoEYS.

Among the main results achieved in the 1996-2000 period, the following
deserve particular mention:
• Development of curriculum, students' competencies, textbooks and teacher
manuals for primary and lower secondary education in three subjects (Khmer, mathematics and science), and training in the use of these new materials, and
also on life skills including HIV/AIDS awareness and prevention.

Among the main results achieved in the 1996-2000 period, the following
deserve particular mention:
• Construction or rehabilitation of 1,672 classrooms with community
participation under UNICEF/MoEYS supervision.

reform_title: Establishment of a nationwide Education Management Information System
reform_description:
Among the main results achieved in the 1996-2000 period, the following
deserve particular mention:
• Establishment of a nationwide Education Management Information System

Among the main results achieved in the 1996-2000 period, the following
deserve particular mention:
• Development of MoEYS management capacities for executing
UNICEF/Government of Sweden supported projects, especially in the areas of
curriculum and textbook development, cluster schools implementation and
school construction.

reform_title: Establishment of the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports
reform_description:
The Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports was established on the basis of
the Law (Kram) dated 24 January 1996.

reform_title: Increase in the entry qualifications to the teaching service
reform_description:
In 1996, the entry qualifications to the teaching service were
increased to twelve years of schooling and two years of teacher training (in cities and
towns; a 9 + 2 scheme is applied in remote areas).
since 1996/97 textbooks for Grades VII-IX are being provided on a one book per student per subject basis.

reform_title: Reducrion of test periods in the admission cycle
reform_description:
In 1998, the MoEYS reduced the admission cycle from two test periods to
one, which has improved efficiency and reduced costs.

reform_title: Introduction of the priority action programmes (PAP)
reform_description:
The introduction of the priority action programmes (PAP) in 2000 has
provided an opportunity to rationalize organizational structures and responsibilities
within the Ministry.

In early 2000, the Government and the Ministry of Education took a number
of key initiatives to improve the predictability of resourcing, providing real incentives
for forward-looking education financing policy development and programme
planning. A critical step has been the presentation of a three-year rolling Public
Expenditure Plan (PEP)

The Education Strategic Plan (ESP), first proposed for the period 2001–2005,
represents a key milestone in the work of the MoEYS to effectively reform the
education services in Cambodia.

The Ministry's reform strategy is also specified in the Education Sector
Support Programme (ESSP) 2001-2005.
In 2001, the Ministry abolished start-of-the year contributions in grades 1 to 9

reform_title: Policy framework on pre-school education and guidelines on early childhood care and development
reform_description:
Cambodia developed a policy framework on pre-school education and
guidelines on early childhood care and development in 2002.

In the period of ESP 2006-2010, the Ministry commits itself to achieving the
existing priority policies, which are consistent with EFA 2003-2015

reform_title: Revision of the Education Strategic Plan (ESP)
reform_description:
The Education Strategic Plan (ESP),
revised for the period 2004-2008, and now extended for the period 2006-2010,
represents a key milestone in the work of the MoEYS to effectively reform the
education services in Cambodia

Within the new National Curriculum, set out in December 2004, pupils in
primary education should receive five periods of teaching per day, five days per week,
each teaching period lasting forty minutes, plus 2-5 additional 40-minute periods per
week of the Local Life Skills Program (LLSP).

The Ministry acknowledges that a fundamental challenge in the next five years
will be to improve staff remuneration systems. A priority will be to ensure that any
pay rises are clearly related to better performance, more responsibility and contribute
to staff deployment efficiencies. The new ESP 2004/08 clearly defines these
strategies and their cost implications, including new forms of staff training and
development.

Within the new National Curriculum (2004), schools, in partnership with
parents, their local community, community organizations and NGOs, must develop
and administer a Local Life Skills Program (LLSP) of between 2 to 5 lessons per
week for basic education.

reform_title: Extention of the Education Strategic Plan (ESP)
reform_description:
The Education Strategic Plan (ESP), extended for the period 2006-2010,
represents a key milestone in the work of the MoEYS to effectively reform the
education services in Cambodia

In the period of ESP 2006-2010, the Ministry commits itself to achieving the
existing priority policies, which are consistent with EFA 2003-2015, the Cambodia
Millennium Development Goals 2015 and the National Strategic Development Plan
2006-2010. The priority policies in the ESP 2006-2010 are as follows: 1) ensuring
equitable access to education; 2) increasing quality and efficiency of the education
services; and 3) institutional development and capacity building for decentralization

In the period of ESP 2006-2010, the Ministry commits itself to achieving the
existing priority policies, which are consistent with the National Strategic Development Plan 2006-2010.

Another priority of the ESSP 2006-2010 is to take early and urgent action to
expand education and training opportunities for the number of young people aged
between 12 and 24 years who currently are school dropouts or have limited access to
post- primary education and training opportunities.

reform_title: Adoption of a French-based education system
reform_description:
Before 1975, the country adopted a French-based education system
that required 13 years of education (6+4+2+1) with four or five major examinations.

The ESP is emphatically pro-poor. The broad policy thrust is that the current
education poverty trap will be eliminated. The Plan focuses on a number of measures
that begin to eliminate the cost barriers to education for poor families, while still
assuring well-managed and relevant education.

The Ministry’s overall policy goal is to achieve Education for All (EFA) at the latest by 2015, including the increase of
enrolment rates, participation, attendance, and other quality-oriented indicators.
In the ESP plan period, the MoEYS considers as important to focus down on a narrower range of policy objectives and targets

the Ministry introduced targeted scholarships for the poor, girls and ethnic minority
students in grades 7 to 9.

In the period of ESP 2006-2010, the Ministry commits itself to achieving the
existing priority policies, which are consistent with the Cambodia
Millennium Development Goals 2015

According to Article 68, "The State shall provide free primary and secondary
education to all citizens in public schools. Citizens shall receive education for at least
nine years."

Within the new National Curriculum, students in grades 7-10 should receive
30 periods of teaching per week, each teaching period lasting fifty minutes, plus 2-5
additional 50-minute periods per week of the Local Life Skills Program (LLSP).
Students in grades 11-12 should receive 32 periods of teaching per week, each
teaching period lasting fifty minutes.

reform_title: The Education Sector Plan (ESP) financing policy
reform_description:
The fundamental thrust of the Education Sector Plan (ESP) financing policy
for 2001-05 was to reduce average parental contributions for basic education.

reform_title: Establishment of a network of nine public higher education institutions
reform_description:
A network of nine public higher education institutions have been established
which provide for instruction in areas such as agriculture, medicine, economics,
industry, technology, teacher training, science, art and culture.

reform_title: Policy limiting government involvement in higher education
reform_description:
The Government has adopted a policy of limiting its own involvement in
higher education to public funding of only 2,000 students per annum.

reform_title: Establishment of the Special Education Office
reform_description:
The newly established Special Education Office is located in the Primary and
Pre-School Department of the Ministry of Education. The responsibility of the Office
is to promote and support education for children with disabilities, minorities, girls and
other vulnerable groups.

The strategy of the NFE policy is defined as follows:
• Creating opportunities so that all people have access to lifelong learning.
• Promoting literacy and continuing education by creating links, which provide
credit, employment and vocational skills so that the population can live in
happiness.
• Regularly providing information and knowledge about vocational skill training
and technical and scientific subjects to everybody.
The Ministry of Education launched a ten-year education system structured as 4+3+3 to address the country's urgent post-conflict educational needs following the destruction of the previous system.
The education system was expanded from ten to eleven years as part of ongoing post-conflict reconstruction and curriculum reform efforts.
Articles 65–68 of the national Constitution established the right to free primary and secondary education for all citizens and mandated at least nine years of compulsory education.
The Ministry of Education Youth and Sports formulated an Education Investment Plan detailing priority strategies programmes and investment requirements for the education sector.
A new twelve-year education system structured as 6+3+3 was introduced along with reformed curriculum and new textbooks to replace the previous eleven-year system.

reform_title: Establishment of the Ministry of Education Youth and Sports
reform_description: The Ministry of Education Youth and Sports was formally established by national law (Kram) as the central government body responsible for education governance and administration.

reform_title: Establishment of Nationwide Education Management Information System (EMIS)
reform_description: A nationwide Education Management Information System was established under the Department of Planning to collect process and analyze school data across the country for use by educational administrators at all levels.

An institutional framework of school clusters was established to strengthen education quality and improve the efficiency of planning implementation and monitoring by grouping nearby schools for mutual technical and material assistance.
The government prepared its first Socio-Economic Development Plan setting out broad education development policies strategies and targets for the sector.

reform_title: Increased Teacher Entry Qualifications for Primary Teaching Service
reform_description: The entry qualifications for the primary teaching service were raised to require twelve years of schooling plus two years of teacher training in cities and towns with a 9+2 scheme applied in remote areas.

reform_title: Curriculum Development for Primary and Lower Secondary Education
reform_description: The government developed new curriculum student competencies textbooks and teacher manuals for primary and lower secondary education in Khmer mathematics and science including life skills and HIV/AIDS awareness.

The organizational structure of the Ministry of Education Youth and Sports was revised to establish five General Departments covering General Education; Higher Education and TVET; Youth and Sports; Administration Finance and Planning; and the Inspectorate.

The government introduced the Priority Action Programme mechanism providing protected and post-audit financing channelled from the central treasury to district accounts for the implementation of agreed education priorities.

reform_title: Three-Year Rolling Public Expenditure Plan (PEP) for Education
reform_description: The government introduced a three-year rolling Public Expenditure Plan projecting increases in education recurrent spending as a mechanism for medium-term education financing predictability and forward planning.

reform_title: Abolition of Start-of-Year Contributions in Grades 1–9
reform_description: The Ministry abolished mandatory start-of-year financial contributions for students in Grades 1 to 9 resulting in approximately 0.6 million additional student enrolments.

reform_title: Targeted Scholarships for Poor Girls and Ethnic Minority Students in Grades 7–9
reform_description: The Ministry introduced targeted scholarships for poor girls and ethnic minority students in Grades 7 to 9 to sustain demand and support disadvantaged groups in accessing lower secondary education.

The Ministry of Education Youth and Sports adopted an Education Strategic Plan first for 2001–2005 then revised for 2004–2008 and extended to 2006–2010 to guide sector reform with priorities of equitable access quality improvement and institutional development for decentralization.

The Ministry adopted the Education Sector Support Programme specifying reform strategies for quality and efficiency improvements at central provincial and institutional levels alongside financial reform through a joint MoEYS and Ministry of Economy and Finance committee.

reform_title: Pre-School Education Policy Framework and Early Childhood Care Guidelines
reform_description: Cambodia developed a national policy framework on pre-school education and guidelines on early childhood care and development.

A new National Curriculum was set out in December 2004 establishing structured teaching periods for primary and secondary education and introducing the Local Life Skills Program (LLSP) of 2–5 lessons per week across basic education.

A Special Education Office was newly established within the Primary and Pre-School Department of the Ministry of Education to promote and support education for children with disabilities minorities girls and other vulnerable groups.
The Ministry of Education launched a ten-year education system structured as 4+3+3 to address the country's urgent needs following a period of conflict.[cite: 1]
The government expanded the national education system from a ten-year to an eleven-year system.[cite: 1]
The Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports formulated this plan to detail priority strategies, programs, and investment requirements for education development.[cite: 1]
Introduction of a new twelve-year education system (6+3+3) accompanied by curriculum reform, new textbook development, and new skills training for teachers.[cite: 1]
The government prepared its first broad socio-economic plan which set out comprehensive education development policies, strategies, and targets.[cite: 1]

reform_title: Establishment of the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports
reform_description: A formal Law (Kram) was enacted to officially establish the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports.[cite: 1]
Establishment of a nationwide institutional framework grouping schools into clusters to provide mutual technical assistance, strengthen education quality, and improve planning efficiency.[cite: 1]
The government established a nationwide Education Management Information System (EMIS) center tasked with collecting, processing, and compiling data from all schools across the country.[cite: 1]
The government permitted the establishment of private universities, stimulating the growth of private provision and market-oriented programs in higher education.[cite: 1]
The organizational structure of the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports was revised into five General Departments and an Inspectorate.[cite: 1]
The Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports reduced the higher education admission cycle from two test periods to one to improve efficiency and reduce costs.[cite: 1]
Introduction of a new financing mechanism providing protected, post-audit funding directly to district accounts to facilitate local implementation of agreed education priorities.[cite: 1]
The government introduced a three-year rolling Public Expenditure Plan that restructured forward-looking education financing and projected significant increases in recurrent spending.[cite: 1]
The Ministry abolished start-of-the-year financial contributions for students in grades 1 to 9, leading to the enrollment of approximately 0.6 million additional students.[cite: 1]
The Ministry introduced targeted scholarships specifically for poor, female, and ethnic minority students in grades 7 to 9 to sustain and expand educational demand.[cite: 1]
A comprehensive pro-poor strategic plan designed to eliminate cost barriers to basic education, restructure spending, and introduce new partnerships in education planning.[cite: 1]
A systemic reform strategy emphasizing quality, efficiency, and financial reform across central, provincial, and institutional levels of the education sector.[cite: 1]
Cambodia developed and adopted a national policy framework on pre-school education and official guidelines on early childhood care and development.[cite: 1]
A new national curriculum was introduced that standardized teaching periods and introduced a Local Life Skills Program (LLSP) for extra-curricular and pre-vocational activities.[cite: 1]
The Ministry of Education launched a ten-year education structure organized as four years of primary, three years of lower secondary, and three years of upper secondary education after 1979.
The Ministry of Education expanded the post-1979 education structure from a ten-year system to an eleven-year system, in place from 1986 to 1996.
The Ministry introduced a twelve-year education structure consisting of six years of primary, three years of lower secondary, and three years of upper secondary education in the 1996/97 school year.

reform_title: Plan for Rebuilding Quality Education and Training
reform_description: A government education plan identified as a key milestone in the early 1990s shift toward policy development, basic needs provision, quality improvement, expanded teacher training, textbook supply, and examination improvement.
MoEYS formulated an education investment plan detailing priority strategies, programmes, and investment requirements for education reconstruction and development.

reform_title: Socio-Economic Development Plan I education policies
reform_description: The first national Socio-Economic Development Plan set out broad education development policies, strategies, and targets for 1996-2000.

MoEYS established a school cluster institutional framework to strengthen education quality and improve planning, implementation, and monitoring, including participatory bottom-up planning in UNICEF-supported provinces.

reform_title: Primary and lower secondary curriculum and textbook development
reform_description: MoEYS developed curriculum, student competencies, textbooks, and teacher manuals for Khmer, mathematics, and science in primary and lower secondary education, with teacher training on the new materials and life skills including HIV/AIDS awareness.
MoEYS established a nationwide Education Management Information System to collect, process, analyze, and distribute school statistics and indicators for education administrators.

reform_title: Law establishing the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports
reform_description: A Law (Kram) dated 24 January 1996 established the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports.
MoEYS raised entry qualifications for the primary teaching service to twelve years of schooling plus two years of teacher training in cities and towns, with a 9+2 scheme applied in remote areas.
With donor assistance, textbooks for Grades 7-9 began to be provided on a one book per student per subject basis.
The establishment of private universities stimulated growth of private provision and more market-oriented programmes in higher education.

MoEYS revised its organizational structure into five General Departments covering general education, higher education and TVET, youth and sports, administration finance and planning, and the Inspectorate.
MoEYS reduced the higher education admission cycle from two test periods to one, improving efficiency and reducing costs.

The Government introduced the Priority Action Programme mechanism, providing protected and post-audit financing for agreed education priorities and channeling funds to district accounts through provincial treasuries.

MoEYS introduced an Education Strategic Plan to reform education services, expand access to quality education and training, reduce cost barriers for poor families, increase local authority in education management, and strengthen accountability and partnership.

MoEYS specified its reform strategy in the Education Sector Support Programme, guiding education-sector development through policies and programmes emphasizing quality, efficiency, and financial reform.

reform_title: Abolition of start-of-year contributions in Grades 1-9
reform_description: MoEYS abolished start-of-year parental contributions in Grades 1-9, reducing cost barriers to basic education.

reform_title: Targeted scholarships for disadvantaged lower secondary students
reform_description: MoEYS introduced targeted scholarships for poor, female, and ethnic minority students in Grades 7-9 to sustain demand for schooling and support disadvantaged groups.

reform_title: Pre-school education and early childhood care policy framework
reform_description: Cambodia developed a policy framework on pre-school education and guidelines on early childhood care and development.

MoEYS revised the Education Strategic Plan for 2004-2008, defining strategies and cost implications for improved staff remuneration, performance, responsibility, staff deployment, and new forms of staff training and development.

MoEYS set out a new National Curriculum requiring revised instructional periods, Local Life Skills Program periods in primary and Grades 7-10, compulsory foreign languages at lower secondary level, subject choice guidance in Grade 10, and elective vocational education opportunities in Grades 11-12.

MoEYS extended the Education Strategic Plan for 2006-2010 with priority policies on equitable access, quality and efficiency of education services, and institutional development and capacity building for decentralization.

MoEYS adopted the Education Sector Support Programme for 2006-2010, including programmes to eliminate informal payments in Grades 1-9, expand ECE, increase school operating support, expand scholarships, improve teacher supply and deployment, provide textbooks nationwide, expand re-entry and non-formal programmes, and strengthen monitoring.

The Royal Government recognized non-formal education as an official education system, mandated flexible NFE activities and curriculum components including literacy, vocational skills, continuing education, family education, and re-entry programmes, and allocated a separate expenditure line for NFE.
MoEYS established a Special Education Office in the Primary and Pre-School Department to promote and support education for children with disabilities, minorities, girls, and other vulnerable groups.

The TVU system was started in the early 1960s to train science
teachers but was closed during the Cultural Revolution.

The TVU system resumed broadcasting in 1979 with the establishment of a Central Radio and
Television University (CRTVU) and 28 provincial television universities (PTVUs).

reform_title: Establishment of the International Investment and Loan Office (IILO)
reform_description: the International Investment and Loan Office (IILO) established within MOE in 1982.

reform_title: Resolution on the Reform of the Science and Technology System
reform_description:
In 1985, the Government released its "Resolution on the
Reform of the Science and Technology System", which signalled the beginning of an explicit attempt at

reform_title: Establishment of the Central Radio and Television University (CRTVU) and Provincial Television University System
reform_description: The government re-established and expanded the Television University system after its closure during the Cultural Revolution by creating a Central Radio and Television University (CRTVU) administered by the Ministry of Education and 28 provincial television universities (PTVUs) administered and funded by provincial governments. The system broadcast educational programs nationally and offered unified curricula in science technology and social sciences to enrolled workers and secondary school graduates.

reform_title: Establishment of the National Polytechnic System
reform_description: The Chinese government decided to establish a new system of polytechnics (called Short-term Vocational Colleges) offering two to three years of postsecondary technical education to local residents. The polytechnics were designed to be non-residential institutions charging student fees and not guaranteeing job assignments to graduates introducing market forces into higher education. The government launched the first phase with 17 polytechnics with plans to expand to approximately one hundred by 1990.

reform_title: New Higher Education Policies for Polytechnics
reform_description: The government formally adopted three new policies to be implemented across the 17 polytechnics: non-residential institutions accepting only day students from the local municipality; introduction of student tuition fees of Y50 per student; and elimination of guaranteed government job assignment for polytechnic graduates replacing it with placement assistance through local enterprises.

The project also included curriculum development staff training technical assistance and establishment of evaluation and planning units within the Ministry of Education and CRTVU.

governance and fighting corruption; restoring security throughout the country; rebuilding

health services.

The Education Sector Project contributes to the higher level objectives set out by the
Transitional Support Strategy (TSS) in February 2004 to mitigate the acute risks perpetuating the
erosion of social capital in the DRC. The Project will also
provide the building blocks to devise a medium term education sector development program.

In order to achieve its objectives, the Project will provide support for: (i) reducing primary
school fees and providing free access to textbooks to increase equity in the supply of primary
education services; (ii) building quality through monitoring learning achievement and improving
the status for teachers; (iii) restoring access to primary education of at least minimal quality,
especially in areas affected by the conflict of the last decade; (iv) providing technical assistance
and material support to build the system capacity of government and prepare policies which
update and re-structure the legal governance, financing, and administration of the education
sector; and (v) strengthening accountability relationships and empowering local communities to
enable them to take part in school-level decisions and oversee the supply of education services.

The Project will also support strengthening accountability and empowering local
communities to participate in school-level decisions. Citizen’s Report Cards (CRCs) conducted
on an annual basis during the Project aim to permit students and parents to assess primary
education along basic quality dimensions. A Needs Assessment of training needs for parent
committees, parent-teacher associations (PTAs), and teachers’ trade unions will establish a
program at local levels for strengthening their capacity for planning, budgeting, financial
management, and fighting corruption. The objective is to empower school communities to work
better in their function as oversight bodies, and strengthen their abilities to influence school
management in more efficient ways. In addition, a communication strategy that covers all
phases and components of the Project will be formulated. The strategy is to facilitate both the
flow of information and the dialogue on the fee policy, governance, corruption, tendering of
contracts under the Project and other service delivery issues. The Project will assess compliance
through technical audits and through focus group discussions with school management and
parent committees.

Component 3: Strengthen Institutional and Financial Capacity of the Education Sector
The objective of this component is to contribute to revising the legal and institutional
framework of the education system, and to reinforce institutional capacities for planning, budget
formulation, and program execution. Key aspects include the career structure of teachers,
teacher training, and development of coherent, technically sound and results-oriented strategies
for education sector development which are based on realistic financing plans and budgets.

Sub-Component 3a Support Policy Reform-Teacher Career Structure (US$0.91 million).
This sub-component will provide technical advisory services to support policy reform in the
field of pre-service and in-service teacher training, including inter alia: (i) analysis of existing
resources and reform options for pre-service teacher education; (ii) evaluation of existing in-
service teacher training experiences and options for scaling up; (iii) feasibility studies for reform,
including study tours in selected countries; (iv) assistance to a National Commission to be
created to review the career structure and reward system for teachers and draft the policy
document on the proposed reform; and (v) information and communication activities to help
build national consensus on reform proposals. The new training policies will be formulated in
tandem and linked with the Government’s redefinition of the career structure of teachers within
the ongoing civil service reform. Expected results are: (i) anew statute on teachers’ careers and
salaries, formulated and adopted within the civil service reform; (ii) a plan to modernize the
system of pre-service teacher training; and (iii) a strategy and action plan for in-service teacher
training with the view to providing certification in line with the new statute and guidelines on
teacher qualifications.

Sub-Component 3b Support Preparation of a Sector-wide Education Strategy (US$4.15
million).
This sub-component will provide support to the formulation and implementation of: (i) a
comprehensive education strategy including an appropriate legal framework and financing plans
for all relevant sub-sectors including primary, secondary and higher education and literacy
programs; and (ii) pilot programs designed to increase literacy and school remediation across the

strengthen civil society’s powers to oversee and influence decisions at school levels. CRC
surveys will be used as an instrument for empowering parents as well as to get feedback on
implementation and results on the ground. These interventions will seek to strengthen and
establish sustainable accountability relations between beneficiaries and service providers.

As the DRC starts to recover from decades of conflict, economic crisis, and
mismanagement, the Project will:
(i) Provide an injection of resources into the severely under-resourced education sector;
(ii) Prevent further deterioration in the delivery of essential services for primary education;
(iii) Reduce the burden of financing education largely borne by households in a country
where estimated per capita income is among the lowest in the world; and
(iv) Initiate the establishment of broader accountability mechanisms between service
providers, beneficiaries, and the political level.

The Project has been
designed around an assumption, based on documented research, that parents will find education
to be of greater value if they can see visible improvements in the availability of essential inputs -
textbooks, teachers trained to use them, classrooms which offer adequate shelter from the
elements - and can see that this translates into learning in the classroom. This is the rationale for
including a sub-component to scale out assessments of learning achievement and to build up a
culture of monitoring the relationship between quality of education, learning and retention in
school. It is expected that this will help to raise teacher awareness that they are accountable for
learning in the classroom, and that the research will also generate specific recommendations
which can be fed into the policy work on teacher development and other areas.

Works procured under this Project would include (i) the
rehabilitation of about 260 schools including approximately 1570 classrooms scattered in all
provinces, and (ii) the rehabilitation of the National Pedagogical University (UPN) in Kinshasa.

The project addresses the above issues by reducing the cost of education to households and
rehabilitating 1570 classrooms, which will boost both the demand for, and supply of, education.
Expected results include a turn around from the decade-long decline of the gross enrollment rate
in primary education to an increase of enrollment rates from 64 percent in 2007 to 75 percent by
Project end in 2012. The proportion of girls is also expected to grow from 85 percent to 95
percent during the same period.

The Project covers the entire country and aims to rehabilitate 1570 primary school classrooms
and the National Pedagogical University.

reform_title: Constitution Mandating Free and Compulsory Primary Education
reform_description: The DRC Constitution stipulated that primary education is compulsory and fee-free for all children establishing a legal right to free basic education at the primary level.

reform_title: Arrêté No MINEPSP/CABMIN/6635/2005 – Primary School Fee Abolition
reform_description: A ministerial bylaw announced that all but four fees (Minerval school insurance and two forms of examination fees) were no longer to be charged for primary school effectively establishing the illegitimacy of the two main fees FDM and FDF which together made up more than three quarters of all fees paid by households.

reform_title: 100 Percent Increase in Primary and Secondary Teacher Salaries
reform_description: The government used domestic budget resources to implement an average 100 percent increase in primary and secondary teacher salaries nationwide to end teacher strikes and reduce household co-financing of teacher compensation.

reform_title: HIPC-Funded Block Grant Program for School Operating Costs
reform_description: The government introduced a block grant program using HIPC funds to reduce running costs at publicly administered schools covering approximately 22900 primary and secondary schools with approximately US$530 per school per year to offset the collection of FDF fees.

reform_title: Inter-Ministerial Steering Committee for Education (Arrêté Inter-Ministériel EPSP-ESU-AS No0510/2005)
reform_description: Creation of an inter-ministerial steering committee comprising the Ministry of Primary Secondary and Professional Education the Ministry of Higher Education and Universities and the Ministry of Social Affairs and National Solidarity to coordinate education sector investments and oversee project preparation and implementation.

reform_title: Revised Block Grant Allocation Criteria for Schools
reform_description: As a follow-up to the initial block grant program the government set new criteria for allocation of block grants according to the size of the school to address inequities and inefficiencies in the original flat-rate distribution.

A World Bank IDA grant of US$150 million to the DRC government to prevent deterioration in primary education services and prepare for sustainable sector development including reducing primary school fees integrating 30000 teachers onto the public payroll rehabilitating 1570 classrooms providing textbooks for grades 1-2 building learning assessment capacity and developing a sector-wide education strategy.

reform_title: Simplified Temporary Payment System (Paie Transitoire Simplifiée – PTS) for Teacher Salaries
reform_description: Establishment of a simplified temporary payment system to integrate all teachers currently enrolled in the public payroll schedule furnished by SECOPE and to progressively regularize teachers onto the public payroll with SECOPE granted exclusive authority to handle all payments of teachers' salaries.

A World Bank-financed project aimed at increasing access to primary education by subsidizing school operating costs to reduce household fees, rehabilitating classrooms, providing textbooks, establishing learning assessment capacity at the National Pedagogical University (UPN), and strengthening institutional capacity to develop a comprehensive sector-wide education strategy.

reform_title: Freeze on Primary School Personnel Transfers and New School Openings
reform_description: The enactment of ministerial and inter-ministerial bylaws freezing the transfer of primary school personnel and halting the opening or accreditation of new public primary schools until a comprehensive census of primary schools, students, classrooms, and teachers is completed.

reform_title: Simplified Temporary Payment System (Paie Transitoire Simplifiée)
reform_description: The integration of all teachers currently enrolled in the public payroll schedule into a new Simplified Temporary Payment System (PTS) to streamline and manage the payment of teachers' salaries.

reform_title: Fee-Free and Compulsory Primary Education in the Constitution
reform_description: The 2005 Constitution established primary education as compulsory and fee-free, creating a national legal basis for universal primary education and reducing household cost barriers.

The government introduced a block grant program using HIPC funds to subsidize operating costs at publicly administered primary and secondary schools and reduce school running-cost fees charged to households.

reform_title: Arrêté No. MINEPSP/CABMIN/6635/2005 on Primary School Fees
reform_description: A ministerial order prohibited most primary school fees, allowing only four specified charges and declaring the two main household fees for teacher motivation and school running costs illegitimate.
The Ministry of Primary, Secondary and Professional Education developed and distributed a new primary-level curriculum to schools with support from UNICEF, UNESCO, and Belgian Cooperation.
The government, with Belgian Cooperation support, delivered reading and mathematics textbooks for grades 5 and 6 to all primary schools in the country.

reform_title: Emergency Urban and Social Rehabilitation Project School Block Grants
reform_description: The government, through the World Bank-financed Emergency Urban and Social Rehabilitation Project approved in March 2007, financed national block grants to primary and secondary schools to offset school operating fees.

reform_title: Education Sector Project Primary Fee and Teacher Payroll Reform
reform_description: The IDA-financed Education Sector Project supported eliminating parents’ monetary contributions for primary school operating costs and integrating 30000 primary teachers into the public payroll.

reform_title: Inter-Ministerial Bylaw on Primary Education Fees
reform_description: As an effectiveness condition of the Education Sector Project, the government was to enact an inter-ministerial bylaw establishing an exclusive nomenclature and fee levels for primary education.

reform_title: Primary Teacher Payroll Safeguards and SECOPE Authority Reform
reform_description: The Education Sector Project required reforms to the teacher payment system, including integrating payroll-listed teachers into the Simplified Temporary Payment System and granting SECOPE exclusive authority to handle teacher salary payments.

reform_title: Primary School Personnel and Accreditation Freeze
reform_description: The Education Sector Project required bylaws freezing personnel transfers in primary schools and freezing the opening or accreditation of new public primary schools until a comprehensive census of schools, students, classrooms, and teachers was completed.

The Education Sector Project supported the establishment of a sustainable national strategy for school rehabilitation, including low-cost construction norms, community participation, maintenance systems, and rehabilitation of primary classrooms.

reform_title: School-Community Accountability and Citizen Report Cards
reform_description: The Education Sector Project introduced annual Citizen Report Cards and capacity-building for parent committees, parent-teacher associations, and teacher unions to strengthen community oversight of primary education services.

reform_title: SECOPE Modernization and Public Expenditure Tracking
reform_description: The Education Sector Project strengthened SECOPE’s central and provincial database, resource management, and monitoring capacity, and introduced Public Expenditure Tracking Surveys for teacher salary payments and primary school operating expenditures.

reform_title: Grade 1-2 Textbook Provision and Textbook Management System
reform_description: The Education Sector Project financed national provision of reading and mathematics textbooks for grades 1 and 2, teacher guides, textbook-use training, school textbook management manuals, and a MEPSP database to track textbook stocks.

The Education Sector Project established capacity at the National Pedagogical University and the Inspectorate to assess learning achievement, conduct research on learning and retention, and administer rounds of primary education assessments.

reform_title: Teacher Career Structure and Training Reform
reform_description: The Education Sector Project supported creation of a new teacher career and salary statute within civil service reform, a plan to modernize pre-service teacher training, and an in-service teacher training strategy linked to certification and qualification guidelines.

The Education Sector Project required the government to establish a representative structure to oversee formulation and implementation of a comprehensive education strategy with legal framework and financing plans for primary, secondary, higher education, and literacy programs.

In accordance with the Law No. 49 of 1972, Egyptian universities are public
entities that have both an academic and cultural nature and an autonomous character.
A university has the freedom to accept donations that do not contradict its ultimate
aim. The Law also stipulates that every university must have a separate budget similar
to the budgets of public entities.

reform_description:
Since 1990, educational policy has been formulated within a democratic framework.
Thus, many individuals, and particularly through the conferences, are given an
opportunity to take part in the decision-making process concerning education.

previously acquired in everyday life activities for fear of a relapse into illiteracy.

* Expanding teaching foreign languages side by side with the set official

* Teaching special curricula according to a decision from the Minister of

reform_title: Government intentions to promote the improvement of children’s health and education
reform_description:
In 2001, the government announced its intention to promote the improvement
of children’s health and education by increasing the enrollment rates for KG
programmes, and reforming early childhood education. The specific aims are to
provide a place for each child in a preschool institution; integrate preschool into the
compulsory education system for the age group 4-5; and increase the percentage of
children in preschool from 13 to 60% by 2010.

reform_title: New requirements for public KG teacher applicants
reform_description:
New applicants for public KG teacher positions are required to have at least
a bachelor’s degree with specialization in education and early childhood development.

The main areas of reform identified under the Egypt’s Higher Education
Enhancement Programme (HEEP) include: administrative restructuring and
management development; use of information technology and new teaching
methodologies as well as setting-up a multimedia national center; establishment of a
comprehensive faculty development programme; development of faculties of
education; development of higher and middle technical institutes; setting up library
automation and centers of excellence; enhancement of graduate studies and research,
establishment of quality assurance and accreditation mechanisms; establishment of a
National Qualifications Framework (NQF); and encouragement of distant and lifelong
learning.

The owner of the school, or the person who represents him/her, is responsible
for its management through implementing the Education Law. He/she is also
responsible for the maintenance of the building, the school equipment and furniture,
and school expansion. Moreover, he/she is in charge of studying the staff needs,
wages and remuneration.

reform_title: Ministerial decrees applied in public schools
reform_description:
Councils of parents and teachers, and student unions are established in private
schools according to the ministerial decrees applied in public schools.

reform_title: Comprehensive plan to develop education through technology
reform_description:
The Ministry has put forward a comprehensive plan to develop education
through technology, considering that technology is not an end in itself but a means to
the real end of educational development.

Advanced science laboratories are also established in 500 schools and another
fifty schools are being equipped. These laboratories are being generalized in the
framework of the One Thousand Schools project (400 secondary, 200 preparatory,
200 primary and 200 kindergartens throughout the country).

The E-government project has been completed and now connects all schools in
the country (around 35,000 schools) with the Ministry main office and with each other
through the Intranet of the Ministry. They are also connected with the information
international network (250 educational administrations in 27 educational directorates).

which now falls under the umbrella of university faculties and other higher education
institutes

reform_title: Law No. 103/1961 on the Re-organization of Al-Azhar
reform_description: National law restructuring Al-Azhar and its educational bodies into two types of institutes: those providing basic Islamic and general education and qualitative institutes preparing students in religious and Arab sciences.

reform_title: Law No. 49/1972 on the Organization of Universities
reform_description: National law establishing Egyptian universities as autonomous public entities with independent budgets governed by university councils and the Supreme Council of Universities.
National law establishing the right to free education for all citizens and setting compulsory education at eight years covering primary (five years) and preparatory (three years) cycles.

reform_title: Law No. 233/1988 Amendment to Pre-University Education Law
reform_description: Amendment to Law No. 139/1981 modifying provisions regarding pre-university education.

reform_title: Law No. 227/1989 Establishing an Education Financing Fund
reform_description: National law establishing a fund for supporting and financing educational projects.

reform_title: 1989 Teacher Qualification Policy for Primary Education
reform_description: Government policy mandating that all primary education teachers must hold a university degree specializing in education (B.Ed) as a minimum qualification for appointment.

reform_title: Establishment of the Centre for Developing Curricula and Educational Materials
reform_description: Creation of a national centre responsible for preparing revising modifying and developing school curricula content across all education levels in collaboration with consultative committees.

reform_title: Law No. 8/1991 on the Eradication of Illiteracy and Adult Education
reform_description: National law designating literacy and adult education as a national duty and establishing a General Authority for Illiteracy Eradication to plan coordinate and evaluate illiteracy eradication and adult education programs.

reform_title: Ministerial Decree No. 71/1993 Restructuring the Primary School Cycle
reform_description: Ministerial decree dividing the primary school cycle into two levels: Grades I-III focusing on basic literacy and numeracy skills and Grades IV-VI focusing on functional application of those skills.
Ministerial decree establishing the regulatory framework for private schools including objectives staffing curriculum requirements examination rules and supervision by educational directorates.

reform_title: Reform of In-Service Teacher Training System
reform_description: Reorganization of in-service teacher training by internal training centres in collaboration with university faculties introducing distance learning and training missions abroad in addition to face-to-face training.

Reform of primary education curricula to incorporate recent technological and scientific progress reduce the number of school subjects and quantity of information and emphasize skills-based and self-directed learning.

reform_title: Law No. 2/1994 Amendment to Pre-University Education Law

reform_title: Law No. 23/1999 Extending Compulsory Basic Education to Nine Years
reform_description: Amendment to pre-university education law extending compulsory basic education from eight to nine years covering the six-year primary cycle and three-year preparatory cycle for children aged 6-15.

reform_description: A government program to equip 400 secondary 200 preparatory 200 primary and 200 kindergarten schools nationwide with multimedia laboratories advanced science laboratories and computer equipment.

reform_title: Ministerial Decree No. 99/2003 Introduction of English in Grade 1
reform_description: Ministerial decree requiring the introduction of English language teaching in the first grade at three periods per week starting from the school year 2003/04.

reform_title: National Project for Literacy and Adult Education 2003-2007
reform_description: A national government project launched in July 2003 targeting the literacy of approximately 7.5 million persons aged 14-45 years over a four-year period.

reform_title: Law No. 103 on the Re-organization of Al-Azhar
reform_description: A national law reorganizing the Al-Azhar education system and its bodies establishing distinct institutes for basic education in Islamic and Arab culture as well as specialized institutes for foreign students.

reform_title: Law No. 49 on the Organization of Universities
reform_description: A higher education law organizing Egyptian universities as autonomous public entities with academic and cultural independence and the right to manage separate budgets.

A national education law that established an eight-year compulsory basic education system consisting of a five-year primary cycle and a three-year preparatory cycle and guaranteed free public education.

reform_title: Law No. 227 Establishing an Educational Projects Fund
reform_description: National legislation that created a dedicated fund for supporting and financing educational projects.

reform_title: Establishment of the Centre for Developing Curricula and Educational Materials
reform_description: The creation of a national center dedicated to preparing revising modifying and developing the content of various school curricula.

reform_title: Law No. 8 on the Eradication of Illiteracy and Adult Education
reform_description: A national law identifying adult literacy as a national duty and establishing a general authority with defined functions to oversee illiteracy eradication efforts.

A ministerial decree organizing the field of private education by regulating the establishment objectives curricula and examination systems of private schools to align with or supplement public education.
A government initiative sending teachers to advanced countries for training in modern teaching methods and the use of advanced technology in education.
National legislation and subsequent bylaws focusing on child protection and the development of pre-primary and early childhood education.

reform_title: Law No. 103 on the Re-organization of Al-Azhar
reform_description: Reorganized Al-Azhar and its bodies, establishing Al-Azhar institutes for basic education in Islamic and Arab culture, general education, religious and Arab sciences, and Quran reading preparation.

reform_title: Law No. 49 on the Organization of Universities
reform_description: Established the legal organization of Egyptian universities as autonomous public academic and cultural entities with separate budgets, university councils, and governance responsibilities.
National pre-university education law establishing free education rights and compulsory basic education, initially covering eight years across primary and preparatory cycles.

reform_title: Law No. 227 Establishing the Education Project Support Fund
reform_description: Created a fund to support and finance educational projects.

reform_title: University Degree Requirement for Primary Teachers
reform_description: Mandated that all primary education teachers hold a university degree specializing in education, raising qualification requirements for primary teaching.

reform_title: Law No. 8 on Illiteracy Eradication and Adult Education
reform_description: Declared literacy and adult education a national duty and established a general authority responsible for planning, coordinating, and evaluating literacy and adult education programs.

Reorganized in-service teacher training through internal training centers in collaboration with faculties, expanding training methods to include face-to-face training, distance learning, and overseas training missions.

reform_title: Ministerial Decree No. 71 on Primary Cycle Structure
reform_description: Divided the primary school cycle into two levels: Grades I-III for basic literacy, numeracy, and religious education, and Grades IV-V/VI for functional use of basic skills.

reform_title: National Conference Decisions on Primary Education Curricula
reform_description: Introduced primary curriculum reforms emphasizing basic skills, self-education, reduced information load, updated scientific content, and more active teaching and learning strategies.

Regulated private education by defining objectives, management requirements, curriculum obligations, fee rules, licensing conditions, supervision, examination rules, and staff evaluation requirements for private schools.

reform_title: National Conference Decisions on Preparatory Education Curricula
reform_description: Introduced preparatory curriculum reforms emphasizing skills, understanding and analysis rather than rote memorization, labor-market-relevant abilities, updated content, and active learning methods.

reform_title: Law No. 12 on Child Protection and Early Childhood Education
reform_description: Established a legal framework supporting the development of pre-primary education and early childhood education and development.

reform_title: Law No. 23 Extending Compulsory Basic Education
reform_description: Extended compulsory basic education to nine years for ages 6-15, covering a six-year primary cycle and three-year preparatory cycle.

reform_title: Reform of Parents' Councils and Establishment of Trustee Councils
reform_description: Strengthened societal participation in education by reforming parents' councils to monitor education and establishing community trustee councils to support schools financially and connect them with local environments.

Introduced a higher education reform programme covering administrative restructuring, management development, information technology, new teaching methodologies, faculty development, technical institute development, library automation, centers of excellence, graduate studies and research, quality assurance, accreditation, a National Qualifications Framework, and distance and lifelong learning.

reform_title: Ministerial Decree No. 65 and No. 15 on Kindergartens
reform_description: Issued additional regulations for kindergartens as part of the development of pre-primary and early childhood education.

reform_title: National Project for Literacy and Adult Education 2003-2007
reform_description: Launched a national literacy and adult education project targeting literacy for about 7.5 million people aged 14-45.

reform_description: Introduced English teaching in the first grade beginning in the 2003/04 school year.
