reform_title: Basic Research and Implementation in Developing Education Systems Project (BRIDGES)
reform_description:
In 1986 USAID arranged for the Basic Research and Implementation in
Developing Education Systems Project (BRIDGES), directed by the Harvard Institute for
International Development, to work with the Ministry of Education to identify ways to improve
the management and organization of the education system. The objective of the BRIDGES project was to work cooperatively with the MOE
toward the development of improved educational planning, an educational management
information systems (EMIS), and policy research and analysis.

The importance attached to basic education stems from the crucial roles it plays in developing fundamental skills, such as literacy and numeracy, and in preparing children for more advanced levels of education, both of which are necessary for the continued socioeconomic development of the country. A critical step toward this end has already been taken by the Ministry of Education (MOE) through a series of reforms aimed at improving the quality, availability and efficiency of the education
system. The primary objective of this reform program, which began in 1987 and has received
support from the US Agency for International Development (USAID), the British Overseas
Development Agency (ODA), the United Nations Children's Fund (UNICEF) and other donors,
has been the expansion of the basic education system to provide universal access at that level.
Significant emphasis has also been placed on improving MOE's institutional information and
planning capabilities, and on developing new curricula and teaching methods for the classrooms.

reform_title: Consolidation of the primary level from six to five grade
reform_description:
In 1988 the primary level was consolidated from six to five grades, which resulted in a drop in
primary school enrollments and an increase in secondary school enrollments due to the
preparatory existence of a double cohort (see Statistical Charts 1-3).

In 1988, Dr. Fathi Sourour, Minister
of Education, presented a new five-year plan for educational reform. That reform was aimed at a
much improved quality of basic education, relevant to Egypt's modern requirements, while
simultaneously expanding access to achieve universal participatiort.

reform_title: Shortening of the basic education stage
reform_description:
The shortening of the basic education stage in 1989 from nine to eight years, has
relieved some of the erollment pressure on schools by reducing class sizes at the primary stage.

reform_title: Development of a full range of new curricula
reform_description:
As part of MOE's reform program, three new semiautonomous agencies were
established by ministerial decrees in 1990 and 1991 to help direct improvements in these areas.
The first of these agencies, the Center for Curriculum and Instructional Materials Development
(CCIMD), has completed the development of a full range of new curricula. Its first product, a
new subject termed 'environmental studies', combines science, home economics, agriculture and
social studies to show children how these subjects relate to their lives; it was introduced into
grades 1-3 nationwide for the 1991-92 school year. Preliminary indications are that this new subject is being adequately utilized by teachers and students. In addition, new textbooks for
mathematics, science, Arabic, religion, art and physical education were field-tested earlier this
year and are expected to be introduced gradually nationwide in grades 1-5 for the 1992-93 and
1993-94 school years. Other instructional materials are being evaluated and revised and will be
introduced during the 1992-93 school year. Topics such as population studies, environmental
protection and even tourism are being integrated into the new curriculum, with emphasis placed
on developing children's abilities to observe, compare, analyze and predict.

reform_title: Changes introduced by the National Center for Examinations and Educational Evaluation (NCEEE)
reform_description:
changes are being introduced by the National Center for
Examinations and Educational Evaluation (NCEEE), with ODA assistance, in the area of testing
and evaluation, including the revision of national achievement tests to include questions that
measure a student's reasoning and critical thinking abilities. Furthermore, semiannual exams are
being devised to improve the validity of the test results, and to reduce the importance of the end-
of-year and end-of-cycle examinations.

reform_title: Eelevation of the preservice requirement for basic-level teaching to a university degree
reform_description:
Recognizing the need to upgrade teacher quality and reduce the oversupply of
inadequately trained teachers, MOE decided in 1988 to elevate the preservice requimnt for
basic-level teaching to a university degree

reform_title: Phasing out and transformation of Teachers' Institutes
reform_description:
Recognizing the need to upgrade teacher quality and reduce the oversupply of
inadequately trained teachers, MOE decided in 1988 to phase out the Teachers' Institutes by 1993. Many of the phased-out Teachers' Institutes have been transformed into specialized colleges of
education to train, at the university level, teachers of art, music, physical education, home
economics and educational technology.

reform_title: Establishment of a national board to promote functional literacy
reform_description:
A newly established national board to
promote functionaliteracy, assistance by many donors, is implementing nationwide programs.

The proposed IDA Basic Education Project is intended to be the first in a series of
operations in the subsector aimed at preserving and consolidating MOE's achievements to date in
its reform of the basic education system. The project would assist the Governmento improve
and accelerate the implementation of its strategy to (i) stn MOE's institutional capacity to
effectively manage the system; (ii) improve instructional quality; (iii) provide universal access to
basic education, with special focus on females and the poor; (ih) reduce illiteracy among
children; and (v) mobilize community support and resources for the education sector. Through
measures in a selected key areas of intervention, the specific objectives of the project are
fourfold:
(a) to improve access and equity through a well designed school construction and
maintenance program that could be used as a model by MOE;
(b) to improve the quality of teaching and consolidate advancements in curricula
design through reinforcement of the in-service teacher training system;
(c) to enhance MOE's institutional capacity in policy analysis, management and
education planning through training of MOE staff and further development and
efficient use of an education management information system (EMIS); and
(d) to help defie policy options and alermatives in addressing two selected priority
education issues through studies to complement ongoing Bank-sponsored public
sector investment and human resource development studies.

To accomplish the above objectives, the project would comprise the following four
interrelated components: (i) school construction and maintenance; (ii) in-service teacher training;
(iii) institutional development in management, informton and planning in MOE; and (iv) relatd
policy-oriented studies.

School Construction and Maintenance
The primary objective of this component is to increase primary and preparatory
school enrollments in urban and rural schools in seven priority governorates-balancing the needs
to reduce severe problems of overcrowding and multiple shifts in urban areas and to improve
access for girls and the poor in rural areas. Furthermore, the component would (i) ensure
adequate financial and manpower resources for the maintenance of schools constructed under the
project; (ii) consolidate in the seven priorty governorates the maintnance initives begun by
the Minister involving local communities; and (iii) strengthen the institutional capacities of
MOE's Central Directorate for Educational Planning and Informadon (CDEPI) and the General Authority for Educational Buildings (GAEB) to carry out their respective roles in planning and
executing school construction and maintenance work.

Consistent with the Ministry's priority defined in its Five-Year School
Construction Plan, the proposed component would allow for three phases of construction of
primary and preparatory level schools in areas where there are currendy no such facilities, where
enrollment of girls is low, or where overcrowding is severe and/or there are multiple shifts.
Owing to the modest size of the proposed IDA credit, relative to the total physical and fiancial
needs of the basic education systems and to the serious impact of the recent earthquake (Annex
1), the proposed component has targeted seven governorates and specific areas within each to fit
the project criteria for school construction, with the selection reviewed by the IDA twam during
appraisal. IDA would finance only school construction (civil works), while the Govermnent
would provide all the needed equipment and furniture for the IDA-financed schools. MOE shall
obtain the Association's concurrence to the plans for furnishing and equipping the schools
included in the prcject. Worth noting is that the project would establish a new sustainable
methodology for the development of school designs and a functionalinkage between the MIS and
the identification of needs for schools, teachers and maintenance.

Consistent with the Ministry's priority defined in its Five-Year School
Construction Plan, the proposed component would allow for three phases of construction of
primary and preparatory level schools in areas where there are currendy no such facilities, where
enrollment of girls is low, or where overcrowding is severe and/or there are multiple shifts.

Owing to the preponderance of inadequately trained teachers already in the system
and the continuing need to update teacher skills, in-service teacher training would be the main
focus of the component's activities. The component would provide financial and technical
assistance to the in-service training system for a wide range of improvements that are consistent
with the demands of curriculum development, teaching approaches, and examination practices.
The component would focus on developing cost-effective teaching methods and enhancing
management and instructional capabilities as a step towards enhancing instructional quality. In
particular, the component would help establish the capability within MOE to plan, design,
produce and use high quality teacher training materials.

Approximately 70 senior trainers, 3800 supervisors, and 120,000 teachers of math and

To address the needs of the large numbers of teachers who face difficulties (costs

delivery are expected to include study assignments, TV and/or videotape viewing, personal
contact sessions, and guidance by supervisors and head teachers. In addition to internal
evaluation of all activities, the distance education pilot training will be evaluated by NCERD
and/or other appropriate institutions other than those directly involved in the training (evaluation
to be completed by end of first quarter of year 5).

reform_description:
The concept of creating a specialized center in charge of curriculum and materials
development began in 1988, and was a part of the then Minister's overall education reform
program. The creation of the Center for Curriculm and Instructional Matrals
Design (CCIMD) was a major project of the plan for educational reform in Egypt.

reform_title: Creation of the National Center for Educational Evaluation and Examinations (NCEEE)
reform_description:
The National Center for Educational Evaluation and Examinations (NCEEE)
was a second new institution established as a means of realizing policy objectives to reform
education. The NCEEE was created by Presidential Decree in 1991 with the following mission:
(i) to establish the standards, norms and criteria for evaluating all levels of education; (ii) to
prepare examinations, including the Preparatory, Secondary and University Entrance
examinations; (iii) to regulate the quality of all examinations; (iv) to conduct research on
evaluation methods and instruments; (v) to train educators and administrators in the use of
examinations; and (vi) to provide consultations for universities and other institutions.

Government has identified four priorities for educational development In the Plan.
While continuing to expand access, the MOE is committed to improving the quality of education

Five-Year Plan

The Ministry of Education launched a reform program aimed at improving the quality, availability, and efficiency of the basic education system with support from USAID, ODA, UNICEF, and other donors. The program focused on expanding the basic education system to provide universal access, improving MOE's institutional information and planning capabilities, and developing new curricula and teaching methods.

reform_title: Primary Level Consolidation from Six to Five Grades
reform_description: The primary education level was restructured from six to five grades, resulting in a drop in primary school enrollments and an increase in secondary school enrollments due to the existence of a double cohort.

reform_title: Establishment of Three Semiautonomous Education Agencies
reform_description: Three new semiautonomous agencies were established by ministerial decrees to direct improvements in curriculum and evaluation: the Center for Curriculum and Instructional Materials Development (CCIMD), the National Center for Examinations and Educational Evaluation (NCEEE), and the National Center for Educational Research and Development (NCERD).

A new integrated subject called 'environmental studies' combining science, home economics, agriculture, and social studies was introduced into grades 1–3 nationwide as part of MOE's curriculum reform program.

reform_title: New Textbooks Introduction for Primary Grades
reform_description: New textbooks for mathematics, science, Arabic, religion, art, and physical education were field-tested and introduced gradually nationwide in grades 1–5 for the 1992–93 and 1993–94 school years as part of the ongoing curriculum reform.

The National Center for Examinations and Educational Evaluation (NCEEE), with ODA assistance, revised national achievement tests to include questions measuring reasoning and critical thinking abilities, and devised semiannual exams to improve validity and reduce reliance on end-of-year examinations.

reform_title: Elevation of Preservice Teacher Qualification Requirement
reform_description: MOE decided to elevate the preservice requirement for basic-level teaching to a university degree and to phase out the Teachers' Institutes by 1993. Many phased-out institutes were transformed into specialized university-level colleges of education.

reform_title: Shortening of Basic Education Stage from Nine to Eight Years
reform_description: The basic education stage was shortened from nine to eight years, relieving some enrollment pressure on schools by reducing class sizes at the primary stage.

reform_title: Establishment of Education Support Fund and School Maintenance Fee System
reform_description: The government institutionalized school maintenance financing by establishing the Education Support Fund (ESF) and introducing annual per-student maintenance fees collected from primary, preparatory, and secondary students to finance school upkeep.

reform_title: Establishment of Higher Commission for Training
reform_description: A Higher Commission for Training was established by Ministerial Decree No. 219 of September 24, to plan and develop integrated and coordinated in-service teacher training programs, particularly with respect to curriculum and testing developments.

An IDA-financed project implemented by the Egyptian Ministry of Education comprising four components: (i) school construction and maintenance of approximately 130 primary and 110 preparatory schools in seven governorates; (ii) in-service teacher training including upgrading of regional training centers and development of distance education; (iii) institutional development of MOE's management, planning, and education management information system (EMIS); and (iv) related policy-oriented studies on teacher preparation and education financing.
A national education law that established a unified and compulsory nine-year basic education cycle, integrating the previously separate primary and preparatory education stages.

reform_title: Amendment to the Basic Education Law (Law No. 233)
reform_description: Legislation that amended the basic education structure by reducing the compulsory basic education cycle from nine to eight years to address system capacity constraints.

reform_title: Establishment of the General Authority for Educational Buildings (GAEB)
reform_description: Creation of a centralized national authority responsible for the planning, design, construction, and maintenance of educational facilities and school infrastructure nationwide.

reform_title: Establishment of the Center for Curriculum and Instructional Materials Development (CCIMD)
reform_description: Creation of a dedicated national institution tasked with developing, revising, and evaluating curricula and instructional materials for the basic education system.

A World Bank-financed national project implemented by the government to improve access, equity, and quality of basic education through school construction, expansion of in-service teacher training, distance education programs, and institutional capacity building.

Donor-supported government basic education program that constructed schools in underserved rural areas, supplied instructional materials and equipment to basic education schools, established an education data base and trained education information staff, and modernized parts of the curriculum.

Ministry of Education reform program to improve the quality, availability, and efficiency of basic education, expand access toward universal basic education, strengthen information and planning capacity, and develop new curricula and teaching methods.

reform_title: Consolidation of Primary Education from Six to Five Grades
reform_description: Structural reform consolidating the primary level from six grades to five grades, shortening the basic education stage and changing enrollment flows across primary and secondary levels.

reform_title: University Degree Requirement for Basic-Level Teachers
reform_description: Teacher preparation reform elevating the preservice requirement for basic-level teaching to a university degree, phasing out Teachers' Institutes by 1993, and transforming many institutes into specialized colleges of education.

reform_title: Creation of Semi-Autonomous Curriculum, Examination, and Research Agencies
reform_description: Ministerial decrees established three semi-autonomous agencies to guide curriculum development, examinations and evaluation, and educational research and development.
Introduction of a new national environmental studies subject in grades 1-3, combining science, home economics, agriculture, and social studies to connect learning with children's lives.

Development, field-testing, and phased national introduction of new primary textbooks and instructional materials in mathematics, science, Arabic, religion, art, and physical education, with population, environmental, and tourism topics integrated into the curriculum.

reform_title: National Assessment and Semiannual Examination Reform
reform_description: Assessment reform revising national achievement tests to include reasoning and critical-thinking questions and devising semiannual examinations to improve test validity and reduce reliance on end-of-year and end-of-cycle examinations.
Institutional reform assigning the General Authority for Educational Buildings responsibility for school maintenance.

reform_title: Education Support Fund and School Maintenance Fees
reform_description: Financing reform institutionalizing school maintenance through creation of the Education Support Fund and annual maintenance fees for primary, preparatory, and secondary students, with revenues directed to GAEB and local councils for maintenance.

Ministerial Decree No. 219 created a Higher Commission for Training to coordinate in-service teacher training with curriculum, testing, and research centers and senior Ministry of Education officials.

reform_title: Basic Education Improvement Project: School Construction and Maintenance
reform_description: IDA-financed government project component to construct and equip primary and preparatory schools in seven governorates, target underserved girls and poor children, establish school maintenance arrangements, train maintenance staff, and strengthen school construction and maintenance planning capacity.

reform_title: Basic Education Improvement Project: In-Service Teacher Training System Strengthening
reform_description: IDA-financed government project component to establish GDIST units for training module design, production, evaluation, and distance education; create or upgrade regional training centers; produce prototype training modules; and train senior trainers, trainers, and teachers in science, mathematics, and English.

reform_title: Basic Education Improvement Project: Institutional Development and EMIS
reform_description: IDA-financed government project component to strengthen Ministry of Education management, planning, and policy analysis through staff training, creation and maintenance of a Technical Committee for Institutional Development, development of EMIS capacity, and expansion of education data systems at central and governorate levels.

An educational policy began to be defined with the Second Five-Year Plan (1962-67). In addition to quantitative growth, this Plan stressed improvements in quality and the relationship of the system to national needs particularly at secondary and university levels.

Two education projects already assisted by IDA in Ethiopia; these projects, financed in 1966 and 1971 respectively, are helping secondary, technical, agricultural, and teacher education and assisting a broadening of the curricula at various levels. The first project has been successfully completed.

Ethiopia has received two IDA credits for education. The first project, financed in 1966 and now successfully completed, assisted the expansion of secondary, technical and teacher education and the diversification of the curricula at various levels.

reform_title: Start of systemic planning at the university
reform_description: Improvements in the quality and re-orientation of the content of education were also only partially successful. At the university, systematic planning started only in 1969.

reform_title: New Institute for the training of junior secondary school teachers
reform_description: A new Institute for the training of junior secondary school teachers was established in 1969 in provisional facilities in the Addis Ababa Technical School

reform_title: New training college for secondary teachers of French (Ecole Normale Superieure)
reform_description: A training college for secondary teachers of French (Ecole Normale Superieure) was established in Addis Ababa in 1969-70

Two education projects already assisted by IDA in Ethiopia; these projects, financed in 1966 and 1971 respectively, are helping secondary, technical, agricultural, and teacher education and assisting a broadening of the curricula at various levels. Implementation of the second project is proceeding satisfactorily.

Ethiopia has received two IDA credits for education. The second project, financed in 1971, is assisting teacher and agricultural education and a limited growth of secondary education to improve its quality and geographical distribution. The implementation of this project is proceeding satisfactorily.

The Third Five-Year Plan (TFYP), which the government has recently revised and extended for a sixth year to 1973-74, aims at strengthening national unity, broadening the monetization of the economy, expanding employment and reaching and sustaining a 5.5% annual growth in GDP. The Third Plan gives priority to agriculture and to infrastructure which directly supports agriculture, such as feeder roads, research and training.

reform_title: Modification to the syllabi of the individual science subjects
reform_description: The syllabi of the individual science subjects have been modified for relevance to Ethiopian conditions and include now an adequate emphasis on environmental studies.

reform_title: Reorganization of the system of production and distribution of textbooks and visual aids
reform_description: The Government is, therefore, reorganizing its system of production and distribution of textbooks and visual aids with a view to establishing a business-oriented semi-autonomous Educational Materials Production Center, operating under the Commercial Code.

reform_title: Development of a mechanism for continuing improvements in the curriculum and in the individual courses
reform_description: A mechanism for continuing improvements in the curriculum and in the individual courses has been developed; since development of a sound curriculum is essential, the finalized curriculum would be sent to the Association for review and comment within one year of signing of the Credit Agreement.

reform_title: Introduction of Amharic as the medium of instruction
reform_description: Improvements in the quality and re-orientation of the content of education were also only partially successful. At the primary level no substantial changes occurred except for the introduction of Amharic as the medium of instruction.

Improvements in the quality and re-orientation of the content of education were also only partially successful.  At the secondary level, the first IDA education project had a major impact in introducing a diversified curriculum including pre-vocational streams in agriculture, industrial art, commerce and home economics.

reform_title: Agreement to add or replace classrooms
reform_description: The government has an agreement with the Swedish International Development Authority (SIDA) to add or replace about 5,000 classrooms by June 1974

A diversified curriculum including industrial arts, commerce, home economics and agriculture was introduced in 54 new and 23 existing secondary schools with the first IDA education project. The use of the new curriculum has just started.

reform_title: First IDA Education Project – Secondary and Technical Education Expansion
reform_description: IDA-financed project that assisted the expansion of secondary, technical, and teacher education and introduced a diversified curriculum at various levels including pre-vocational streams in agriculture, industrial arts, commerce, and home economics in 54 new and 23 existing secondary schools.

reform_title: Second IDA Education Project – Teacher and Agricultural Education
reform_description: IDA-financed project assisting teacher and agricultural education including construction of new Teacher Training Institutes, a new Teacher Training College at Nazareth, expansion of Jimma Agricultural Institute, an Academy of Pedagogy, and limited growth of secondary education to improve quality and geographical distribution.

reform_title: Education Sector Review and Long-Term Educational Development Strategy
reform_description: The Ethiopian government conducted a comprehensive sector review organized by 14 task forces and adopted a long-term educational development strategy. The Council of Ministers approved major policy decisions including prioritizing primary and adult education, restructuring the school system into Minimum Formation Education (4 years), Middle School (4 years), and Senior Secondary (4 years), introducing work-oriented non-formal education, using radio to extend education nationwide, decentralizing education administration, restructuring the Ministry of Education, and introducing tuition fees with a student loan scheme at post-secondary level.

Agricultural Training, and Educational Materials, IDA-financed interim investment project providing renovation and expansion of the Science Center of Haile Selassie I University, expansion of the Science Curriculum Development Center, establishment of an Educational Materials Production Center, construction of three new Agricultural Training Centers, expansion of the Animal Health Institute, and a Training Center for the Ethiopian Orthodox Church Development Commission, along with technical assistance for implementing educational reforms.

reform_title: Establishment of the Educational Materials Production Center
reform_description: The government reorganized its system for production and distribution of textbooks and visual aids by establishing a semi-autonomous Educational Materials Production Center operating under the Commercial Code with a publishing capacity of 3.5 million copies per year to serve primary and secondary schools nationwide.

reform_title: Establishment of the National Council for Teacher Education
reform_description: The government established the National Council for Teacher Education (NCTE) to coordinate all teacher training activities in the country, establish better links between the Ministry of Education and teacher training agencies, and assist in designing pre-service and in-service teacher training schemes.

reform_title: Establishment of the National Vocational Training Center
reform_description: A National Vocational Training Center was established under the Ministry of National Community Development and Social Affairs with UNDP assistance and ILO as executing agency to provide accelerated courses for skilled workers.

reform_title: Junior Secondary Teacher Training Institute Establishment
reform_description: A new institute for the training of junior secondary school teachers was established in 1969 in provisional facilities in the Addis Ababa Technical School offering a two-year program following grade 12.

reform_title: Ecole Normale Superieure – Secondary Teacher Training College for French
reform_description: A training college for secondary teachers of French was established in Addis Ababa in 1969-70 under the administrative responsibility of the Ministry of Education and almost entirely financed by foreign assistance.

reform_title: Primary School Building Program with SIDA
reform_description: The government entered into an agreement with the Swedish International Development Authority to add or replace approximately 5,000 classrooms by June 1973 through an Elementary School Building Unit implementing the scheme with SIDA providing technical assistance and 50% of the cost.
The first IDA education project introduced a diversified curriculum including industrial arts, commerce, home economics, and agriculture in 54 new and 23 existing secondary schools across Ethiopia.

reform_title: Education Tax on Urban Land and Personal Income
reform_description: The government introduced an education tax on urban land and personal income to generate additional revenue for financing education, yielding approximately Eth$5 million per year.

A donor-financed education project that expanded secondary, technical, and teacher education and introduced a diversified curriculum, including pre-vocational streams in agriculture, industrial arts, commerce, and home economics, into secondary schools.

reform_title: Establishment of Institute for Junior Secondary Teacher Training
reform_description: Creation of a new institute in Addis Ababa offering a two-year program following grade 12 to train junior secondary school teachers, replacing a phased-out university program.
Establishment of a training college in Addis Ababa under the administrative responsibility of the Ministry of Education to train secondary school teachers of French.

A donor-financed project assisting teacher and agricultural education, supporting the expansion and geographic distribution of secondary schools, and establishing new primary and junior secondary teacher training facilities.
A system-wide reduction of the secondary school timetable from 40 periods of 40 minutes each to 30 periods of 45 minutes each per week to improve cost distribution and reduce teacher costs.

reform_title: Establishment of the National Vocational Training Center
reform_description: Establishment of a national center under the Ministry of National Community Development and Social Affairs, assisted by UNDP and ILO, to provide accelerated courses for skilled workers.

Major policy decisions formally adopted by the Council of Ministers to restructure the school system into a 4-4-4 cycle, shift to practical and work-oriented education, extend the school year to 210 days, introduce post-secondary tuition fees with a student loan scheme, and decentralize educational administration.

reform_title: Education Tax on Urban Land and Personal Income
reform_description: Introduction of a new education tax on urban land and personal income to generate national revenue for financing education.

A government scheme implemented by the newly established Elementary School Building Unit (ESBU) and supported by the Swedish International Development Authority to systematically add or replace 5,000 primary school classrooms.

reform_title: Introduction of Amharic as Medium of Instruction
reform_description: A systemic instructional reform introducing Amharic as the primary medium of instruction across the primary education system.
IDA-financed education project supporting expansion of secondary, technical, and teacher education and diversification of curricula at various levels.
IDA-financed education project supporting teacher and agricultural education, limited secondary education growth, and improvements in quality and geographic distribution.
Government acceptance of the major recommendations of the Education Sector Review to develop a new long-term education strategy aligned with national development needs and financial constraints.

reform_title: Priority to Primary Schooling and Adult Education
reform_description: Council of Ministers policy decision giving priority to primary schooling and adult education while linking secondary and tertiary growth to manpower requirements.
Council of Ministers policy decision to make education practical and work-oriented, with particular emphasis on rural skills.

reform_title: Rural and Non-Government School Subsidy Policy
reform_description: Council of Ministers policy decision to prioritize rural and less developed areas, encourage non-government schools where government schools do not exist, and allocate 2-3 percent of the recurrent education budget for subsidies.
Council of Ministers policy decision to use radio service to extend education nationwide.

Council of Ministers policy decision to restructure schooling into four years of Minimum Formation Education, four years of Middle School Education, and four years of Senior Secondary Education, with a two-year course for youth aged 12-16, work-oriented non-formal education, shift use, and an extended school year.
Council of Ministers policy decision to use a mixture of teachers with high and lower formal qualifications and expand upgrading courses.

reform_title: Post-Secondary Tuition Fees and Student Loan Scheme
reform_description: Council of Ministers policy decision to introduce tuition fees at the post-secondary level accompanied by a student loan scheme.

reform_title: Education Administration Decentralization and Ministry Restructuring
reform_description: Council of Ministers policy decision to decentralize education administration, restructure the Ministry of Education head office, and establish inter-ministerial commissions.

reform_title: Third IDA Education Project Interim Investment Program
reform_description: IDA-financed interim investment program to improve science teaching and educational materials, expand trained agricultural manpower, and use the Ethiopian Orthodox Church rural education network more effectively.

reform_title: Haile Selassie I University Science Center Expansion
reform_description: Project-supported renovation and expansion of the Science Center of Haile Selassie I University to expand science instruction, laboratories, and science teacher preparation capacity.

Project-supported replacement and expansion of the Ministry of Education Science Curriculum Development Center to strengthen science curriculum development, testing, examination evaluation, handbooks, teacher guides, in-service training, and science equipment prototype development.
Government reorganization of textbook and visual-aid production and distribution through establishment of a business-oriented semi-autonomous Educational Materials Production Center.
Project-supported creation of three agricultural training centers to provide practical field-assistant training and short courses for farmers in Gojjam, Kaffa, and Wollo.
Project-supported expansion of the Ministry of Agriculture Animal Health Institute at Debre Zeit to increase training capacity for animal health assistants.

reform_title: Ethiopian Orthodox Church Development Commission Training Center
reform_description: Project-supported establishment of a training center for Ethiopian Orthodox Church clergymen in applied science, agriculture, community development, hygiene, and arithmetic to mobilize the Church rural education network for development.

The ODECA-ROCAP books were developed

to help develop and expand pre-vocational education at the secondary level, to
provide qualified teachers in these areas, and to improve and expand the supply
of agricultural technicians.

schools with 8,000 student places, a secondary teacher training institution
with 700 student places and expansion of facilities at the Agricultural Tech-
nical Institute. The project also financed specialist services and fellow-
ships for secondary school curriculum development and for training teachers.

In 1975 the National Planning Council prepared a 1975-1979 Develop-

The Second Education Project (Loan 1212-GU, US$14.5 million) was
approved in March 1976 and is scheduled to be completed on June 30, 1983.
The purpose of the project is to continue expanding and improving the
secondary education program financed by the first education loan. Sixteen
out of 21 secondary schools have been constructed and the remaining 6 are
about 80% completed.

reform_title: Appointing of an ad-hoc Basic Textbook Commission
reform_description:
Textbook manuscripts have been developed by an ad-hoc Basic
Textbook Commission appointed in November 1982.

Three basic education projects are expected to receive external
financing: (a) a rural primary education project was submitted to IDB and
was signed in March 1983. It aims at increasing school enrollment in rural
areas by providing some 25,000 new student places;

The textbook program for 1983-1990 would
have two overlapping phases (Annex 3). The first phase (1983-1987) was
designed by the government to accomplish three goals: (a) to test in the
field the textbook manuscripts; (b) to pilot, on a small scale, the produc-
tion and distribution functions of CENALT*X; and (c) to provide textbooks and
workbooks to the most needy children of the Altiplano and northern regions of
the country. The second phase (1985-1990) would aim at national coverage
with provision of textbooks for all children in the six primary school
grades. The project would support the first four years of the program,
1983-1987, including the first phase and part of the second phase.
the government has recently increased significantly its investment in education

The proposed project is designed to assist the government in imple-
menting more fully the objectives supported by the first project, especially
through programs of teacher training and technical assistance, and to com-
plement government efforts in non-formal and primary education which are
underway with assistance from other bilateral and multilateral institutions.

In mid-1975 the govern-
ment completed a national development plan as well as specific plans for
agriculture and education. The main objectives of the National Plan are to
continue economic growth, to establish greater self-sufficiency in basic foods
and materials, and to distribute income more equitably. In agriculture the
main aims are to expand production and reserves of basic grains, to increase
exports, and to improve the life of the small farmer. In manufacturing the
aim is to increase industrial output and exports through labor intensive
industries, and in services the aim is to expand health, education and
tourism.

reform_title: New curriculum outline for lower secondary schools
reform_description:
The government's new curriculum outline for lower secondary schools
would provide 10 class periods a week (out of 32 class periods) of introduc-
tions to industrial, agricultural, commercial and home economics subjects
to be offered to both girls and boys. These courses would be designed to
assist students in choosing further on-the-job or non-formal training and to
direct continuing students towards more relevant further studies.

The project would provide for cons-
truction, furnishing, and equipping of two new experimental agricultural train-
ing schools, to be located in the priority western highlands and northern
regions of the country. Each school would have student places and full board-
ing facilities for 180 students, principally rural Indian youths; and the
school in the western highlands would also have 30 places for non-formal
training of farmers and others. The main training course in each school
would be 3 years in length and would be designed to prepare about 120 students
per year who would perform direct functions in agricultural development by
employment in government service, on farms, in farmer youth clubs, and in
the proposed non-formal education and training activities. New students
would have to be at least 13 years old and provide evidence of completion
of primary school or an equivalent reading and mathematical achievement.
Since graduates would perform direct functions in agricultural development,
principally in the rural disadvantaged areas, entrance requirements would
give due consideration of the linguistic and socio-economic background of
students necessary to meet the schools' objectives.

reform_title: Establishment of a joint council of the Ministries of Education and Agriculture
reform_description:
Agricultural Training Schools. The government has pro-
posed to administrate these schools through a joint council of the Ministries
of Education and Agriculture. This council would be established and its
director appointed within six months of the signing of the Loan Agreement.

The Bank also supported school construction as part of the
Reconstruction Project (Loan 1314-GU, US$4.2 million), which assisted the
government in its reconstruction efforts following the 1976 earthquake.
Thirteen schools with 4,320 students places were built.

Bilingual textbooks have been developed in four languages for the
first two primary school grades
A new curriculum was recently adopted by the government.

Reduced repetition and drop out
rates are necessary to improve efficiency of the sector. During the plan,
repetition rates are anticipated to fall from 17.2% to 12%.

A textbook program has been prepared to provide all school
children with adequate textbooks in the next eight years. Curriculum developments would run parallel to textbook provisions, training
of teachers and increased provision of teaching aids.

of teachers and increased provision of teaching aids.

reform_description:
Three basic education projects are expected to receive external
financing: (c) a program to expand and improve out-of-school education
programs was recently approved by USAID.

The project would support the first four years of the program,
1983-1987, including the first phase and part of the second phase.

The bilingual education materials have been developed for the MOE
under a separate project with USAID.

reform_title: Adaptation of the series developed by OPECA-ROCAP to the curriculum
reform_description:
Textbook manuscripts have been developed by an ad-hoc Basic
Textbook Commission appointed in November 1982. This group has adapted to
the Guatemalan curriculum the series developed during the 1960s by OPECA-
ROCAP.

reform_title: First Education Project – Secondary Schools and Teacher Training
reform_description: World Bank-financed project begun in 1968 providing US$6.3 million for construction, furnishing, and equipping of 14 new secondary schools, a secondary teacher training institution at San Carlos University (EFPEM), and expansion of facilities at the Agricultural Technical Institute (ITA) to develop and expand pre-vocational education at the secondary level and improve the supply of agricultural technicians.

reform_title: Establishment of Curriculum Department in Ministry of Education
reform_description: The government established a Department of Curriculum Development within the Ministry of Education to plan qualitative aspects of education including curriculum reform and teacher training and to supervise educational aspects of World Bank-supported projects.

reform_title: 1975-1979 Development Plan for Education Science and Culture
reform_description: The National Planning Council prepared a five-year education development plan proposing ambitious programs for expansion and quality improvement across primary, secondary, non-formal, and higher education levels with increased capital and recurrent expenditure targets.

The government introduced a new curriculum outline for lower secondary schools providing 10 class periods per week of introductions to industrial, agricultural, commercial, and home economics subjects for both girls and boys to better align secondary education with national development needs.

reform_title: Second Education Project – Secondary and Agricultural Education Expansion
reform_description: World Bank-financed project providing US$14.5 million to construct and equip 11 new lower secondary schools, 2 combined upper and lower secondary schools, 2 common facilities centers, and 2 experimental agricultural training schools; provide teacher and administrator training for approximately 700 staff; and deliver technical assistance and fellowships to implement a pre-vocational curriculum and improve education planning and administration.

A World Bank-financed project that constructed 14 new secondary schools, established a secondary teacher training institution at San Carlos University (EFPEM), and expanded facilities at the Agricultural Technical Institute (ITA) to develop pre-vocational secondary education.
A national education plan that significantly increases capital investment in education and outlines new programs for expanding and improving primary, secondary, and non-formal education.

reform_title: Establishment of the Department of Curriculum Development
reform_description: Creation of a Department of Curriculum Development within the Ministry of Education to plan qualitative aspects of education, including curriculum reform and teacher training.
Implementation of an improved curriculum for lower secondary schools to address prior deficiencies and develop pre-vocational education.
Establishment of an inter-ministerial committee to coordinate and oversee non-formal education programs.

A World Bank-assisted project to expand pre-vocational secondary education by constructing new secondary schools and common facilities centers, establishing experimental agricultural training schools, and providing extensive teacher and administrator training.

World Bank-financed government project to construct and equip 14 secondary schools, establish a secondary teacher training institution at San Carlos University, expand the Agricultural Technical Institute, develop pre-vocational secondary education, train teachers, and increase agricultural technician supply.

reform_title: 1975-1979 Development Plan for Education Science and Culture
reform_description: National education plan prepared by the National Planning Council to expand and improve education, including primary and non-formal education, and to increase recurrent and capital allocations for education.

Creation of a Ministry of Education department responsible for planning qualitative aspects of education, including curriculum reform and teacher training, and supervising educational aspects of the Bank project.
Establishment of an inter-ministerial national committee to coordinate and support non-formal education programs.
Introduction of a new lower secondary curriculum outline allocating class periods to industrial, agricultural, commercial, and home economics subjects for both girls and boys.

Creation of four new non-formal training programs corresponding roughly to grades 1-4, 5-9, 10-12, and higher education, including mixed-media education in agriculture, health, and community development.

reform_title: Second Education Project Secondary School Reform
reform_description: World Bank-supported project to expand and improve lower and upper secondary education through new schools, extensions, equipment, and implementation of pre-vocational curricula outside Guatemala City and in selected existing schools.

Creation of two shared secondary education facilities in Guatemala City to provide science laboratories, practical-subject workshops, and non-formal training facilities for nearby schools and communities.

Creation of two experimental agricultural training schools in the western highlands and northern regions to train lower middle-level agricultural technicians, principally rural Indian youths, through a new three-year course and short farmer-training courses.

reform_title: Project Teacher and Administrator Training Program
reform_description: Introduction of in-service and pre-service training for about 600 practical-subject teachers and about 100 school directors, supervisors, and non-formal education coordinators to staff project schools and build permanent teacher-training capacity.

reform_title: Establishment of the National Pedagogical Institute (IPN)
reform_description: The National Pedagogical Institute (IPN) has been established with French assistance.

reform_title: Establishment of the National Pedagogical Institute (IPN)
reform_description: Creation of the Institut Pédagogique National with French assistance to develop primary education curricula prepare learning and teaching aids including textbooks and literacy materials develop appropriate education technology and organize retraining and upgrading courses for teachers education administrators supervisors and school directors.

reform_title: First Education Project – Rural Basic Schools and Community Learning Centers
reform_description: Construction of 65 new rural basic schools serving Grades 1-4 in a restructured four-year course providing basic education to approximately 17400 school-age children and literacy and training opportunities to 3000-4000 out-of-school youths and adults. Centers also serve as community learning centers for ONAAC functional literacy and other community development activities.

reform_title: First Education Project – Urban Primary Schools and Community Learning Centers
reform_description: Construction of 10 new urban primary schools serving Grades 1-6 providing basic education to approximately 5400 school-age children and literacy and training opportunities to 1500-2500 out-of-school youths and adults. Centers also function as community learning centers.

reform_title: First Education Project – Primary Teacher Training College Expansion and Construction
reform_description: Expansion of one existing primary teacher training college in Port-au-Prince and construction of two new colleges in Gonaives and Cap Haitien replacing small obsolete colleges. Enrollment capacity expanded from 400 to 720 students with a revised curriculum training teachers in the dual role of teacher and community development agent.

reform_title: First Education Project – ONAAC Literacy and Community Program Equipment
reform_description: Provision of equipment valued at US$0.4 million to central and regional offices of the National Literacy and Community Development Office (ONAAC) to improve supervision and counseling services to village literacy centers and enhance functional literacy programs.

reform_title: Rural Primary School Restructuring – Four-Year Satellite School Network
reform_description: Government adoption of a proposal by the Department of Agriculture to establish a network of small neighborhood four-year basic schools (Grades 1-4) to serve as satellite schools to six-grade centralized schools replacing the ineffective seven-year rural primary structure.

reform_title: Reduction of Rural Teacher Pre-Service Training Course
reform_description: Government decision to reduce the rural teacher pre-service training course from three years to two years to accelerate teacher output and effect economies while maintaining training quality through adjusted program content.

An IDA-financed project introducing systemic changes, including the establishment of community learning centers, the restructuring of the rural basic education course to four years, and the reform of primary teacher training with a revised curriculum preparing teachers for a dual role as educators and community development agents.

reform_title: First Education Project community learning centers
reform_description: IDA-supported creation of 65 rural and 10 urban basic school/community learning centers to expand access to restructured basic education, functional literacy, community development activities, relevant curricula, and learning-teaching aids.

reform_title: Primary teacher training college reform and expansion
reform_description: Expansion of one primary teacher training college and construction of two new colleges, replacing or consolidating small obsolete colleges and introducing a revised curriculum to train teachers as both classroom teachers and community development agents.

reform_title: Strengthening of ONAAC literacy and community programs
reform_description: Provision of equipment and technical support to ONAAC central, regional, and literacy centers to improve supervision, counseling, learning conditions, literacy programs, and supervisor and instructor training.

Establishment of the National Pedagogical Institute with French assistance to develop primary curricula, teaching-learning aids, textbooks, literacy materials, education technology, teacher retraining, and training for administrators, supervisors, and school directors.

reform_title: Franco-Haitian education technical assistance and fellowship program
reform_description: Government agreement with France to provide short-term education specialists and 60 annual fellowships for rural and urban education personnel, including teacher trainers, school directors, supervisors, and administrators.

The 1990 Amendment of the 1985 Act on Public Education put an end to the state monopoly of education, and made it possible for individuals and legal entities to take part in the provision of educational services.

reform_title: Modifications to the general secondary school
reform_description: In past decades, the general secondary school (gymnasium) offered a four-year  course. After the modifications approved in 1989, there are three main forms of this  type of school covering different age groups. The eight-year general secondary school  caters for pupils between 12 and 18 years of age; the six-year gymnasium covers the  age group 12-18, while the four-year gymnasium is for pupils aged 14-18.
Other important laws must be mentioned, such as: the Law on Self Government (1990), which conferred wide-ranging autonomy to local authorities and  transferred to them the ownership of schools

The principles to fund Hungarian public education follow from the 1990 Local  Government Act. According to the provisions of the Act, primary and secondary  education is provided by local governments, but the overall responsibility remains  with the State. Financial guarantees are given jointly by the state and the local  governments.

reform_title: Emergence of the special apprenticeship school
reform_description: In 1990 a new type of school emerged, the special apprenticeship school, which is a unique combination of special schools and vocational-technical courses.

reform_title: The 1990 Amendment of the 1985 Act on Public Education

As it is stated in the Preamble, in 1993 the Parliament enacted the Public Education Act: “in order to ensure exercise of the right to education based on equality of opportunity, a right laid down in the Constitution of the Republic of Hungary; in order to achieve freedom of conscience, freedom of religion and the nurturing of patriotism in public education; in order to make good the right of national and ethnic minorities to education in their mother tongue; in order to realize freedom of education and freedom of teaching; in order to define the rights and duties of children, students, parents and those of employed in public education; and also in order to direct and operate a public education system that ensures up-to-date knowledge.

The Education Act No. 79 of 1993 (amended in 1995, 1996, 1999, 2002 and  2003) established a uniform framework for the education system. Asserting the  principle of the freedom of education, it has eliminated the State monopoly on schools  and has attempted to dissolve the former rigid structure of the school system in a  regulated way. The Act guarantees the right of every natural and legal person to  establish and maintain schools. However, the burden and tasks of maintenance are  primarily the responsibility of local governments, and their limited material resources  are compensated by the state budget. This subsidy extends to churches and other  entities maintaining schools and thus sharing the tasks of the state.
Recent changes have made it imperative to amend the 1993 Education Act in order to establish a predictable, stable and lasting system of public education, capable  of self-correction and renewal.

Education Development Strategy. The overall education strategy of Ireland is to promote greater

The Compulsory Education Law (1949). According to this law, and to the
changes introduced by the reform of the school system in 1968, compulsory education
applies to all children between the ages of 5 (compulsory kindergarten) and 15 (grade
10) inclusive. This education is provided free of charge. In addition, the law provides
for free education for adolescents aged 16-17, as well as for 18-year-olds who did not
complete their schooling in Grade XI in accordance with the curriculum. The state is
responsible for provision of free primary education under this law. Maintenance of
official educational institutions, however, is the joint responsibility of the State and
the local education authority. Parents have the right to choose one of the recognized
educational trends for their children. Important additions to the original version of this
law include a prohibition against discrimination on the grounds of ethnic origin, a
prohibition against discrimination in acceptance, placement, and advancement of
pupils as well as a prohibition against punishing pupils for actions or omissions on the
part of their parents. According to recent amendments, the Law covers the age group
3–15 years, for a total of 13 years. The Law will be gradually implemented for ages 3
and 4 in communities where the Ministry will implement it.

The State Education Law (1953). This law provides for a six-day school
week and determines the content and procedures of State education. State education is
defined as education provided by the State on the basis of the curriculum approved by
the Minister of Education, without attachment to a party, communal body or other
non-government organization, and is under the supervision of the Minister of
Education. According to the law, State education is to be based on the values of
Israel’s culture, the achievements of science, love of the homeland, loyalty to the
State and people of Israel, remembrance of the Holocaust, practice in agricultural
work and handicrafts, and on building a society on the foundations of freedom,
equality, tolerance, mutual assistance and love of mankind. This law enables the
Minister of Education to approve, at the request of 75% of the parents, an additional
institutional curriculum comprising up to 25% of the existing curriculum, or an
additional curriculum specifically requested by the parents.

According to the changes introduced by the reform of the school system in 1968, compulsory education applies to all children between the ages of 5 (compulsory kindergarten) and 15 (grade
10) inclusive. This education is provided free of charge.

The School Inspection Law (1968). This law regulates the conditions under
which schools that are not part of the State education system may be opened, and
contains provisions for their operation.

The Special Education Law (1988). According to this law, the purpose of
special education is to advance and develop the abilities and potential of handicapped
children, to correct and improve their physical, mental, psychological and behavioural
performance, to convey knowledge, skills, and habits and to adapt them to behaviour
acceptable to society with the purpose of becoming part of it and being integrated in
the world of work. The law provides for special education for individuals between the ages of 3 and 21 whose capacity for adaptive behaviour is limited and who are in need
of such education, including physiotherapy, speech therapy, and occupational therapy,
as well as treatment in additional areas.

An important objective of the Ministry of Education has been to complete the
implementation of the Special Education Law (1988). To accomplish this, the
Ministry has prepared a master plan for its implementation, defined a new policy for
placing pupils in special education frameworks, increased allocated resources for
special education, accelerated the development of special education facilities, and
emphasized the integration of pupils with special needs into regular classrooms.

Extension of
the school day is being carried out in stages. An experimental stage was implemented
in 1990, with the participation of a limited number of localities and neighbourhoods.

reform_title: Programme for State and State-religious kindergartens

reform_description:
Educational activities have been implemented according to three framework
programmes: (c) the programme for 2-year-olds
(published in 1996).

The Derekh Eretz Programme aims to encourage norms of positive behaviour,
with the goal of creating an attitude of mutual respect in schools and in society as a
whole. The programme is accompanied by an information campaign in the media to
encourage public awareness. Schools are a significant arena of social interaction, and
thus they must create an atmosphere that fosters fair play and promotes mutual
respect. Teachers are trained to establish a framework of clear rules and procedures,
based on proper relations among pupils, with themselves serving as role models for
exemplary personal behaviour. The approach is a comprehensive one, comprising all
the school’s cultural components, in integration with, and with support from, the
framework of general society. Schools are developing distinctive programmes for
improving modes of behaviour, in accordance with their needs. The programme’s
planning and implementation embraces teachers, parents, local officials, inspectors,
children and the community in order to create involvement, commitment to action and
positive change. The programme, which was implemented in all schools in 1996, is
carried out by civics teachers with the help of all the elements in the school and the
community. An instructional booklet for teachers has been published on the subject.

reform_title: The Long School Day and Enrichment Studies Law (1997)
reform_description:
The Long School Day and Enrichment Studies Law (1997). The purpose of
this law is to add study and instructional hours to the existing hours in educational
institutions. The law states that, in all educational institutions and in all sectors, from
compulsory kindergarten to Grade XII pupils shall remain in school for forty-one
hours per week. The long school day will be applied as follows: (a) four days a week:
eight study hours at least; (b) one weekday, except Friday: five study hours at most;
(c) Friday: four study hours at most. Gradual implementation of this law started in
1997/98, according to priorities set by the Ministry of Education.

reform_title: Programme for special education and integration of children with special needs into regular education

reform_description:

(mother tongue and foreign languages). It involves: increasing the number of
compulsory years of study of Arabic (or, in some cases, French) for Hebrew speakers
to Grade X; encouraging the study of the Russian language and other immigrant
languages also among native Hebrew speakers; and encouraging schools to specialize
in language studies. The programme began in 1997.

The Pupils’ Rights Law of 2000 establishes principles regarding student
rights in compliance with the UN Charter for Children’s Rights. According to this
law, a child has a right to education, to participate in matriculation examinations, a
right to confidentiality and to realize his or her rights.

reform_title: Basic curriculum of the pre-primary education system
reform_description:
Activities are now implemented according to the basic
curriculum of the pre-primary education system (in use since March 2000).

The main features of the new structure of secondary education (implemented starting
from Grade X during the 2000 school year) are the following:
• A comprehensive concept of secondary school, which presents an educational
continuum from Grade VII to Grade XII and enables schools to employ
flexible solutions over time.
• Increased school autonomy, by significantly expanding the freedom of choice
offered to schools and students regarding the subjects taught and learning
methods.
• Increased independent work, by developing independent learning skills,
motivation for learning and intellectual curiosity among students, while giving
them an opportunity for more extensive study in their own fields of interest.
• Clusters and interdisciplinary subjects, by encouraging secondary schools to
reorganize their studies and schedules.
• Alternative means of evaluation, in different fields such as critical thinking,
creativity, originality, ethical consideration and taking a stand with reference
to others and the society.
• Teamwork by the school teaching staff, within frameworks that combine
different fields of knowledge and when applying alternative methods of
evaluation.

reform_title: Programme promoting children and youth from weaker populations

reform_description:
A number of key and innovative programmes have begun to operate on a wide

reform_title: Amendments to the Compulsory Education Law
reform_description:
The Compulsory Education Law (1949). According to recent amendments, the Law covers the age group
3–15 years, for a total of 13 years.

reform_title: Changes to organization of studies and the class schedule
reform_description:
In recent years, the Ministry of Education, Culture and Sport has undertaken
investigations into the organization of studies and the class schedule in pre-primary,
primary and secondary education (lower and upper). The discussions concerned
various aspects of the relationship between compulsory and elective elements in
studies, the single discipline approach as opposed to the inter-disciplinary approach,
the number of subjects studied in the course of a school year, the school’s autonomy
in the organization of studies, etc. The outlined programme is already operating in
pre-primary education (kindergartens). At the secondary education level (lower and
upper), directives concerning the organization of studies and the class schedule have
been officially published; the implementation of this reorganization began in Grade X
in the 2000 school year.

reform_title: Introduction of the National Assessment Test (NAT)
reform_description:
In addition to school-based assessment programmes, i.e. exams administered
within and by schools, the National Assessment Test (NAT) has been introduced in
primary and lower secondary schools in order to provide information on the extent to
which educational goals set by the Ministry are being attained. The test is given only
to a representative sample of the pupil population. The exam is administered at
different grade levels in accordance with the need for feedback expressed by the
national co-ordinators for each subject. A major change introduced in the NAT is the
attempt at performance assessment. Open-ended questions and items that require
higher-order thinking are used in a greater proportion than ever before.

reform_title: Addition of school hours to the school day
reform_description:
Study hours have recently been added to the school day in order to improve
the level of learning achievement in the education system (especially in scientific and
technological studies), to enrich cultural and social activities (art, communication,
physical education, and social education) and to broaden Jewish studies.

In recent years special programmes have been implemented, among which the
promotion of education in the Arab and Druze sectors. The objective of this plan, which has been updated over the years, is to bring the educational institutions in these
sectors to a position where they can operate properly, by bringing them up to
educational and budgetary standards equal to those of the Jewish education sector.
Programmes were prepared which reflected the various activities of the education
system: the construction of classrooms; adding classroom hours; teacher training and
in-service teacher training; pedagogic assistance for teachers, teacher-trainers and
pedagogic centres; developing curricula and textbooks, and producing programmes
for educational television; promoting science and technology education; nurturing
gifted pupils; expanding the truant officers scheme and the psychological counselling
and guidance services; expanding activities to prevent dropping out of school;
significant expansion of special education; expanding informal education; and
increasing budgets for culture and sports.

reform_title: Development of teaching of civics and education for democracy, tolerance and peace
reform_description:
The teaching of civics and education for democracy, tolerance and peace has been
developed. The objective is to introduce a comprehensive reform in the teaching of
civics in schools and create a uniform civics curriculum for all sectors–state schools,
state-religious schools, Hebrew education and Arab education. Teaching civics
includes theoretical studies as well as inculcation of civic values, the most important
of which are the commitment to a democratic way of life, and learning skills and
abilities necessary for active citizenship. In addition to civics being studied as a
subject in the classroom, civics education will be incorporated into every aspect of the
curriculum and school life. The amount of time allotted to civics education and study
in the schools in all sectors of the education system will be increased significantly.
Obligatory study of civics will be expanded to all schools, in addition to lessons by
the classroom teacher, part of which must be devoted to topics related to civics.

the matriculation exams are undergoing a reform which is intended to reduce
the number of governmentally-conducted exams in favour of school-based
assessments. Three subjects are chosen randomly every year from among the pool of
compulsory national exams (with certain restrictions), and made voluntary. The
remaining exams are compulsory. In the subjects deemed voluntary, pupils can choose
whether to take the state exam or to receive the grade in that subject based on the
school’s assessment. If the pupil passes all the above exams, he/she is entitled to a
matriculation certificate.

reform_title: Introduction of the National Assessment Test (NAT)
reform_description:
The National Assessment Test (NAT) has been introduced in primary and
lower secondary schools in order to provide information on the extent to which
educational goals set by the Ministry are being attained. The test is given only to a
representative sample of the pupil population. Neither pupil nor school name is
disclosed, in order to avoid their becoming a focus of interest, and to reduce test
anxiety experienced by pupils, teachers, schools, and parents. The stated purpose of
the NAT is to provide information on a national scale regarding the extent to which
the mandatory curriculum is being taught and how well the pupils have understood,
retained, and absorbed it; to provide such information to the Ministry’s executives, to
the Knesset Education Committee, to school districts, the schools themselves, and to
the general public; and to focus public attention on the national curriculum. The NAT
is scheduled to be administered in a four-year cycle. Each year different subjects from
the curriculum are chosen. The subjects covered in the process of implementation are
Hebrew, mathematics, English, social sciences, and Biblical studies. The exam is
administered at different grade levels in accordance with the need for feedback
expressed by the national coordinators for each subject. For example, mathematics
and Hebrew language are assessed at the end of the fourth and eighth grades, while
the sciences exam is administered at the beginning of the seventh grade (which is also
the beginning of lower secondary school). A major change introduced in the NAT is
the attempt at performance assessment. Open-ended questions and items that require
higher-order thinking are used in a greater proportion than ever before.

reform_title: Establishment of the Planning and Budgeting Committee (PBC)
reform_description:
The Council is empowered by law to advise the government on the
development and financing of higher education and scientific research. To this end, it
has established a permanent subcommittee, the Planning and Budgeting Committee
(PBC), which is based on the model of the former University Grants Committee in
United Kingdom.

reform_title: Development of the accreditation process of regional, technological, and teacher training colleges
reform_description:
The objectives for developing the accreditation process of regional, technological, and
teacher training colleges are to take up the bulk of the increase in applicants and to
guarantee the quality of the academic degree received at these colleges.

In order to help schools implement programmes for pupils with special needs
and to integrate those pupils as fully as possible into the regular school framework, a
programme was introduced in primary and lower secondary schools. In 1995 the
programme operated in 180 schools, and in 1996 the number of schools reached 280.
The objectives of the programme are: to reinforce teachers efforts as they cope with
various exceptional problems in their classrooms, and to provide them with the tools
to deal with these children on a professional basis; to inculcate in pupils an attitude of
tolerance towards the different and the exceptional in general, and towards fellow
pupils with difficulties in particular; and to reduce the number of pupils being referred
to a special education framework in cases where, with proper treatment, they can be
integrated into regular education. In order to implement this programme, educators are
trained to work with special needs pupils within the regular classroom.

The promotion of children and youth from weaker populations is one of the
intervention programmes implemented in recent years. It assists and nurtures two groups in particular, namely pupils with a chance (i.e. pupils completing twelve years
of study without a matriculation certificate, including those who lack some of their
exams but who have a chance of completing the matriculation certificate), and pupils
at risk (i.e. pupils in the process of dropping out, dropouts, and new immigrant youth
at risk). Most of these activities are implemented in both the Hebrew and the Arab
education sectors. The ‘Pupils with a Chance’ programme is intended to locate pupils
with a chance of completing matriculation certificates and increase the proportion of
matriculation certificate holders.

Activities for children and youth at risk include: operating drop-out prevention
programmes in schools (for example, the Mena Programme,
operating in ninety schools)

reform_title: Truant officers working with drop-outs programmes
reform_description:
Activities for children and youth at risk include: truant officers working with drop-outs (in 1996, these
programmes reached approximately 11,000 drop-outs);

Activities for children and youth at risk include: operating after-school Children’s Home programmes providing a second
home for children and youth, who attend from the end of the school day until the
evening (in 1996, approximately 500 of these programmes were in operation, serving
about 8,000 children);

reform_title: Reintegration of drop-outs in the schools
reform_description:
Activities for children and youth at risk include: reintegrating drop-outs in the
schools;

reform_description:
Activities for children and youth at risk include: day-care centres under the responsibility of the school

the National Assessment Test (NAT), introduced in
