National law establishing free and compulsory education for children aged 5 to 15 (Grade 10) inclusive with subsequent amendments extending coverage to ages 3–15 over 13 years and prohibiting discrimination in acceptance placement and advancement of pupils.

National law defining the content and procedures of state education establishing a six-day school week placing education under the supervision of the Minister of Education and allowing parents to request supplementary institutional curricula of up to 25% of the existing curriculum.

National law establishing the Council for Higher Education as the independent statutory body responsible for accrediting and authorizing institutions of higher education to award degrees and advising the government on higher education development and financing.
National law regulating the conditions under which schools outside the state education system may be opened and operated.
Structural reform of the school system introducing pluralism within learning frameworks and programmes of study ending selection in secondary education and adopting an equal opportunity approach.

National law establishing the framework for special education for individuals aged 3 to 21 with limited adaptive behaviour capacity providing for development of abilities and integration into society including physiotherapy speech therapy and occupational therapy.

Reform of the national matriculation examinations reducing the number of governmentally-conducted exams in favour of school-based assessments allowing three compulsory subjects per year to become voluntary and transferring evaluation of six out of nine exams to schools.

reform_title: Promotion of Education in Arab and Druze Sectors
reform_description: Government plan to bring educational institutions in the Arab and Druze sectors to educational and budgetary standards equal to those of the Jewish education sector including construction of classrooms teacher training curriculum development science and technology promotion and expansion of special and informal education.

Comprehensive reform introducing a uniform civics curriculum across all school sectors including state state-religious Hebrew and Arab education expanding obligatory civics study and incorporating civic values and democratic education into all aspects of curriculum and school life.

reform_title: Establishment of General Colleges for Higher Education
reform_description: Government-approved expansion of the higher education system through establishment of new general colleges offering a broad spectrum of undergraduate degree programmes to meet growing demand with Bachelor's degrees equivalent to those awarded by universities.

Government programme introduced in primary and lower secondary schools to integrate pupils with special needs into regular classrooms by training educators providing diagnostic tools and developing differential curricula expanding from 180 schools in 1995 to 280 schools in 1996.

Introduction of a national assessment test in primary and lower secondary schools administered to a representative sample of pupils on a four-year cycle covering subjects including Hebrew mathematics English social sciences and Biblical studies with emphasis on performance assessment and higher-order thinking.

Government programme introducing national test item banks for school-based performance assessment encouraging schools to use constructive exams to facilitate changes in classroom instruction with items developed by expert teams.

National programme implemented in all schools to encourage norms of positive behaviour and mutual respect through teacher training school-based programmes and a public media information campaign carried out by civics teachers in collaboration with school and community stakeholders.

Ministry of Education decision introducing a mandatory four-year consecutive study format in all teacher training college programmes including a year of professional specialization as a half-time internship during the fourth year implemented in 1996 for secondary school teacher training.

reform_title: Long School Day and Enrichment Studies Law
reform_description: National law requiring all educational institutions from compulsory kindergarten to Grade XII to provide 41 hours of school per week with gradual implementation beginning in 1997/98 according to Ministry priorities.

National policy reform increasing compulsory years of Arabic or French study for Hebrew speakers to Grade X encouraging study of Russian and other immigrant languages among native Hebrew speakers and encouraging schools to specialize in language studies.

National law establishing principles regarding student rights in compliance with the UN Charter for Children's Rights including the right to education participation in matriculation examinations and confidentiality.

Introduction of a national basic curriculum for the pre-primary education system covering linguistic skills learning skills social-emotional skills physical-movement skills and cultural-social-civil educational contents implemented across all public kindergartens.

Implementation of a new structure for secondary education beginning in Grade X presenting a comprehensive educational continuum from Grade VII to XII with increased school autonomy independent learning clusters and interdisciplinary subjects and alternative means of evaluation.

Established free and compulsory education for children between the ages of 5 and 15, making the state responsible for primary education provision and introducing prohibitions against discrimination.[cite: 1]
Established the content, procedures, and centralized curriculum for State education, defining it as independent of non-government organizations and overseen by the Minister of Education.[cite: 1]
Created the Council for Higher Education as an independent statutory body responsible for accrediting institutions of higher education and authorizing them to award academic degrees.[cite: 1]

A structural reform that ended selection in secondary education, adopted an equal opportunity approach, and instituted pluralism within frameworks of learning to accommodate weaker populations.[cite: 1]
Established regulations, conditions, and provisions for the opening and operation of non-State recognized educational institutions.[cite: 1]

Mandated special education services, including therapeutic treatments, for individuals aged 3 to 21 with special needs to advance their abilities and integrate them into society and the workforce.[cite: 1]

A phased programme introduced in primary and lower secondary schools providing intensive training and didactic tools to help educators integrate pupils with special needs into regular classrooms.[cite: 1]

A comprehensive national programme implemented in all schools to foster positive behavior, mutual respect, and clear rules of conduct among students and teachers, supported by a media campaign.[cite: 1]

Introduction of standardized national assessments in primary and lower secondary schools to evaluate pupil achievement in subjects like Hebrew, mathematics, and English, shifting toward performance-based assessment.[cite: 1]

Introduction of a mandatory four-year consecutive study format for all teacher training college programmes preparing lower and upper secondary school teachers, including a year of professional specialization.[cite: 1]

reform_title: Long School Day and Enrichment Studies Law
reform_description: Increased instructional hours across all educational institutions from kindergarten to Grade XII, mandating forty-one hours of school per week.[cite: 1]

A systemic language policy that increased mandatory years of Arabic or French study for Hebrew speakers, promoted immigrant languages, and encouraged schools to specialize in language instruction.[cite: 1]

Enshrined student rights in the education system, guaranteeing the right to education, participation in matriculation exams, and confidentiality, aligning with international child rights standards.[cite: 1]
Implementation of a comprehensive basic curriculum for pre-primary education focused on linguistic, cognitive, social-emotional, physical, and cultural skills.[cite: 1]

A structural reorganization of secondary education providing an educational continuum from Grades VII to XII, increasing school autonomy, introducing interdisciplinary study clusters, and implementing alternative evaluation methods.[cite: 1]

National law establishing free and compulsory education, originally covering children aged 5 to 15 and later amended to expand coverage, prohibit discrimination in admission, placement, and advancement, and gradually extend compulsory education to ages 3 and 4.

National law defining state education, establishing a six-day school week, placing state education under Ministry supervision, requiring ministerially approved curricula, and allowing approved additional institutional curricula requested by parents.
National law establishing the Council for Higher Education as the licensing and accrediting authority for higher education institutions and authorizing it to accredit institutions and degrees.
National inspection law governing day care centers and pre-nursery play groups for children under age 3, placing them under state supervision.
Reform of the school system that changed compulsory education arrangements and ended selection in secondary education, supporting pluralistic learning frameworks and equal educational opportunity.
National law regulating the opening and operation of schools outside the state education system.

National law providing special education services for children and youth ages 3 to 21 with disabilities or limited adaptive behavior, including therapies and support for integration into society and work.

Government experimental extension of the school day in selected localities and neighborhoods as the initial stage of a broader policy to add study hours, improve achievement, enrich activities, and broaden Jewish studies.

reform_title: State and State-Religious Kindergarten Framework Programme
reform_description: National framework programme for state and state-religious kindergartens, structuring pre-primary educational activities before incorporation into the later basic curriculum.

reform_title: Special Needs Integration Programme in Primary and Lower Secondary Schools
reform_description: Government programme introduced in primary and lower secondary schools to train teachers, provide tools, reduce referrals to special education, and integrate pupils with special needs into regular classrooms where possible.
Voluntary school-based in-service training programme for education workers, aimed at improving professional knowledge, teaching methods, school staff development, and autonomous teaching capacity.
Published national pre-primary programme for two-year-old children, later incorporated into the basic curriculum of the pre-primary education system.

Introduction of a national assessment test in primary and lower secondary schools to monitor attainment of Ministry educational goals, curriculum coverage, and pupil understanding using representative samples and performance-oriented items.
Government-supported school-based assessment programme using national test-item banks and performance assessment tasks to support changes in classroom instruction and assessment practice.

Nationwide school programme implemented in all schools to promote mutual respect, positive behavior, school rules, teacher role modeling, and cooperation among schools, parents, local officials, inspectors, children, and communities.
Ministry decision introducing four consecutive years of study in all teacher training college programmes, implemented in 1996 for lower and upper secondary teacher training.
Addition of a compulsory professional specialization year in teacher training, conducted as a half-time position during the fourth year of study and launched experimentally in academic colleges.

reform_title: Special Education and Integration Framework Programme for Pre-Primary Education
reform_description: National pre-primary framework programme for special education and integration of children with special needs into regular education.

reform_title: Long School Day and Enrichment Studies Law
reform_description: National law adding study and instructional hours in educational institutions from compulsory kindergarten through Grade XII, requiring a forty-one-hour school week and phased implementation according to Ministry priorities.

Ministry language policy programme increasing compulsory Arabic or French study for Hebrew speakers through Grade X, encouraging Russian and other immigrant-language study, and encouraging schools to specialize in language studies.
National basic curriculum for pre-primary education, in use from March 2000, covering linguistic, learning, social-emotional, physical-movement, and cultural-social-civic foundations.

Reorganization of secondary education beginning in Grade X, introducing a comprehensive Grade VII–XII continuum, expanded school and student choice, independent learning, interdisciplinary clusters, alternative evaluation, and teacher teamwork.
National law establishing student rights in line with the UN Charter for Children's Rights, including rights to education, matriculation examinations, confidentiality, and realization of rights.

Assessment reform reducing the number of centrally conducted matriculation examinations, transferring evaluation in some subjects to schools, and allowing school-based assessment in selected compulsory subjects.

Comprehensive reform of civics education creating a uniform civics curriculum across state, state-religious, Hebrew, and Arab education, expanding obligatory civics study and integrating democratic citizenship across curriculum and school life.

reform_title: Arab and Druze Education Promotion Programme
reform_description: Sector-wide government programme to bring Arab and Druze educational institutions up to educational and budgetary standards equal to the Jewish sector through classrooms, instructional hours, teacher training, curricula, textbooks, science and technology education, special education, informal education, and support services.

reform_title: Higher Education College Expansion and Accreditation Reform
reform_description: Higher education reform expanding general, regional, technological, and teacher training colleges and developing accreditation processes so colleges could absorb growing demand and award bachelor's degrees equivalent to university degrees.

The Marshall Islands's 1979 Constitution gives people the right to education and charges the Cabinet with the responsibility for establishing and maintaining public schools and for making other provisions that may be reasonable and necessary to provide educational opportunities for the people of the Republic. Technology is not mentioned.

The 1991 Education Act does not mention the term information and communication technology (ICT) and educational technology (Edtech) nor other technology-specific terminology (e.g., distance education, open educational resources and assistive technologies in education). 
The 1991 Education Act aims to provide a thorough and efficient system of education to provide all children the educational opportunity that will prepare them to develop into self-reliant individuals and to function socially, politically and economically in society.  
Through skill-maintenance programs for students, the 1991 Education Act aims to achieve minimum standards of students’ proficiency and self-reliance. However, it does not mention the type of skills included in these programs.
Developing in-service training programmes for teachers is emphasised in the 1991 Education Act. However, it does not mention the content of in-service teachers' training programmes.

reform_title: The 2004 Individual with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
reform_description:
The 2004 Individual with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA): Part B of the U.S.A only mentions the term assistive technology. However, the act defines the term assistive technology device as any item, piece of equipment, or product system, whether acquired commercially on the shelf, modified, or customized, that is used to increase, maintain, or improve functional capabilities of a child with a disability. Furthermore, the act also defines the term assistive technology service as any service that directly assists a child with a disability in the selection, acquisition, or use of an assistive technology device.
The 2004 Individual with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA): Part B of the U.S.A aims to provide a framework for designing educational media services that are of educational value in the classroom setting to children with disabilities; and to promote the development, demonstration, and use of technology in those services.
Within the framework of the principles of the 2004 Individual with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA): Part B, the Special Education Program of the Public School System (PSS) of the Ministry of Education, Sports & Training aims to equip students with disabilities with technology and media skills.

Similarly, the 2010 Comprehensive Technology Plan mentions the term ICT; however, it does not define the term.
The 2010 Comprehensive Technology Plan recognises technology as an important tool for moving the Ministry of Education, Sports & Training forward. The plan targets students, teachers, school leaders (principals and head teachers), staff of the ministry and envisions them to become technology literate.
The 2010 Comprehensive Technology Plan has established the K-12 digital skills standards for students and the digital skills standards for teachers based on the National Educational Technology Standards for Students and Teachers, developed by the International Society for Technology in Education (ISTE). Furthermore, the plan has identified key competencies for school leaders, staff of the Ministry of Education, Sports & Training, and support personnel throughout the system.
The K-12 standards established within the 2010 Comprehensive Technology Plan calls for students to: demonstrate an understanding of technology concepts; effectively use computer-based technologies for word processing, analysing and displaying data; using spreadsheets, preparing presentations, and communicating using e-mail and other tools; use a variety of technologies to communicate and work collaboratively to support their learning and the learning of others, including at a distance; employ technology tools to gather, evaluate, and use information; use critical thinking skills to build knowledge and create products using technology; apply thinking skills to carry out research, manage information, solve problems, and make decisions using technology; understand and apply appropriate behaviour when using technology.
The teacher standards established within the 2010 Comprehensive Technology Plan call for teachers to: demonstrate an understanding of technology concepts and operations; effectively use computer-based technologies for word processing, analysing and displaying data, using spreadsheets, preparing presentations, and communicating using e-mail and other tools; use a variety of technologies to communicate and support their professional development thus strengthening their teaching practice and productivity; plan and design appropriate technology-enriched learning experiences for their students; effectively implement curriculum plans that include methods and strategies for using technology to enhance student learning; understand and apply appropriate behaviour when using technology and ensure that their students do the same.
The authority, as the primary communications and Internet service provider (ISP), played a key role in the implementation of the 2010 Comprehensive Technology Plan both through their continued infrastructure development and establishment of rates for access to their systems.
The 2010 Comprehensive Technology Plan established the Implementation Leadership Team (ILT) (comprising of the Secretary of Education, Assistant Secretaries, and member of the Fiscal Management Team of the Ministry of Education, Sports & Training) responsible for the overall implementation and monitoring of the plan.

reform_title: The 2013 Public School Education System Act
reform_description:
The 2013 Public School Education System Act does not mention the term information and communication technology (ICT) and educational technology (Edtech) nor other technology-specific terminology (e.g., distance education, open educational resources and assistive technologies in education). 
The 2013 Public School Education System Act aims to provide a thorough and efficient system of education to provide all children the educational opportunity that will prepare them to develop into self-reliant individuals and to function socially, politically and economically in society.  
Through skill-maintenance programs for students, the 2013 Public School Education System Act aims to achieve minimum standards of students’ proficiency and self-reliance. However, it does not mention the type of skills included in these programs. 
Developing in-service training programmes for teachers is emphasised in the 2013 Public School Education System Act. However, it does not mention the content of in-service teachers' training programmes.
The 2014 Child Protection Policy aims to protect children's data (of age less than 18 years) in educational settings, especially children with disabilities.

The Marshall Islands' 2014 Child Protection Policy aims to establish a framework that the Ministry of Education, Sports & Training uses to assess and manage the risk to the children participating in all programs, including any donor-funded programs, and the measures and systems put in place to respond to concerns about their wellbeing.
The Marshall Islands' 2014 Child Protection Policy aims to train all regular and volunteer teachers in child protection issues, including recognising and responding to signs of abuse through an induction program.
The 2014 Child Protection Policy aims to protect children's data (of age less than 18 years) in educational settings, especially children with disabilities.
The Marshall Islands' 2014 Child Protection Policy protects children (less than 18 years) in educational settings, especially children with disabilities, from online abuse and cyberbullying.

reform_title: The 2017-2020 Strategic Plan of the Public School System (PSS)
reform_description:
The 2017-2020 Strategic Plan of the Public School System (PSS) of the Ministry of Education, Sports & Training aims to educate and prepare all students to be independent, literate critical thinkers and problem-solvers, and be culturally and globally competent and responsive in order to reach their greatest potential through promoting the effective use of technology in education as one of the important goals. 
Providing internet access and/or an appropriate package of online computer-based learning resources to all schools by 2020 was one of the important targets of the 2017-2020 Strategic Plan of the Public School System (PSS) of the Ministry of Education, Sports & Training. According to the 2017-2020 Strategic Plan, internet connectivity has been achieved for all public schools on Majuro.  
The 2017-2020 Strategic Plan of the Public School System (PSS) of the Ministry of Education, Sports & Training aims to support Science, Technology, Engineering and Math (STEM) education using technology in education.
Furthermore, the 2017-2020 Strategic Plan of the Public School System (PSS) aims to hire full-time instructional technology specialists to support teachers in the use of instructional technology.

reform_title: The 2017-2020 Strategic Plan of the Public School System (PSS)
reform_description: The 2017-2020 Strategic Plan of the Public School System (PSS) of the Ministry of Education, Sports & Training aims to support Science, Technology, Engineering and Math (STEM) education using technology in education.

The 2018 Electricity Roadmap emphasises the need to increase the quality of teaching and students’ interest in STEM subjects at high school and elementary school in order to establish a highly skilled Marshallese energy workforce.

National education law establishing a thorough and efficient system of education to provide all children educational opportunities and prepare them to develop into self-reliant individuals; also emphasizes developing in-service training programmes for teachers.

A national ministry-level plan recognizing technology as an important tool for education advancement; established K-12 digital skills standards for students and digital skills standards for teachers based on ISTE standards; identified key competencies for school leaders and ministry staff; and established the Implementation Leadership Team (ILT) to oversee implementation and monitoring.

National legislation reaffirming the framework for a thorough and efficient public school education system aimed at preparing all children to function socially politically and economically in society; also emphasizes in-service teacher training.

A Ministry of Education Sports and Training policy establishing a framework to assess and manage risks to children in all educational programs; aims to protect children's data in educational settings and to train all teachers in child protection issues including recognizing and responding to signs of abuse.

reform_title: 2017-2020 Strategic Plan of the Public School System
reform_description: A multi-year strategic plan of the Ministry of Education Sports and Training aiming to educate and prepare all students as independent critical thinkers; promotes effective use of technology in education; targets internet connectivity for all public schools; supports STEM education; and plans to hire full-time instructional technology specialists to support teachers.

A government plan funded through the Global Partnership for Education to ensure continuous access to high-quality education during health emergencies; introduced paper-based and internet-based distance learning at primary and secondary levels; supported teacher training on distance learning and social-emotional learning; and provided communication materials to schools.

National education legislation aimed at providing an efficient education system to prepare self-reliant individuals[cite: 29]. It established skill-maintenance programs to achieve minimum standards of student proficiency [cite: 62] and emphasized the development of in-service training programs for teachers[cite: 88].

reform_title: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA): Part B
reform_description: Implementation of a framework to design educational media services and promote the use of technology for children with disabilities[cite: 30, 31]. It mandated the Special Education Program to equip students with disabilities with technology and media skills[cite: 64].

A systemic plan targeting technology literacy across the education system[cite: 33]. It established K-12 digital skills standards for students and teachers based on international frameworks, and created an Implementation Leadership Team for monitoring and implementation[cite: 41, 123].

Systemic education legislation aimed at developing self-reliant students capable of functioning in society[cite: 29]. It mandated skill-maintenance programs for student proficiency [cite: 62] and emphasized the ongoing development of in-service teacher training programs[cite: 88].

A framework established by the Ministry of Education, Sports & Training to assess and manage risks to children in all programs[cite: 34]. It mandated that all regular and volunteer teachers receive training in child protection issues [cite: 90], and aimed to protect children's data and shield them from online abuse and cyberbullying in educational settings[cite: 104, 108].

reform_title: 2017-2020 Strategic Plan of the Public School System
reform_description: A strategic initiative promoting the effective use of technology to develop globally competent students[cite: 39]. The plan aimed to provide internet access to all schools [cite: 50], support STEM education through technology [cite: 77], and hire full-time instructional technology specialists to support teachers[cite: 92].

An emergency education reform implementing paper-based and internet-based distance learning for continuous student access[cite: 40, 57, 58]. The plan supported teacher training on distance learning and safe school operations [cite: 93], and emphasized the acquisition of 21st-century and social-emotional skills for both students and teachers[cite: 72, 98].
The Constitution established the right to education and assigned the Cabinet responsibility for establishing and maintaining public schools and making provisions to provide educational opportunities.
The Education Act established a national legal framework for providing a thorough and efficient education system intended to prepare children for social, political, and economic participation.

reform_title: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act: Part B
reform_description: The act provided a framework for educational media services for children with disabilities and promoted the development, demonstration, and use of technology in those services.

The act established a public school education system aimed at providing all children with educational opportunities to become self-reliant and function socially, politically, and economically in society.

The policy established a Ministry of Education, Sports & Training framework to assess and manage risks to children participating in education programs, including donor-funded programs, and introduced measures on child protection training, children’s data protection, and protection from online abuse and cyberbullying in educational settings.

The strategic plan promoted effective use of technology in education, targeted internet access or offline computer-based learning resources for all schools, supported STEM education through technology, prioritized online learning training for teachers, and planned instructional technology specialists to support teachers.
The roadmap provided for solar and battery stand-alone power systems for schools without reticulated power, including installed systems in seven schools and planned extension to remaining schools.

The plan supported continuity of education during health emergencies through paper-based and internet-based distance learning, radio and TV messaging, parent and caregiver guidelines, teacher training in distance learning and education in emergencies, and communication materials for learning continuity.

reform_title: Lagos State Compulsory Free Universal Basic Education Law 2005
reform_description: Education service provision in the state is guided by Lagos State Compulsory Free Universal Basic Education Law 2005, which reiterates the state government’s policy of providing free, compulsory universal basic education for every child of primary and secondary school age. The law places an obligation on parents to ensure that their children attend and complete primary and junior secondary school. Failure of any parent to fulfill this duty amounts to a criminal offence punishable by fine or imprisonment. The Act created both the Local Government Primary and Junior Secondary Education Authority, which is in charge of implementation of education policy at the local government level, and the Universal Basic Education Board, which is in charge of implementation of education policy at the state level.

reform_title: The Guidelines for the Establishment of Private Schools of Lagos State (2011)

reform_title: Lagos State guidelines on the establishment and operation of private schools

reform_title: The Guidelines for the Establishment of Private Schools in Lagos State
reform_description: According to Section 10 of the Guidelines for the Establishment of Private Schools in Lagos State, “The School shall employ adequate number of teachers at all times to cater for pupils, and the ratio of teacher to pupils per class shall be 1:25 in the nursery, while it shall not be more than 1:35 for both primary and secondary levels.”

reform_title: Article 7 of the Guidelines for Public and Private Schools in Lagos State
reform_description:

reform_title: Article 10 of the Guidelines for Public and Private Schools in Lagos State
reform_description: Article 10 of the Guidelines for Public and Private Schools in Lagos State stipulates that school inspection procedures will not adhere to a specific calendar, but that “periodic inspection, monitoring and supervision” will be conducted by the State Ministry of Education (MoE) Quality Assurance (QA) Department, or the school inspectorate.

reform_title: The Guidelines for Public and Private Schools in Lagos State
reform_description: Part 3 of the Guidelines for Public and Private Schools in Lagos State describes the mechanism for grading schools in the state. The guidelines also indicate that strengths and weaknesses be included in the inspection follow-up, as shown in the following table of school grades.

reform_title: Article 19 of the Guidelines for Public and Private Schools in Lagos State
reform_description: Article 19 of the Guidelines for Public and Private Schools in Lagos State underlines that an inspection can result in the closure of school by the State Education Commissioner “if/when any of the existing guidelines and regulations is contravened”.

reform_title: Article 21 of the Guidelines for Public and Private Schools in Lagos State
reform_description: Article 21(d), (e), and (f) of the Guidelines for Public and Private Schools stipulate that school performance information must be available to parents on the website of the State Ministry of Education. Information may also be advertised by the school: “. . . included on their notice boards, letter headed-paper, and other external branding documents and artifacts.”

reform_title: Article 16 of the Guidelines for Public and Private Schools in Lagos State
reform_description: Article 16(e) of the Guidelines for Public and Private Schools in Lagos State states, “[T]he Honorable Commissioner of Education may give official, general, or specific directives on approved fees, as may be deemed fit.” Furthermore, point 16(c) adds that schools are not allowed to raise their fees during an academic year.

Article 5.2 of the aforementioned Guidelines outlines the requirements for creating a primary school: 
 A minimum land requirement of 18 meters by 36 meters. 
 A school Master Plan that clearly indicate the location of classrooms and other facilities, including offices. 
 A purpose-built structure that contains: a head teacher’s office, a staff room, a library, a minimum of six classrooms, toilets (one staff toilet and at least one toilet per classroom), and a regular water supply. 
 Open spaces on school premises for sports, recreational, and other approved purposes. 
 First-aid facilities and/or an adequately equipped sick bay affiliated with a government- approved hospital close to the school. 
 Standard classrooms with average floor space of 1.2 square meters per student. Pro-rated floor space may be applied where classes contain fewer or greater numbers of students. 
 Well- lit and ventilated classrooms, each with two doors and four windows. 
 Classroom furniture consisting of desks, benches or tables, and chairs adequate in size for the age of the pupils. 
 Suitable teaching aids, together with sports and games equipment. 
 Adequate fire-fighting and other emergency equipment in all buildings that are maintained at a functional level at all times. 
 Certification of Buildings and premises by the State Ministries of Physical Planning and Urban Development and of Education.

Article 5.3 of the same Guidelines outlines the requirements for creating a secondary school which include, but are not limited to: 
 A minimum land requirement of 18 meters by 36 meters. 
 Standard classrooms with average floor space of 1.2 square meters per student. A pro-rated floor space may be applied where classes contain fewer or greater numbers of students. 
 In schools that offer technical subjects, adequately equipped woodwork, metalwork, electronics, auto mechanics and/or building workshops with a floor area of 140 square meters, or an average of 4 square meters per student. 
 Where possible, specialist rooms of 70 square meters (or 2 square meters per student) for geography, technical drawing, fine art, business studies, computer studies, and home-related subjects (i.e., food, nutrition, home management, clothing/textiles). 
 A large hall for assemblies, school and public events, and examinations. 
 A regular water supply and at least eight water closet toilets. In mixed-sex schools, female-only toilets must be provided in the ratio of 2:1 to male toilets. 
 First-aid facilities and/or an adequately equipped sick-bay affiliated with a government- approved hospital close to the school. 
 Fire control equipment in line with the regulations of the State Fire Service Department: at least 5 fire extinguishers per school. 
 Adequate property fencing and gates.

reform_title: Introduction of a new funding model for higher education
reform_description: Norway has introduced several revisions to its funding model for higher education. The current model was introduced in 2002 to improve overall quality, to promote student progression, and to better align higher education offers with student and labour market demand. One of the major changes at that time was the introduction of a performance-based funding component.

Norway has implemented some measures to increase affordability of ECEC attendance (2004-19). These measures include the introduction of a maximum fee for ECEC in 2004, and subsequent measures to reduce the costs borne by households

reform_title: Extention of statuary right to ECEC
reform_description: In 2009, Norway extended the statutory right to ECEC from the age of 1

The Educational Act (2010) introduced a requirement for municipalities to offer eight hours per week of homework assistance to students in Grades 1-4 from 2010. The aim is to reduce the impact of parents’ level of education on student achievement, and has since been extended to all levels of compulsory education.

The Assessment for Learning Programme (2010-18) was developed to support schools, training providers, and local authorities in improving formative assessment practices. The Directorate for Education and Training set guiding principles for the content and organisation of the programme, while local authorities were charged with local-level implementation. The Directorate also organised seminars and conferences for participating local authorities and provided online training and resources for schools.
Accreditation regulations had already been revised in 2011 to include criteria on labour market relevance.

reform_title: The National Qualifications Framework for Lifelong Learning (2011)
reform_description: The National Qualifications Framework for Lifelong Learning (2011) aims to provide an overview of the Norwegian education system, and of its different levels of qualifications. The framework places qualifications on seven levels, starting from the end of lower secondary education to doctoral level. Levels are defined in terms of learning outcomes relating to knowledge, skills and general competences. The framework is also intended to improve communication between the education sector and the labour market and to facilitate comparison between qualifications acquired in Norway and in other countries.
Further regulations introduced in 2015 mean that no family pays more than 6% of their annual income towards the cost of ECEC.
Norway has introduced a scheme offering low-income families 20 hours of ECEC per week free of charge. In 2015, only 4- and 5-year-olds could benefit from these core hours

The Certificate of Practice Scheme (Praksisbrev) is an alternative VET pathway aimed at students who are struggling in mainstream VET. Its aim is to improve completion rates among these students. The scheme was piloted between 2008 and 2011 and was adopted as a permanent arrangement in 2016. Whereas most VET programmes in Norway involve two years of school-based learning before beginning a two-year apprenticeship period, certificate of practice candidates alternate between school-based training and training in enterprise. At the end of the programme, students can transition to the journeyman’s certificate (a formal VET qualification) or continue their training in a particular trade.

reform_title: The National Programme to Promote Educational Success (Programa Nacional de Promoção do Sucesso Escolar, PNPSE, 2016)
reform_description: The National Programme to Promote Educational Success (Programa Nacional de Promoção do Sucesso Escolar, PNPSE, 2016) is a comprehensive strategy to combat school failure and grade repetition. PNPSE takes a preventive approach, promoting academic success in the first cycle of primary education via enhanced classroom interactions, early-intervention, teacher collaboration and comprehensive evaluation of student competences. There has been strong emphasis on building capacity for teachers and school leaders; PNPSE supports schools to develop improvement plans for their context and by 2018, 663 schools had done so. PNPSE also supports municipalities to develop local projects aligned with school actions; by 2018, 2 915 different actions had been defined locally, then disseminated nationally.

reform_title: The National Credit System (Sistema Nacional de Créditos, 2016)
reform_description: The National Credit System (Sistema Nacional de Créditos, 2016) reorganised VET curriculum to improve alignment with the ECVET framework and enhance the flexibility, mobility and quality of VET programmes.

Portugal’s leading policy initiative on adult learning is the Qualifica Programme (Programa Qualifica, 2016), an integrated strategy to stimulate the training and qualifications of adults to enhance their employability. It has four primary objectives: 1) improve qualification levels among adults and foster employability; 2) increase digital and functional literacy; 3) align training with labour market needs; and 4) better tailor training pathways to adults’ needs. Qualifica targets the unemployed, NEETs and individuals with less than upper-secondary educational attainment. The programme has been working towards the goal of ensuring that, by 2020, 50% of the active population have upper-secondary education qualifications, 40% of 30-34 year-olds have tertiary qualifications, 600 000 NEETs participate in training and 15% of adults participate in lifelong learning activities, growing to 25% by 2025. To achieve these goals, the programme has three key strategic actions: 1) Establish a national network of Qualifica Centres (Centros Qualifica, QCs, 2016): QCs, which replace the former Centres for Qualification and Vocational Education, aim to reinforce the system’s proximity to its target populations. They provide information and guidance, designing training plans for adults and NEETs. They also offer the Recognition, Validation and Certification of Competences (RVCC) process, which provides formal recognition for formal, informal and non-formal learning. This process requires a training component of 50 hours. QCs also provide training activities through partnership networks with employers, providers and municipalities and play an active role in motivating and recruiting adults for lifelong learning. 2) Introduce the Qualifica Passport (Passaporte Qualifica, QP, 2017): this user-oriented digital tool records the qualifications and skills individuals have attained, their training pathways and their areas of training interest. It also provides guidance as to recommended pathways to qualification. The QP can be modified and updated at any time, rendering it a tool to support lifelong learning. It is available to any individual, irrespective of enrolment in a training programme. 3) Launch the National Credit System (Sistema Nacional de Créditos do Ensino e Formação Profissionais, 2017): this initiative aims to cement Portugal’s alignment with the ECVET system. It sets out units of learning outcomes which make up qualifications and can be assessed and validated individually. It is expected that this should enable learners to accumulate learning outcomes gradually and flexibly across different contexts, accumulating them for certification and enhancing learner mobility

reform_title: Updated Curriculum Guidelines for Pre-School Education (Orientações Curriculares para a Educação Pré-escolar, OCEPE, 2016)
reform_description: An external evaluation of pre-school education in 2013 led to curriculum revision. Updated Curriculum Guidelines for Pre-School Education (Orientações Curriculares para a Educação Pré-escolar, OCEPE, 2016) aimed to improve alignment with the first cycle of basic education and encourage collaboration between educators during students’ transition to primary education. OCEPE is both a reference for curriculum development and a set of general pedagogical and organisational principles for ECEC professionals. It promotes an integrated and globalised approach to content areas, providing practical examples of learning processes and staff reflection. Adding Physical and Artistic Education to the curriculum has broadened its scope.

reform_description: The Programme for the Modernisation and Valorisation of the Polytechnic Sector (Programa de Modernização e Valorização dos Institutos Politécnicos, 2016), aims to modernise the model of polytechnic higher education and applied research and improve perception.

reform_title: Portugal’s Schools’ Participatory Budget (Orçamento Participativo das Escolas, 2016)
reform_description: Portugal’s Schools’ Participatory Budget (Orçamento Participativo das Escolas, 2016) allocates all public secondary schools EUR 1 per student, to be used according to students’ wishes. Students develop proposals for school improvement, secure a minimum number of signatures from their peers and then submit to the principal. Once approved, proposals are voted on by all students.

reform_title: Preference for disadvantaged students receiving School Social Assistance (ASE)
reform_description: School choice is enshrined in Portuguese legislation, with non-academic selection criteria for oversubscribed schools, including, since 2017, preference for disadvantaged students receiving School Social Assistance (ASE).

reform_title: Legislation to gradually reduce class sizes

reform_title: The Students’ Profile at the End of Compulsory Schooling (Perfil dos Alunos à Saída da Escolaridade Obrigatória, 2017)
reform_description: In 2017, Portugal published a new framework for teaching, learning and assessment delineating what young people are expected to achieve by age 18. The Students’ Profile at the End of Compulsory Schooling (Perfil dos Alunos à Saída da Escolaridade Obrigatória, 2017) establishes Essential Learnings for each discipline at all levels of compulsory schooling and embraces the idea of transversality, calling upon each curriculum area to develop all competency areas defined in the Students’ Profile.
