It strengthened the active role of special education teachers within school teams called for multilevel and multidisciplinary intervention and mandated that inclusive education be assessed through external and internal school evaluation cycles.

National legislation introducing new curriculum principles of autonomy and curricular flexibility across the education system following the pilot Project for Autonomy and Curricular Flexibility. Regional teams were established to monitor and support schools through local school networks to ensure correct implementation of the new curriculum design.

reform_title: Third Cycle of External School Evaluation
reform_description: Launch of the third cycle of external school evaluation with a revised model widening the programme's goals and scope of action including strengthening information from evaluations providing greater support to schools enhancing capacity to ensure quality learning and placing stronger focus on classroom-level practices and inclusive education.

reform_title: Special Contest for Tertiary Admissions for VET and Specialised Artistic Graduates
reform_description: A new special admissions contest for tertiary education specifically for vocational education and training and specialised artistic graduates approved in 2020 and to be implemented from the 2020/21 academic year to facilitate transitions from upper-secondary VET into higher education.
A long-term structural reform to cluster schools under single executive leadership teams and close or merge small, isolated schools for greater efficiency.
Establishment of an external school evaluation system conducted on a five-year cycle under the Inspectorate-General of Education and Science.
A comprehensive system established to expand and regulate adult learning and qualifications across the country.
A financial and welfare support program aimed at assisting socio-economically disadvantaged students based on needs identified by school-based social workers.

reform_title: Third Education Territories of Priority Intervention Programme (TEIP3)
reform_description: A targeted program adopting a territorial approach to reduce early school-leaving and improve educational outcomes among socio-economically disadvantaged students by promoting school autonomy.
A comprehensive strategy designed to reinforce the duality of the vocational education and training system.
Curriculum revisions to improve transitions between vocational, general, and tertiary education, expanding higher-skilled occupation pathways and introducing more work-based programs.
Establishment of a continuous professional development framework linking teacher training hours to formal career progression steps.

reform_title: Restructuring of School Association Professional Development Centres (CFAE)
reform_description: Decree-Laws providing CFAEs with formal institutional roles and greater autonomy to determine and provide local professional development training aligned with school needs.
A policy initiative guaranteeing universal free pre-primary education for all children aged 3 to 5 years old.
A national strategy aimed at improving adult learning, raising awareness of skills, improving training quality and relevance, and strengthening governance and financing.
Launch of a national digital portal providing transparent data access to school and student results from internal and external assessments.

reform_title: Institute for the Management of Educational Finance (IGeFE)
reform_description: Creation of a centralized body managing education finance through digital platforms like SmartMEC to improve financial planning, centralize salary payments, and increase data accessibility.

reform_title: National Programme to Promote Educational Success (PNPSE)
reform_description: A comprehensive strategy to combat school failure and grade repetition through a preventive approach, early intervention, and teacher collaboration in primary education.
Reorganization of the vocational education and training curriculum to improve alignment with the ECVET framework, enhancing flexibility and mobility.
A strategy supporting young people under 25 in finding employment, education, or apprenticeships within four months of becoming unemployed or leaving formal education.
An integrated strategy to stimulate the training and qualifications of adults, particularly the unemployed and low-skilled, to enhance digital and functional literacy and employability.

Establishment of a national network of centers to replace former qualification centers, providing information, guidance, and the Recognition, Validation and Certification of Competences (RVCC) for adults.

reform_title: Comprehensive Model for External Student Assessments
reform_description: A new assessment model that replaced end-of-cycle examinations in grades four and eight with lower-stakes, formative national assessments in grades two, five, and eight.
Updated curriculum guidelines for pre-primary education promoting an integrated pedagogical approach and better alignment with primary education.
Creation of an advisory body comprising diverse stakeholders and higher education experts to advise on national higher education priorities.

reform_title: Programme for the Modernisation and Valorisation of the Polytechnic Sector
reform_description: A funding program investing in equipment, applied research, and operational modernization to improve the quality of polytechnic higher education.
A national funding initiative allocating public secondary schools dedicated funds to be spent according to project proposals developed and voted on by students.

reform_title: Quality Assurance Framework for VET Courses
reform_description: Introduction of a quality assurance framework aligned with the European Quality Assurance Reference Framework for VET (EQAVET) to improve vocational education.
Introduction of a user-oriented digital tool that records the qualifications, skills, and training pathways individuals have attained to support lifelong learning.

reform_title: National Credit System for Adult Learning
reform_description: An initiative to align Portugal's adult learning with the ECVET system by setting out units of learning outcomes that can be individually assessed and accumulated over time.
A systemic pilot granting selected school clusters full autonomy to experiment with innovative pedagogies and school organization to reduce early school-leaving and grade repetition.
Cancellation of salary cuts and freezes, which had been in place since 2011, enabling salary and career progression for teaching and non-teaching staff.

reform_title: Extraordinary Recruitment and Contract Reforms for Teachers
reform_description: A human resources policy that extraordinarily recruited teaching staff on permanent contracts and reduced the requirement for conversion from annual to permanent contracts from five to three successive contracts.
Introduction of national legislation to gradually reduce student class sizes in primary education.
Implementation of a centralized digital information management system to handle administrative data for students from pre-school through upper-secondary education.
A cross-sectoral strategy focused on enhancing digital literacy, integrating digital skills into teaching, and expanding the ICT curriculum.
A strategy establishing mandatory teaching areas such as human rights, gender equality, and environmental sustainability to support citizenship skills.

reform_title: Students' Profile at the End of Compulsory Schooling
reform_description: Publication of a holistic national curriculum framework defining the transversal competency areas and essential learnings students must achieve by age 18.

reform_title: Project for Autonomy and Curricular Flexibility (PAFC)
reform_description: A systemic pilot program granting volunteering schools autonomy over a portion of their curriculum time to design innovative learning experiences aligned with the new Students' Profile.
Changes to the study grant system to increase efficiency and support for disadvantaged students, including standardization, digitizing applications, and introducing multi-year grants.

reform_title: Study Grants for Special Educational Needs
reform_description: Introduction of a dedicated system of higher education study grants specifically for students with special educational needs.
A law emphasizing the responsibility of schools to identify and overcome barriers to individual students' learning through multidisciplinary intervention and inclusive practices.

reform_title: Decree-Law on Autonomy and Curricular Flexibility
reform_description: Formal legislation (Decree-Law 55/2018) embedding curriculum flexibility into law, supported by regional teams to monitor and support school implementation nationwide.
A financing policy restricting state funding for private schools exclusively to localities where the public school offer is deemed insufficient.

reform_title: Third Cycle of External School Evaluation
reform_description: Launch of a revised external evaluation model with a stronger developmental focus on classroom-level practices, inclusive education, and student competencies.

reform_title: Special Tertiary Admissions Contest for VET and Artistic Graduates
reform_description: Approval of a new specialized admissions process to facilitate the entry of vocational education and training (VET) and specialized artistic graduates into tertiary education.
Long-term consolidation reform closing or merging small isolated schools and clustering schools under one leadership team to improve efficiency and reduce managerial costs.
Five-year external school evaluation system for pre-primary, basic and upper-secondary education combining external inspection with internal self-evaluation and school improvement planning.
Policy allowing high-performing schools and clusters to enter four-year autonomy contracts granting greater control over finances and, in some cases, curriculum.
National framework supporting adult learning and qualifications, forming a basis for the expansion of adult education and training.

Systematic teacher appraisal framework assessing teachers' content knowledge, pedagogical skills, contributions to the school community and commitment to professional improvement, with positive appraisal required for career progression.

reform_title: Higher education institutional autonomy and governance reform
reform_description: Higher education governance reform granting institutions greater autonomy and enabling governance through a General Council, Rector and Management Council overseen by a Board of Trustees with internal and external stakeholders.
Reform of school management replacing collegial management with a school leader and three governance bodies, and requiring qualified candidates to take precedence in school leader appointments.
Student support financing mechanism providing assistance to students living in poor conditions based on applications submitted by school-based social workers.

reform_title: Third Education Territories of Priority Intervention Programme
reform_description: Third phase of the TEIP programme using a territorial approach to reduce early school leaving and improve outcomes among disadvantaged students by promoting school autonomy, annual evaluation, and national monitoring.
National integrated vocational education and training strategy aimed at reinforcing the duality of the VET system.
Changes to VET syllabi to improve transitions between VET, general education and tertiary education, introduce more work-based programmes, and expand higher-skilled occupational offerings.
Creation of a fully autonomous body specialising in external evaluation and national tests.
Teacher professional development framework linking career progression to required training hours across career steps.

reform_title: Clarification of School Association Professional Development Centres
reform_description: Decree-Law 22/2014 and Decree-Law 127/2015 clarified the role of School Association Professional Development Centres as formal teacher training institutions with greater autonomy to identify local training needs and develop training plans.

reform_title: Universal free pre-primary education for 3-5 year-olds
reform_description: Policy to guarantee universal free pre-primary education for children aged 3-5, supported by classroom expansion and full implementation targeted by 2019.
National strategy developed with OECD support to improve skills, including actions to strengthen adult learning, improve access, quality and relevance, and strengthen governance and financing.

National information portal providing data for all public and government-dependent schools and students from grades 5 to 12, including internal and external assessment results and school partnership information.

reform_title: Institute for the Management of Educational Finance and SmartMEC
reform_description: Creation of the education finance management institute and digital financial management platforms to centralise school financial information, salary payments, financial data and budget planning tools.

reform_title: National Programme to Promote Educational Success
reform_description: Comprehensive national strategy to reduce school failure and grade repetition through early intervention, improved classroom practice, teacher collaboration, student competence evaluation, and school and municipal improvement plans.
Reorganisation of VET curriculum to align with the ECVET framework and improve flexibility, mobility and quality in vocational education and training programmes.

Strategy supporting young people under 25 to access employment, education, apprenticeships or traineeships within four months of unemployment or leaving formal education, using local partner networks and an electronic platform.

Integrated adult learning strategy to improve adult qualifications, employability, digital and functional literacy, labour-market alignment, and tailored adult training pathways through Qualifica Centres, the Qualifica Passport and related tools.

reform_title: Comprehensive Model for External Student Assessments in Basic Education
reform_description: Assessment reform replacing grade 4 and 8 end-of-cycle exams with lower-stakes national assessments in grades 2, 5 and 8, introducing grade 9 national examinations, and using assessment information to support learning and reduce school failure.

Updated pre-school curriculum guidelines to improve alignment with the first cycle of basic education, support transition to primary education, define pedagogical and organisational principles, and broaden the curriculum.
Creation of an advisory body for higher education composed of stakeholders from each sector and supported by domestic and international higher education experts.

reform_title: Programme for the Modernisation and Valorisation of the Polytechnic Sector
reform_description: Programme to modernise polytechnic higher education and applied research through investment in professional technical courses, practice-based research and development, and polytechnic research capacity.
Funding mechanism allocating public secondary schools EUR 1 per student for projects proposed and voted on by students to improve schools and strengthen civic engagement.

reform_title: Quality assurance framework for VET courses
reform_description: Introduction of a VET quality assurance framework aligned with the European Quality Assurance Reference Framework for VET, with course guidance taking quality assurance results into account.

Three-year pilot granting selected school clusters full autonomy to depart from legislation and test innovations in curriculum, pedagogy, organisation and community relations to reduce dropout and grade repetition.

reform_title: Teacher career and salary progression measures
reform_description: Cancellation of teacher and education support staff salary cuts and freezes, enabling salary and career progression for teachers and non-teaching education support staff.

Teacher workforce reform through extraordinary recruitment of teaching staff on permanent contracts and reduction of the requirement for conversion from annual to permanent contracts from five to three successive contracts.
Government legislation to gradually reduce class sizes in schools.
Centralised administrative information system for students from pre-school to upper secondary education, piloted in 2017 and rolled out from 2018 to public and private institutions.

Cross-sectoral digital competences initiative with education measures including integrating digital skills and resources into teaching, developing digital resources, extending the ICT curriculum, and creating an ICT reference framework for younger students.
National strategy creating mandatory citizenship education areas, including democratic institutions, human rights, gender equality, environmental sustainability and health education.

reform_title: Students' Profile at the End of Compulsory Schooling and Essential Learnings
reform_description: New framework for teaching, learning and assessment defining what students should achieve by age 18 and establishing Essential Learnings for each discipline across compulsory schooling.

reform_title: Project for Autonomy and Curricular Flexibility
reform_description: Pilot programme granting participating schools autonomy over 0-25% of curriculum time to design learning experiences aligned with the Students' Profile and principles of curricular flexibility, inclusion, citizenship education and assessment for learning.
Reform of tertiary student grants introducing multi-year grants, standardised and digitised application review procedures, and a new grant system for students with special educational needs.

Decree-Law No. 54/2018 requiring schools to identify barriers to individual student learning, develop multilevel and multidisciplinary support strategies, strengthen special education teachers' roles, and include inclusive education in school evaluation.
Curriculum reform introducing new principles of school autonomy and curricular flexibility, supported by regional teams and school networks to implement the new curriculum design.

reform_title: Restriction of state funding for private schools
reform_description: Policy limiting state funding for private schools to localities where public provision is insufficient.

reform_title: Third cycle of External School Evaluation
reform_description: Revised third cycle of external school evaluation with a new model widening the programme's goals, strengthening support to schools, focusing on classroom practices and inclusive education, and aligning with the Students' Profile.

reform_title: Special tertiary admissions contest for VET and specialised artistic graduates
reform_description: Approved special admissions route to higher education for vocational education and training and specialised artistic graduates, to be implemented from 2020/21.
since 1998 (Order 3618/ 1998) the MoNE pays the cost of teacher salaries, with Step by Step implementing the program. Step by Step preprimary programs currently serve 13, 800 children in 36 counties.

reform_title: The National Parenting Education Program in Preschool Education in 2001
reform_description: the National Parenting Education Program in Preschool Education in 2001
The MoNE endorsed a curriculum for preprimary education in 2008 under a World Bank funded project (Social Inclusion Project – Program for Inclusive Early Childhood Education)

In 2010 Romania adopted the Early Learning Development Standards (ELDS). The standards were developed by a group of experts with support from UNICEF, and they have been validated for content and for the population. The standards are used across sectors (health, education, and protection). The standards cover (i) Physical Development, Health, Personal Care and Hygiene; (ii) Socio-Emotional Development; (iii) Approaches to Learning; (iv) Language, Communication, Pre-Reading and Pre-Writing Skills and (v) Cognitive Development and World Knowledge

The National Education Law adopted in 2011 provided a comprehensive framework for the Romanian early childhood care and education (ECCE) system structured in two-tiers: ante-preschool level for children aged between 0-3 years and preschool level for children between 3-6 years.

National legislation requires the provision of early childhood education. Romania’s Education Law 1/2011 stipulates that free public early education services should be provided starting from birth until age six (at which point children enter primary school). This low included for the first time the antepreschool level as part of the formal education system.
According to the 2011 Education Law, the Ministry of National Education (MoNE) took over responsibility for crèches from the MoH.
Among its positive interventions, in 2012 Romania introduced a preparatory grade (grade 0) into compulsory education

reform_title: The Methodology of Organization and Functioning of Nurseries and Other Early Preschool Education Services
reform_description: the organization and functioning of creches is provided in the Methodology of Organization and Functioning of Nurseries and Other Early Preschool Education Services (GD Nr.1252/2012).

Law no. 248 in 2015 which encourages the regular attendance of 3-6-year-old children in Kindergarten by granting €11 per month in food coupons (conditional on the child’s daily attendance in preschool) to households where the monthly income is under 284 lei (€63) per family member.

reform_title: Establishment of The Directorate of Early Education, Primary and Secondary Education within the MoNE
reform_description: The Directorate of Early Education, Primary and Secondary Education within the MoNE was established in 2017 by a ministerial order. Per the order, the directorate of early education is supposed to have 15 staff members.

The MoNE endorsed a curriculum for preprimary education in 2008 under a World Bank funded project (Social Inclusion Project – Program for Inclusive Early Childhood Education) and updated it in 2017 (including age 0-3)

The National Reform Program (NRP) 2018 includes some ECD elements, but there is no explicit objective to address ECD holistically. The NRP includes an objective on reducing early school leaving, with a component focused on early education services.

The MoNE endorsed a curriculum for preprimary education in 2008 under a World Bank funded project (Social Inclusion Project – Program for Inclusive Early Childhood Education) and updated it in 2017 (including age 0-3) and 2018.

reform_title: Establishment of educational and vocational services and of rehabilitation measures
reform_description: The Ministry of Labor and Social Justice (MoLSJ), MoH, and the MoNE established educational and vocational services for children with special needs, and rehabilitation measures for children with disabilities and/or special education needs.

There have been cases of diagnosing children from marginalized groups as having special needs for the purpose of relegating them to special education classes (and thereby further marginalizing them), so there is provision in the Law on Education to prohibit this discriminatory practice.

From 2016 to 2018 the Educated Romania project initiated by the country’s President has carried out a public debate on the future of education in the country. The main conclusions were made public in December 2018. Early education represents a distinct chapter of the project. The ECD program has five objectives: a) developing a network of crèches and other accredited quality services to allow access to early education services; b) promoting gradual universal coverage in preschool of children ages 3-5; c) developing standards for quality assurance; d) improving the quality of pre-service and in-service education for ECD staff; e) revising the curriculum.

reform_title: The Reform Program for Early Education (PRET)
reform_description: The Reform Program for Early Education (PRET - EUR 105 mil financed by CEB), PRET provided training for kindergarten staff in all kindergartens in Romania (35,000 pre-school teacher, 13,000 care and medical staff, and 2,500 managers) covering early education, counseling services, parental education, school readiness, teaching-learning methods, kindergarten-community partnerships.

reform_title: The Program for Inclusive Early Education (PETI)
reform_description: The Program for Inclusive Early Education (PETI - EUR 10 mil. financed by World Bank). The PETI program focused on development of curriculum (0-3 and 3-6), the preparation of guidebooks for early education (0-3), good practices (3-6), inclusive education (0-6).

reform_title: The Strategy to Reduce Early School Leaving (ESL)
reform_description: The Strategy to Reduce Early School Leaving (ESL) promotes specific measures to reduce the ESL risk through ECD, but implementation progress has been slow. This strategy aims at reducing ESL to 11.3 percent by 2020.

reform_title: Regulation of Organization and Functioning of Pre-university Education Units)
reform_description: public (and some private) Kindergartens/preschool programs are found throughout the country and their organization and functioning is regulated through ministerial order (Regulation of Organization and Functioning of Pre-university Education Units).

reform_title: The Minimum Social Insertion Income Program
reform_description: The new Minimum Social Insertion Income Program is supposed to cover the poorest 20-22 percent of the population. It includes conditionality elements such as keeping children in school.

The Roma Education Fund (REF) Romania project Ready, Set, Go! was aimed at improving child development outcomes for Roma children. It expanded access to Roma children to Kindergarten by renovating 14 facilities in 11 localities. The project also established toy libraries and held community events and parenting skills promotion. It was funded by the Norway Grants - Financial Mechanism Office and implemented with World Bank technical assistance.

reform_title: Social Inclusion Project – Program for Inclusive Early Childhood Education
reform_description: The MoNE endorsed a curriculum for preprimary education in 2008 under a World Bank funded project (Social Inclusion Project – Program for Inclusive Early Childhood Education) and updated it in 2017 (including age 0-3) and 2018.
Continuous professional development is mandatory under the Education Law.

reform_title: The Methodology of Organization and Functioning of Nurseries and Other Early Preschool Education Services
reform_description: Infrastructure and service delivery standards for early childhood education are established for public and private providers. ARACIP (the Romanian Agency for Quality Assurance in Pre-University Education) establishes and monitors these standards. The standards are laid out in the Methodology of Organization and Functioning of Nurseries and Other Early Preschool Education Services and the Regulation of Organization and Functioning of Pre-University Education Units. These include standards on the minimum number of operating hours and child-to-teacher ratios.

reform_title: The Regulation of Organization and Functioning of Pre-University Education Units
reform_description: Infrastructure and service delivery standards for early childhood education are established for public and private providers. ARACIP (the Romanian Agency for Quality Assurance in Pre-University Education) establishes and monitors these standards. The standards are laid out in the Methodology of Organization and Functioning of Nurseries and Other Early Preschool Education Services and the Regulation of Organization and Functioning of Pre-University Education Units. These include standards on the minimum number of operating hours and child-to-teacher ratios.

reform_title: Step by Step Alternative Education Program – Government Co-financing
reform_description: Since 1998 the Ministry of National Education has paid teacher salaries for the Step by Step child-centered alternative education program for preschool and primary education (Order 3618/1998) enabling the NGO to implement the program in 36 counties serving approximately 13800 children.

reform_title: National Parenting Education Program in Preschool Education
reform_description: Romania adopted a National Parenting Education Program in Preschool Education that has had an impact on family knowledge attitudes and practices related to early stimulation parent-child interactions and positive discipline.

reform_title: Preprimary Education Curriculum Endorsement and Update
reform_description: The Ministry of National Education endorsed a curriculum for preprimary education under a World Bank-funded project (Social Inclusion Project – Program for Inclusive Early Childhood Education) and subsequently updated it in 2017 to include ages 0-3 and again in 2018. The curriculum is grounded in the Early Learning Development Standards and aligned with the primary curriculum for the preparatory grade.

reform_title: Adoption of Early Learning Development Standards (ELDS)
reform_description: Romania adopted the Early Learning Development Standards covering five domains: Physical Development Health Personal Care and Hygiene; Socio-Emotional Development; Approaches to Learning; Language Communication Pre-Reading and Pre-Writing Skills; and Cognitive Development and World Knowledge. The standards were developed with UNICEF support validated for content and population and are used across health education and child protection sectors.

A comprehensive national education law that established a two-tier early childhood care and education system covering ante-preschool level (ages 0-3) and preschool level (ages 3-6). The law included for the first time the ante-preschool level as part of the formal education system and stipulated that free public early education services should be provided from birth until age six. It also transferred responsibility for crèches from the Ministry of Health to the Ministry of National Education and mandated continuous professional development for teachers.

A government program financed by the Council of Europe Development Bank (EUR 105 million) that provided training for kindergarten staff across all kindergartens in Romania covering 35000 pre-school teachers 13000 care and medical staff and 2500 managers. Training covered early education counseling services parental education school readiness teaching-learning methods and kindergarten-community partnerships.

A World Bank-financed program (EUR 10 million) focused on development of curriculum for ages 0-3 and 3-6 preparation of guidebooks for early education (0-3) good practices (3-6) and inclusive education (0-6) across Romania.

reform_title: Introduction of Preparatory Grade (Grade 0) into Compulsory Education
reform_description: Romania introduced a preparatory grade (grade 0) into compulsory education as part of the primary school system. This grade is implemented nation-wide in schools and is assessed as having had a positive impact on reducing dropout rates in the first school years and helping reduce disparities among students.

reform_title: Methodology of Organization and Functioning of Nurseries and Other Early Preschool Education Services (GD 1252/2012)
reform_description: A government decree establishing the regulatory framework for the organization and functioning of crèches and other early preschool education services including requirements for parental support counseling information and education services.

reform_title: National Strategy for the Protection and Promotion of the Rights of Children (2014-2020)
reform_description: A national strategy adopted in 2014 establishing a framework for child protection and promotion of children's rights across sectors for the period 2014-2020.

reform_title: Law No. 248/2015 – Kindergarten Food Coupon Program
reform_description: A national law that encourages regular attendance of 3-6-year-old children in kindergarten by granting €11 per month in food coupons conditional on daily attendance to households where monthly income is under 284 lei per family member. Originally piloted by NGO Ovidiu RO in partnership with the Ministry of National Education in 2010 and scaled into national legislation in 2015.

reform_title: National Strategy to Reduce Early School Leaving (2015-2020)
reform_description: A national strategy adopted in 2015 aiming to reduce early school leaving to 11.3 percent by 2020. It served as an ex-ante conditionality for Romania's access to EU funds in the 2014-2020 programming period and includes ECD prevention measures as flagship programs.

reform_title: Deinstitutionalization Program for Children in Residential Care
reform_description: The National Authority for the Protection of Children's Rights and Adoptions (NAPCRA) launched a program to develop common procedures and methodologies for central and local public administration authorities to transition children from institutional care to community-based care in line with Law 272/2004.

The Government of Romania introduced a comprehensive anti-poverty program comprising a package of services targeted to specific groups of the population linking social protection with parenting and early childhood development components.

The government revised the teacher salary grid resulting in an average 10 percent increase for beginner teachers in 2016 followed by a 15 percent increase for all teachers in 2017 with additional allowances for teachers in rural areas.

reform_title: Establishment of the Directorate of Early Education Primary and Secondary Education within MoNE
reform_description: The Ministry of National Education established a dedicated Directorate of Early Education Primary and Secondary Education by ministerial order to focus institutional attention on early education policy design implementation and monitoring.

reform_title: Integrated Intervention Methodology for Children with Disabilities
reform_description: The Ministry of Labor and Social Justice Ministry of Health and Ministry of National Education jointly established an integrated intervention methodology for defining the level of a child's disability and established educational vocational and rehabilitation services for children with special needs promoting inclusive education across the three ministries.

A comprehensive legislative framework that structured the early childhood care and education system into a two-tier model, establishing an ante-preschool level for children up to age three and a preschool level for children aged three to six.
A policy introducing a mandatory preparatory grade into the compulsory education system to reduce dropout rates in the early school years and minimize disparities among students.
A donor-financed government program that provided nationwide training to kindergarten staff, care and medical personnel, and managers on early education, counseling, parenting, and teaching methods.
A World Bank-financed initiative that focused on the development of inclusive early childhood curricula for children ages 0-6 and the preparation of early education guidebooks.

reform_title: Social Inclusion through Provision of Integrated Services at the Community Level
reform_description: A multi-sectoral pilot program jointly implemented by several government ministries and UNICEF in Bacau county to systematically deliver integrated health, education, and social protection services to vulnerable families.

reform_title: Step by Step alternative education program
reform_description: Government recognition and partial financing of the Step by Step alternative for preschool and primary education, with MoNE paying teacher salaries for a child-centered program emphasizing family and community involvement.

reform_title: National Parenting Education Program in Preschool Education
reform_description: Adoption of a national preschool parenting education program to improve family knowledge, attitudes, and practices related to early stimulation, parent-child interaction, and positive discipline.

MoNE endorsement of a preprimary education curriculum under the World Bank-financed Social Inclusion Project, later updated to include ages 0-3 and aligned with early learning development standards and the preparatory grade curriculum.

Adoption of cross-sector early learning and development standards covering physical development, socio-emotional development, approaches to learning, language and pre-literacy, and cognitive development.

National Education Law 1/2011 established a two-tier early childhood care and education system, incorporated ante-preschool education into the formal education system, required free public early education from birth to age six, and shifted responsibility for crèches to the Ministry of National Education.
Introduction of a nationwide preparatory grade, or grade 0, into compulsory education before primary school entry.

reform_title: Methodology for nurseries and early preschool services
reform_description: Government Decision 1252/2012 established the organization and functioning framework for nurseries and other early preschool education services, including requirements for crèche provision and parental support services.

reform_title: Strategy to Reduce Early School Leaving
reform_description: Adoption of a strategy to reduce early school leaving to 11.3 percent by 2020, including flagship programs with early childhood development measures, preschool staff training, parental involvement, and allocation of resources to ECD programs.

reform_title: Law No. 248 preschool attendance food coupons
reform_description: Scaling up of the Every Child in Kindergarten initiative through Law No. 248, providing monthly food coupons to low-income households conditional on regular kindergarten attendance by children aged 3-6.

reform_title: Directorate of Early Education, Primary and Secondary Education
reform_description: Establishment by ministerial order of a Ministry of National Education directorate responsible for early education, primary education, and secondary education.

reform_title: National Reform Program early education components
reform_description: The 2018 National Reform Program included early education measures under objectives on reducing early school leaving and social inclusion, including building kindergartens and crèches and developing integrated community services in rural areas.

reform_title: Integrated intervention methodology for children with disabilities and special education needs
reform_description: MoLSJ, MoH, and MoNE established an integrated methodology for defining disability level and providing educational, vocational, and rehabilitation services for children with disabilities and/or special education needs.

A CEB-financed national early education program trained kindergarten staff across all Romanian kindergartens, including preschool teachers, care and medical staff, and managers, in early education, counseling, parental education, school readiness, teaching methods, and kindergarten-community partnerships.

A World Bank-financed inclusive early education program developed curricula for ages 0-3 and 3-6, guidebooks for early education, good practices guidance, and inclusive education materials for ages 0-6.
Operation of a summer kindergarten programme lasting at least 45 days as a catch-up program for children who missed early childhood education for socio-economic reasons.

In the area of TVET, the government has adopted an extensive and forward-looking framework for TVET consisting of a TVET Policy. The TVET Policy (2008) has four pillars: (i) systems development – including development of the national training authority (the Workforce Development Agency,
WDA) and design of a national qualifications framework; (ii) expansion of access; (iii) improved
quality through curriculum development and instructor training; and (iv) establishment of
sustainable financing. The thrust of the policy framework is to strengthen links between
enterprises and training supply, to structure training on the basis of updated occupational
competencies, and to develop an integrated TVET system. Integration has a triple meaning:
integration of all technical and vocational provision, public and private, under the WDA; horizontal integration across the levels of training – from vocational to technical secondary to
technical tertiary; and regional integration of training providers under the coordination and
supervision of Integrated Polytechnic Regional Centers (IPRC) in each province.

The WDA (the Workforce Development Agency,
WDA) Strategy and Action Plan, which was produced in December 2009,
translates strategic objectives into detailed actions and steps assigned to various units of WDA.

The WDA, formally established in 2009, is a legal
entity with administrative and financial autonomy, supervised by the Ministry of Education.
Responsibilities of the WDA include developing TVET curricula and standards; training of
TVET trainers; setting up a system for examinations, certification, and accreditation; and the
esatblishment of a National TVET Qualification framework.

The Government has adopted an integrated approach to the development of the
sector which, in addition to continuing to focus on basic education, includes addressing
constraints related to post-basic education and to the linkages between the overall
education and training system and labor demand. This is reflected in the newly developed
Education Sector Strategic Plan 2010-2015 (ESSP), which identifies as key objectives:
improving completion and transition rates in basic education, along with improving educational
quality and ensuring that the Post-Basic Education and Training (PBET) system is better tailored
to meeting labor market needs. One of the strategic priorities identified in the ESSP to achieve
these objectives is the expansion of access to high-quality and demand-responsive TVET, which
should go hand in hand with continued efforts of the government to increase access to and the
quality of general education on both the basic and the post-basic level.

Strategic priorities identified in the

In the area of TVET, the government has adopted an extensive and forward-looking framework for TVET consisting of an Integrated TVET Concept Paper. The TVET Concept Paper describes how integration will take place with a focus on development of the WDA and IPRCs.

In addition to strategic documents like the Vision 2020 and the Economic
Development and Poverty Reduction Strategy, MINEDUC’s activities are guided by the
Education Sector Strategic Plan and various sub-sector strategies and policies.

The project will help Rwanda achieve its long term development vision of
becoming a middle income, export- and service-oriented knowledge economy by supporting the
development of an appropriately skilled labor force.

The project particularly contributes to the priority of promoting economic transformation and growth as identified in the 2008-2012 Rwanda CAS.
The project will support this priority through contributing to an increase in the pool of skilled
labor for the private (and public) sector. In addition, the project contributes to the CAS priority
of reducing social vulnerability, by providing trainees with increased opportunities for income
generation.

The project development objective is to improve access to quality and demand-
responsive vocational training.

The project will alleviate current and longer term skills constraints to contribute to
Rwanda’s transition to a middle income, export- and service-oriented knowledge economy by
2020.

The government has established a strong policy and institutional basis for the
development of TVET. The project builds upon this basis, focusing on improving access to
quality and relevant training. The project
will both contribute to improving the enabling framework for (private) training
provision through systems development and capacity building, and finance the provision of
quality and relevant training.

The project’s first component aims
to provide competency-based and demand-responsive training in selected priority occupations
and public vocational training centers (VTCs) that will equip graduates with the technical and
catalytic skills to lead productive working lives. The capacity and tools to be developed under
this component will allow scaling-up of the developed approaches to additional public and
private training providers, occupations, and training levels.

The project’s component 2, which is relatively small in terms of
financing requirements, supports overall TVET system strengthening through establishing a
monitoring and evaluation system as well as through developing capacity for research and
development within the WDA.

Component 3 aims to support rapid skills delivery through a competitive
sub-grant mechanism, which will finance the delivery of demand-responsive, short-term training
by a variety of both private and public providers.

This component seeks to strengthen the TVET system through interventions aimed at
improving quality and relevance of training delivery in eight selected priority occupations and
seven selected vocational training centers. It seeks to strengthen the capacity of the TVET
system to align training offerings with employer demands for technical and catalytic skills, raise
the quality of service delivery and increase the employability and performance of training
graduates. The specific objectives of this component are therefore (1) to pilot the delivery of
eight new curricula at vocational level in seven public vocational training centers, including
industrial attachments; (2) to demonstrate the value of an integrated set of interventions to raise
the quality of skills delivery; and (3) to raise the capacity of WDA and training providers to
develop and deliver high quality skills training in priority areas.

The specific objectives of this component are therefore (1) to pilot the delivery of
eight new curricula at vocational level in seven public vocational training centers, including
industrial attachments; (2) to demonstrate the value of an integrated set of interventions to raise
the quality of skills delivery; and (3) to raise the capacity of WDA and training providers to
develop and deliver high quality skills training in priority areas. The objectives and activities
under this component are fully aligned with the WDA’s mandate and Action Plan

35. This component will (a) monitor and evaluate training delivery, in particular the
implementation and performance of the SDP; (b) inform strategic policy decisions and policy

In the area of M&E, this component will ensure that an appropriate M&E system is
developed and rolled-out to (a) assess performance of the SDP, collecting and processing M&E

and tracer studies; and (b) inform adjustments in project activities to improve project

Component 3- Rapid Skills Delivery (Total project costs20: US$ 7.6 million)
39. The key objective of this component is to reduce skills gaps by rapidly increasing the
supply of skills in high demand in the labor market. The instrument which will be used to
achieve this objective is a Skills Development Facility (SDF) that will provide sub-grants to
eligible applicants on a competitive basis for the purpose of raising the quality and volume of
their training in demonstrated areas of skills shortages.
the Skills Development Project

The project targets medium-term systems building through the first two components, and
rapid skills delivery through the third component. Further focus is achieved, among others,
by selectively targeting economic sectors and vocational occupations in which skills are in
short supply in component 1.

Proper training requires a careful sequencing and combination of activities – from
analysis of demands to preparation of training content, training of trainers and institutional
managers, provision of appropriate facilities and equipment, development of testing and
assessment, placement of trainees in internships, management support to training centers, and
follow-up evaluation. The project integrates all these steps for a limited number of occupations.

The project supports various studies which will identify resource generation
options and alternative financing mechanisms to help establish the TVET subsector on a
sustainable basis.

The information role of government will be strengthened under the
project through the monitoring and evaluation component that will generate information on skills demands. Monitoring and evaluation will be strengthened to better inform policy formation and
decision making.

Indicators will be tracked through an M&E system set up under the project (Component
2) and housed in the WDA. It will gradually be expanded to cover the entire TVET system. As
the government attaches great importance to rigorous performance monitoring, the development
of an M&E system is an essential part of the project, and activities to establish the system, train
staff, and collect and analyze data are included in component 2 of the project.

Component 1- Delivery of Quality and Relevant Vocational Training (Total project cost US$
23.6 million)
Objective and approach
1. This component will strengthen the TVET system through interventions aimed at
improving quality and relevance of training delivery in selected priority occupations and selected
pilot vocational training centers. It seeks to strengthen the capacity of the TVET system to align
training offerings with employer demands for skills, raise the quality of service delivery and
increase the employability and performance of training graduates. The specific objectives of this
component are therefore (1) to pilot the delivery of 8 new curricula at vocational level in 7 public
training centers, including industrial attachments; (2) to demonstrate the value of an integrated
set of interventions to raise the quality of skills delivery; and (3) to raise the capacity of WDA
and training providers to develop and deliver high quality skills training in priority areas.

The component will finance comprehensive interventions in a select number of sectors
(hospitality and tourism, and construction) and occupations. Rather than across the board
interventions on a specific aspect of training provision (such as curriculum development for a
larger number of occupations) this approach will result in improved training provision in the
selected occupations for which crucial skills gaps exist on the labor market, and in increased
public and private sector capacity in all essential elements of TVET reform which will be used to
achieve further quality improvements in other training levels, occupations and training centers.
The selected occupations will be ‘showcases’ to act as an example - and catalyst - for reforms in
other training levels, occupations and training centers.

The Training of Trainers
(ToT) program for the targeted occupations is expected to take place from two ToT-centers
which will be rehabilitated and equipped outside of the scope of the project.

activities related to curricula development and roll-out in this component
will use the curricula which are being developed, prior to the start of the project, with the
technical and financial support of VVOB & APEFE and KOICA

For each of the eight occupations, interventions within the following six building blocks
will be developed and offered to the seven identified VTCs which are supported by this
component:
 Curriculum Development: Competency-based, Demand-Responsive (CB-DR) curricula design; classroom implementation and subsequent revision of curricula; design, testing and validation of instructional and teaching materials; and development of equipment, material and consumable standards and requirements to deliver the CB-DR curricula effectively.
 Assessment: Design, test, establish and carry-out summative assessments of trainees to ensure they meet industry standards.
 Infrastructure, Equipment, and Materials: Infrastructure construction and rehabilitation; preparation of specifications of training equipment and consumables; preparation of bidding documents; rehabilitation of infrastructure; delivery and installation of equipment; and provision of materials and consumables in targeted vocational training centers.
 Training of Master Trainers and Instructors: Analysis of staffing requirements; design and delivery of instructors’ training; delivery of training to upgrade new or existing trainers; and provision of professional development for instructors.
 Industrial Attachment Program: Introduce programs to promote trainees’ hands-on experience and exposure to a relevant work environment as a means to enhance their employability; develop guidelines and operational procedures to ensure there is an effective match between the needs of employers and trainees; develop supporting material such as guides and log-books to standardize and keep track of trainees’ attachment progress and performance; develop induction program for trainees and employers; and deliver training modules to master trainers, instructors and institution-based industry supervisors.
 School-based Management & Development: Provision of management and leadership training programs and tools to strengthen the capacity of administrators and instructors to improve general management practices and to adopt and support the implementation of new training methodologies and service delivery approaches; provision of technical support to develop tools to coordinate with local industry to establish partnerships; and strengthen institution-based monitoring and evaluation mechanisms to routinely evaluate performance.

Building block 1: Curriculum Development
9. Objectives: (a) Design and roll-out new Competency-Based, Demand-Responsive
curricula in the Construction as well as the Hospitality & Tourism Sectors; (b) align new CB-DR
training offerings to industry needs; and (c) attain a full cycle of on-the-job training of
facilitators, didactic specialists, pedagogy and entrepreneurial specialists in new CB-DR
curriculum development.

The following activities will be carried out and financed under
this building block: (a) validation of experimental curricula; (b) development of validated
curricula; (c) implementation of the curricula in 7 VTCs; (d) evaluation of curricula; (e)
adjustment of curricula based on evaluation results.

Various activities are essential for the implementation of the actions in this building
block which are implemented through or closely linked to other building blocks, including: (a)
establishment of Standard Training Equipment Lists (STEL) (Infrastructure, Furniture,
Equipment & Materials); (b) establishment of Training Workshop Specifications (TWS)
(Infrastructure, Furniture, Equipment & Materials); (c) specification of entry requirements for
trainers (Training of Trainers); (d) provision of orientation workshops to explain use of the
experimental curricula (Training of Trainers); (e) establishment of national assessment and certification arrangements that are guided by the NQF (Assessment); (f) identifying training
priorities and new program offerings (Research and Analysis activities in component 2).

This building block is expected to implement four cycles of new CB-
DR curricula for 8 occupations in 7 training centers, as well as make adjustments as necessary.
The following results are to be achieved by the end of the project: (a) Finalized full course
structures for all occupations (e.g. competency units, module titles, learning outcomes per
module, credits or notional learning hours -contact & self directed- per module, instructional
methodology, procedure & strategies for assessment of effective training); (b) Completed
Standard Training Equipment Lists (STEL) for every occupation; (c) Completed Training
Workshop Specifications (TWS) for every occupation; (d) Profiles for trainers’ entry
requirements and qualifications; (e) Arrangements for national assessment and certification that
are guided by the NQF; and (f) Validated curricula and instructional/teaching material available
on the WDA website for free downloading by all TVET providers.

Building Block 2: Assessment
13. Objective: The objective of this building block is to put in place and implement an
assessment system for determining the extent to which vocational trainees have acquired the
specified competencies in eight occupations in the selected 7 VTCs.

Technical Description: This building block focuses on the CBT process assessment and
final assessment of the trainees in all the project VTCs and occupations. Activities include: (a)
developing a standard mechanism to develop assessments for TVET occupations; (b) developing
assessments for the eight targeted occupations; (c) training assessment staff on the system; (d)
conducting actual assessment by administering the exams; grading exams and analysis of the
results; (e) providing feedback on results to WDA and training providers; and (f) issuing of
certificates to graduates.

Building Block 3: Infrastructure, Furniture, Equipment, and materials
16. Objective: To ensure that each pilot training center has rehabilitated buildings,
appropriate furniture, equipment and consumable materials and supplies for each of the
occupations.

The project's design, particularly of components 1 and 2, is fully aligned with the Government's strategy
and objectives related to TVET, as translated in the WDA's strategy and action plan. As such, the
project's interventions will provide technical assistance and financing to those aspects of the WDA's
plans for which insufficient public resources or development partner support is expected to be available
during the project implementation period.

Component 2 - TVET System Strengthening (Total project costs: US$ 3.3 million)
Objective and approach:
28. This component will (a) Monitor and evaluate training delivery, in particular the
implementation and performance of the SDP; (b) Inform strategic policy decisions and policy
reforms by providing sound analyses concerning key aspects of the training system; and (c) build
capacity for monitoring and evaluation and for conducting relevant research within the WDA
and TVET providers.

In the
area of M&E, this component will ensure that an appropriate M&E system is developed and
rolled-out to (a) assess performance of the SDP; and (b) inform adjustments in project activities to improve project performance. The system that is set up for this purpose is intended to form
the nucleus of the M&E system of the overall TVET system.

Activities related to research and analyses financed through the project are expected to (i)
allow for evidence-based adjustments in the WDA’s policies and activities; (ii) develop capacity
within the WDA to conduct further research beyond the life of the project; and (iii) generate
awareness of the importance of sound analyses to serve as a basis for strategic and policy
decision making.

The project’s activities related to Monitoring and Evaluation will build upon several
activities that will be completed before project effectiveness, in particular the establishment of
project indicators including their baseline, target values and data collection mechanism. Key
activities following project effectiveness will include: (i) design of data collection mechanism
and installation of M&E software in WDA and targeted VTCs; (ii) design and delivery of
training to relevant staff in the targeted VTCs and various WDA units on the collection and
submission of M&E data; (iii) regular (at least annual) collection and submission of data by
‘primary sources of data’ to WDA M&E staff; (iv) processing of and reporting on collected data
to WDA management; (v) presentation of final report to WDA and other stakeholders. As part of
this component, the WDA will commission a thorough analysis of the performance of the Skills
Development Facility for discussion during the project’s midterm review. In addition, an
additional review will be conducted to assess the efficacy of the SDF’s governance structure, and
to provide information on potential future mechanisms to provide financing to training providers,
and their potential sources of financing outside of the scope of the project.

After a 5-year implementation period, the following outputs are expected to have been
achieved by the project:
1. A system in place to monitor and evaluate project performance, producing timely and
accurate information;
2. Completed evaluation of SDF performance on its internal and external efficiency as a
mechanism for rapid skills delivery - including recommendations related to the SDF’s
priorities.
3. The M&E system has been expanded moving toward covering the performance of the
TVET system as a whole;
4. Two reports identifying critical skills gaps that form particular bottlenecks to economic
growth completed and discussed with relevant stakeholders;
5. Reports on (a) current funding sources and allocation mechanisms; (b) options for
increased mobilization of (non-public) resources for TVET; (c) resource allocation
mechanisms that could provide incentives to raise performance by training providers
completed and discussed with relevant stakeholders.

The objective of this component is to minimize skills gaps by rapidly increasing the
supply of skills in high demand in the labor market. To achieve this objective, this component
aims to expand the number of individuals with the relevant skills in critical sectors and improve
the quality of individual’s skill sets in key occupations and sectors.

The instrument which will be used for rapid skills delivery is a Skills Development
Facility (SDF) which will provide sub-grants to eligible applicants on a competitive basis. Sub-
grant beneficiaries will be able to raise the quality and volume of their training in demonstrated
areas of skills shortages. Thereby, the component will contribute to the overall project
development objective by providing individual and enterprise beneficiaries with market-relevant
skills, greater productivity and improved economic opportunities and incomes. The competitive
sub-grant mechanism allows, among other things: (1) a relatively quick expansion of the skills
base; (2) a flexible response to changing priority skills needs; (3) the strengthening of training
provision and the stimulation of grass-roots innovation through competition; (4) the involvement
of employers in allocation decisions; (5) the promotion of effectiveness through performance-
based contracts; and (6) improvements in efficiency through competition.

The SDF is designed as a 5-year intervention to test whether the sub-grant facility
mechanism can be an effective instrument to rapidly reduce skills gaps and promote
employment. A thorough review of the mechanism will be carried out towards the third year of
implementation, prior to mid-term review, to evaluate its internal and external efficiency and
help determine whether to redefine priorities or implementation arrangements. In the fourth year
of implementation, an additional review will be carried out to assess the efficacy of the SDF’s
governance structure, and to provide information on potential future mechanisms to provide
financing to training providers, and their potential sources of financing outside of the scope of
the project.

The primary rationale for project as a whole is economic—closing critical skill gaps in
the labor market, to provide the skills necessary for productive employment. Equity is a
secondary objective, which will be encouraged through the criteria for evaluation of sub-project
proposals.

The project will be implemented by the Workforce Development Authority (WDA)
which is a semi-autonomous institution under the Ministry of Education (MINEDUC).

Component 1 will be carried out by the relevant units in the WDA, notably the TVET
Training Unit (Curriculum Development, Examination and Certification, and Training of
Trainers), as well as the TVET Accreditation and Certification Unit (IAP), and the TVET
School Development Unit (infrastructure and equipment). The DDG Training is expected
to ensure coordination and integration of the various building blocks and play a leading
role related to the school-based management and development activities. At the VTC
level, management of the VTCs will play key roles in the implementation of this
component.

VTCs will be responsible for the implementation of component 1 on the school level, i.e. ensuring the successful
implementation of the new demand-responsive CBT program in the targeted occupations.

VTCs will be responsible for the implementation of component 1 (and, to a lesser
extent, component 2 related to M&E) on the school level, i.e. ensuring the successful
implementation of the new demand-responsive CBT program in the targeted occupations.

The development of an M&E system is an essential part of the project, and
activities to set up the system, train staff, and data collection and analysis have been
included in the project. In addition to the M&E person in the PMPU, a specialist will be
recruited to design the M&E system for the project, but which can be enlarged as the
scope of work of WDA expands over time.

reform_title: Establishment of a TVET development partner cluster group
reform_description:
The WDA has established a TVET development partner cluster
group, which meets regularly and is ensuring that development partners’ activities are
increasingly aligned and integrated with the GoR’s policies and action plans related to
TVET, and with each other’s activities.

The project builds upon, and will be supported
by, various activities which the WDA is implementing with the support of other
development partners. For example, for component 1, curricula will be used that are
developed with the support of, among others, the Belgian Technical Cooperation (BTC)
and the Association for the Promotion of Education and Training Abroad (APEFE);
training of trainers (construction sector) will be provided in a centre which will be
equipped by KOICA, and the school-based management support package that will be provided to the VTCs is expected to be developed by the Flemish Association for
Development Cooperation and Technical Assistance (VVOB). The design and
implementation of component 3 benefits from experiences gained from the ‘Upgrade
Your Skills’ program, a small scale competitive-grant mechanism which is supported by
the German Association for International Cooperation (GIZ, formerly GTZ).

The project has been designed out of a larger TVET strategy put in place by government, and
which will be implemented by the Workforce Development Agency (WDA).

The project has been designed out of a larger TVET strategy put in place by government, and
which will be implemented by the Workforce Development Agency (WDA). Multiple development
partners (DPs) are implicated in the development and implementation of the TVET strategy, and
assisting the WDA to meet the objectives defined in the government’s strategy.

The GoR has already adopted an extensive and forward looking framework for TVET consisting

Component 3 of the project is expected to provide access to training
to the unskilled, many of whom are active in the agricultural sector.

The project incorporates capacity building and implementation support to the
WDA, as well as school-based management support to the VTCs (component 1) and support to potential and confirmed sub-grant beneficiaries (component 3). Under component 1, particularly the building
blocks targeting industrial attachment programs and school-based management support will include
capacity building and training aspects aimed at strengthening private sector involvement.

the economic and financial justification of the project design relies on the WDA’s use of
capacity and systems developed through the project to scale up competency based and demand driven
training outside of the project through (i) expanding the programs developed under the project to
additional VTCs; and (ii) expanding the levels and number of occupations for which competency based

function together co-operatively; and, since South Africa has no tradition of
municipal responsibility for education, it provides that the national sphere
has exclusive legislative responsibility for tertiary education and shares

reform_title: The National Education Policy Act of 1996

system for the organisation, governance and funding of schools. It seeks to
ensure that all learners have right of access to quality education without
discrimination, and it makes schooling compulsory for all children from the
year they turn 7 to the year in which they turn 15 (or the end of grade 9,
whichever comes first). It regulates the provision of public schools and
education places by provinces, the governance of schools (in particular the
establishment and operation of school governing bodies), the funding of
schools (including state responsibilities, school budgets, fees and the
framework for funding rules or norms) and the establishment and funding of

proper exercise of the rights of learners to education and the redress of past inequalities in educational provision” (Republic of South Africa, 1996c,
Section 34.1).

One of the most important powers devolved upon School Governing
Bodies by the South African Schools Act (RSA, 1996) is the right to charge
school fees, subject to majority parental approval.
