National legislation establishing the governance framework for the education system through a concurrent mode of governance between the national Department of Education and nine provincial departments of education. It established the Minister of Education's authority to set national norms and standards for education planning, provision, governance, monitoring and evaluation.

National legislation establishing a uniform system for the organisation, governance and funding of schools. It made schooling compulsory for children aged 7 to 15, established democratically elected school governing bodies with significant devolved powers including setting school fees and recommending staff appointments, created a framework for school fees and funding norms, required public schools to admit all learners without discrimination, and mandated provision of relevant support services for learners with special educational needs.

An intergovernmental financing mechanism established under the 1996 Constitution whereby a portion of nationally raised revenues is distributed to provinces based on demographically-driven formulae to ensure equitable per-learner spending across provinces regardless of provincial wealth. Phased in from 1996 to 2000.

A national policy requiring school governing bodies to establish criteria for school fee exemptions and obliging them to inform parents of their right to apply. Regulations set a mandatory minimum means test and provide automatic exemptions for orphans, foster children and social grant recipients.

The government undertook a major structural reform in 1996–97 equalising teacher salaries and learner-teacher ratios across all schools, aiming for ratios of 40:1 in primary and 35:1 in secondary schools.

reform_title: National Norms and Standards for School Funding – Progressive Allocation
reform_description: National policy requiring provincial education departments to rank all schools from poorest to least poor and allocate non-personnel recurrent funding progressively, with 35% of available funds earmarked for the poorest 20% of schools.

Establishment of school governing bodies under the South African Schools Act composed of principals, parents, educators, non-teaching staff and learners with powers to determine admissions policy, recommend staff appointments, develop school budgets and charge school fees.

reform_title: National Programme of Action for Children
reform_description: The government established a national programme framework giving priority to nutrition, child health, water and sanitation, early childhood development and basic education, with leadership roles assigned to the Departments of Social Development and Education.

National legislation establishing the legal foundation and framework for a single nationally co-ordinated system of higher education post-Apartheid. It defined institutional autonomy within public accountability requirements, created the Council on Higher Education, and required higher education institutions to increase access for learners with special educational needs.

reform_title: Education White Paper 3: Programme for the Transformation of Higher Education
reform_description: A national higher education policy issued in July 1997 setting out a plan to transform higher education through a programme-based system planned, funded, and governed as a single co-ordinated system. It established principles of equity, quality, development, democratisation, academic freedom, institutional autonomy, effectiveness, efficiency, and public accountability, and introduced planned expansion rather than massification.

National curriculum reform launched in March 1997 and implemented from 1998, introducing an outcomes-based curriculum identifying 8 learning areas with 66 learning outcomes, emphasising integrated active learning and the teacher as facilitator, replacing subjects with integrated learning areas aligned with constitutional values.

reform_title: Closure of Teacher Training Colleges and Transfer to Higher Education
reform_description: The government decided in the late 1990s to close approximately 120 teacher training colleges in the Bantustans and relocate teacher education within the higher education sector in universities and technikons. All colleges of teacher education were formally incorporated into universities and technikons in 2001, reducing the number of public educator education institutions from 150 in 1994 to 82 by 2000.
National legislation establishing the post-Apartheid FET system and regulating all learning and training programmes leading to qualifications at NQF levels 2 to 4.
National legislation governing the terms and conditions of employment for public school educators employed by provincial education departments.

reform_title: Skills Development Act and National Skills Development Strategy 2001–2005
reform_description: National legislation establishing the National Skills Fund and the first National Skills Development Strategy for 2001–2005, administered by the national Ministry of Labour through a statutory National Skills Authority and Sector Education and Training Authorities covering all sectors of the economy. Firms are required to pay a 1% skills development levy.

reform_title: National Norms and Standards for School Funding
reform_description: National policy requiring provincial education departments to rank all schools from poorest to least poor and allocate non-personnel recurrent funding progressively in favour of poorer schools using a 35-25-20-15-5 distribution across quintiles, with 35% of available funds earmarked for the poorest 20% of schools.

National admissions policy issued in 1998 setting norms for learners to proceed through school with their age cohort, allowing a learner to repeat only once per phase while prohibiting automatic promotion.

The Council on Higher Education was established under the Higher Education Act 101 of 1997 and appointed in June 1998 as an independent statutory body with advisory and executive roles. It advises the Minister of Education on higher education transformation and has executive responsibility for quality assurance through the Higher Education Quality Committee (HEQC).

reform_title: Adult Basic Education and Training Act
reform_description: National legislation providing for the establishment, governance and funding of public and private adult learning centres, registration of private centres, and quality assurance mechanisms for adult basic education and training.

reform_title: South African Council for Educators Act
reform_description: National legislation establishing the South African Council for Educators (SACE) as a national statutory body responsible for the professional registration and development of educators, the setting, maintenance and protection of ethical and professional standards, and the authority to deregister teachers for professional misconduct.

National policy establishing a competence-based framework with seven educator roles and 132 specific competences linked to the National Qualifications Framework, governing teacher education qualifications and the professional standards and roles expected of educators.

The government established 25 Sector Education and Training Authorities to regulate qualifications and training for employees and job seekers within their respective sectors, funded through a mandatory skills development levy.

reform_title: General and Further Education and Training Quality Assurance Act
reform_description: National legislation establishing Umalusi as the statutory body responsible for quality assurance and certification for all non-higher education in the general and further education and training bands of the National Qualifications Framework.

reform_title: Education White Paper 5 on Early Childhood Development
reform_description: Government white paper establishing policy for early childhood development aiming to achieve full participation of five-year-olds in Grade R education by 2010, with 85% of provision in public primary schools and 15% through community sites.

reform_title: Education White Paper 6 on Inclusive Education
reform_description: Government white paper requiring the education and training system to be inclusive, establishing a framework distinguishing mainstream schools, full-service schools and special schools, planning conversion of special schools into resource centres over a 20-year period, and giving educational inclusion a broader meaning than simply access to schooling for persons with disabilities.

Legislative amendment giving departments of education more power to intervene when a governing body fails to fulfil its duties, increasing departmental responsibility to build SGB capacity, and placing greater financial accountability on SGBs.

The HEQC was established in 2001 as a permanent subcommittee of the Council on Higher Education with responsibility for programme accreditation and re-accreditation, institutional audits, and the development and promotion of quality in higher education.

A national plan for higher education published in February 2001 operationalising the White Paper 3 policy framework. It established enrolment targets, set a participation rate goal of 20% by 2010–15, introduced a three-year rolling institutional planning process, and addressed the size and shape of the higher education system through planned expansion linked to national development goals.

reform_title: National Integrated Plan on Early Childhood Development
reform_description: Cabinet-approved national plan for early childhood development focusing on learners before they enter the school system, implemented through an interdepartmental committee comprising officials from education, social development and health.

The government announced and implemented a progressive rollout of Grade R reception year programmes across public primary schools supported by a subsidy mechanism and grants-in-aid to community-based ECD centres, aiming to achieve full participation of five-year-olds by 2010.

A government initiative designating 500 schools as centres of excellence in mathematics, science and technology, primarily in rural and township areas, aimed at raising participation and performance of historically disadvantaged learners in these subjects. Expanded from 102 schools in 2001 to 500 schools by 2008.

reform_title: Human Resource Development Strategy for South Africa
reform_description: Joint national strategy under the responsibility of the ministers of education and labour addressing skills development across the economy, first published in 2001.

reform_title: Human Resource Development Strategy for South Africa
reform_description: Joint national strategy under the responsibility of the ministers of education and labour addressing skills development across the economy, first published in 2001.
Legislative amendment specifying that appointment of first-time educators and educators re-entering the system can be made after consulting the SGB but without requiring an SGB recommendation.

A revised national curriculum adopted in 2002 replacing Curriculum 2005, written in plainer language with greater emphasis on basic skills, content knowledge and logical grade progression. Structured into eight learning areas for grades R–9 and 29 subjects for grades 10–12 with learning outcomes and assessment standards specified for each grade, phased in gradually across grades culminating in grade 12 in 2008.

In 2002 SACE launched a three-year pilot project compiling guidelines for professional development portfolios aimed at encouraging teachers to take responsibility for their own professional development and engage in reflection on their practice.

A national teacher performance evaluation system introduced in 2003 linking whole-school evaluation and self-appraisal to salary progression. Teachers are categorised as good, satisfactory, or unsatisfactory, with salary notch advancement tied to evaluation outcomes. Used for salary progression assessments and accelerated grade progression, with future application to performance rewards and incentives.

reform_title: New Funding Framework (NFF) for Higher Education
reform_description: A new goal-oriented and performance-related funding framework for public higher education published in December 2003 and implemented from the 2004/05 fiscal year. It replaced the SAPSE formula system and linked government grants to national policy goals, approved institutional enrolment plans, and graduate outputs rather than student demand alone.

The government finalised and adopted the Broad-Based Black Economic Empowerment framework to encourage previously disadvantaged South Africans to participate in the formal economy, with sectoral charters and scorecards including skills development targets.

Government-led restructuring of the higher education landscape through a programme of institutional mergers and incorporations beginning around 2002–2004, reducing 36 racially segregated public higher education institutions to 23–24 institutions comprising traditional universities, universities of technology, comprehensive universities, and two National Institutes of Higher Education.

reform_title: Education Laws Amendment Act 2005 – No-Fee Schools Policy
reform_description: National legislation declaring all schools in the two poorest quintiles (covering 40% of learners nationally) as no-fee schools. No-fee schools receive a minimum state funding allocation per learner and school governing bodies in these schools are prohibited from charging fees.

The government adopted a National Protocol for Assessment establishing an institutional framework for the recording, reporting and grading of learner achievement of learning outcomes across the General Education and Training band for grades R to 12.

In mid-2005 the Department of Education established a National Management School Leadership Committee to devise and pilot a school leadership programme. The accredited two-year part-time programme involves 12 modules yielding 120 credits at level 6 of the National Qualifications Framework, delivered by universities with provincial departments selecting participants and awarding bursaries.

The government allocated a conditional grant of approximately ZAR 1.9–2 billion for the period 2005/06 to 2008/09 to provinces for upgrading workshops, classrooms and retraining staff across the 50 FET colleges.

The Department of Education adopted a national strategy with an implementation plan for 2005–2009 to improve mathematics, science and technology education and address teacher shortages in these subjects.

The government converted 28 secondary schools serving historically marginalised communities into focus schools in the fields of arts and culture, business, commerce and management, and engineering and technology, with ZAR 82 million allocated for 2005 to March 2007.

The government adopted a second National Skills Development Strategy covering 2005 to 2010 with a ZAR 21.9 billion investment target aimed at halving unemployment and poverty, setting quantitative training targets including 700,000 workers achieving ABET level 4, and identifying scarce or critical skills for training through special programmes, internships, learnerships and apprenticeships.

reform_title: Further Education and Training Colleges Act
reform_description: National legislation superseding the FET Act of 1998, establishing a provincial FET colleges system with 50 multi-campus institutions created through merging 152 smaller colleges, mandating provision of intermediate and high-level skills training, and providing for regulation and quality assurance of private FET provision.

reform_title: Education Laws Amendment Act 2006 – National Quintiles
reform_description: Amendment replacing provincial quintiles with national quintiles for school funding purposes so that the two poorest quintiles reflect actual social conditions across provinces rather than representing a fixed 40% in every province.
The government introduced nationally set examinations for Grades 10 and 11 to monitor implementation of new curricula and provide data for adjusting standards.

reform_title: National Policy on Assessment and Qualifications for Schools in the GET Band
reform_description: National policy establishing assessment and qualification standards for schools in the General Education and Training band, including continuous assessment requirements, progression rules, subject assessment guidelines, and the Common Task for Assessment at grade 9 moderated by Umalusi.

reform_title: National Policy Framework for Teacher Education and Development
reform_description: The Department of Education issued a national policy framework in 2006 governing teacher education and development in South Africa, addressing teacher recruitment campaigns, bursary schemes for initial teacher education students, modes of delivery for the B.Ed. degree, continuing professional teacher development managed by SACE, and the planned establishment of a national electronic database on teacher supply and demand.

A national survey and management system established to document and track the state of infrastructure at every school, updating the Schools Register of Needs, with accompanying upgrading plans and budgets to ensure all schools are supplied with water, electricity, sanitation, science laboratories, libraries, sports fields and ICT access.

reform_title: Guidelines for Early Childhood Development Services
reform_description: The Department of Social Development with UNICEF support published national guidelines for early childhood development services establishing a framework for ECD provision, quality and practitioner development.

reform_title: Level 4 Unit Standards for ECD Practitioner Training
reform_description: New Level 4 unit standards for ECD practitioner training were registered by SAQA, leading to a Further Education and Training Certificate as an entry-level qualification for early childhood development practitioners.

reform_title: Ministerial Committee on Literacy and Kha Ri Gude Mass Literacy Campaign
reform_description: The government established a Ministerial Committee on Literacy in 2006 to plan a mass literacy campaign and subsequently adopted an implementation plan with a ZAR 6.1 billion budget targeting all illiterate adults. The Kha Ri Gude campaign, released in May 2007, targets 4.7 million illiterates over 2007–2012, training master trainers to provide basic literacy classes to 300, 000 adults and youth in 2008 with initial funding of ZAR 850 million.

reform_title: Education Laws Amendment Act 2007 – Principal Accountability
reform_description: Amendment placing greater accountability for learner outcomes on principals of public schools, requiring them to prepare annual reports on academic performance relative to minimum standards and annual plans for improving learner outcomes.

Government policy declaring the poorest two quintiles of schools as no-fee schools, covering 40% of schools nationally, exempting learners from paying school fees to improve access for indigent learners.

National bursary scheme approved by government in 2007 specifically for students studying to become teachers, benefiting approximately 3500 student teachers per annum. Recipients sign contracts committing them to teach in South Africa for a number of years, aimed at addressing the shortage of educator graduates.

A substantially altered national teacher salary structure introduced in 2007 providing more generous entry-level salaries and resolving the flat pay plateau by allowing varying percentage increases across all salary levels through a 12-level framework with a 16-notch progression structure.

A salary package and draft collective agreement developed by the ELRC in October 2007 establishing a merit-based salary progression system for educators with three career streams (general classroom, specialist education, and supervisory/management) and new specialist teacher categories. Salary increases are tied to performance, qualifications, competencies, and experience evaluated on three-year cycles.

reform_title: Educator Continuous Professional Development and Re-licensing System
reform_description: System instituted requiring educators to earn professional development points over a stipulated period by completing approved courses and modules overseen by the South African Council of Educators to maintain their professional accreditation.

reform_title: Further Education and Training Curriculum and National Certificate Vocational
reform_description: The government implemented a new FET curriculum leading to the National Certificate Vocational, delivered through FET colleges with examinations beginning in 2008, covering eleven vocational programme areas.

A national campaign launched by the Minister of Education through Government Notice No. 306 requiring all primary schools to increase average learner performance in literacy and numeracy to no less than 50% by 2011, with detailed directives on time allocations, minimum teaching expectations, quarterly national standardised testing, and teacher development as cornerstones.

reform_title: Quality Improvement Development Support and Upliftment Programme (QIDS-UP)
reform_description: National programme providing educator and district development support to 5000 low-performing primary schools to improve children's learning especially in literacy and numeracy.

reform_title: National Student Financial Aid Scheme for FET Colleges
reform_description: Extension of the National Student Financial Aid Scheme to administer bursaries for poor students in FET colleges to offset course fees.

Proposed revision of the NQF extending qualification levels from eight to ten and clarifying the roles of quality assurance councils for general and further education and training, higher education, and trades and occupations, with SAQA redefined as a juristic person.

Introduction of a new Grade 12 National Senior Certificate as a 130-credit qualification at Level 4 of the National Qualifications Framework, replacing the old Matric and eliminating the distinction between Higher Grade and Standard Grade. Learners are required to take seven subjects including two official languages, mathematics or mathematical literacy. and life orientation.
The Department of Education published assessment guidelines for Grades R through 3 introducing continuous assessment with a four-point reporting code system.

reform_title: Strategy on Screening, Identification, Assessment and Support (SIAS)
reform_description: The Department of Education developed and began testing a national inter-sectorial Strategy on Screening, Identification, Assessment and Support to ensure rigorous and consistent screening and assessment of learners with special educational needs across the system.

reform_title: New Norms and Standards for Inclusive Education Funding
reform_description: The Department of Education approved a new framework for norms and standards for inclusive education funding, shifting the basis for funding full-service schools and special schools from head-count to level of support needed by learners.
An act establishing the overarching policy and legislative framework for the national education system to advance democratic transformation and combat discrimination.
A radical national curriculum reform introduced to standardise education across all schools in South Africa.

reform_title: South African Council for Educators (SACE) Act
reform_description: Legislation establishing the South African Council for Educators to manage the registration, professional development, and ethical standards of teachers.
A national policy establishing standardized norms and expectations for educator qualifications and practices.

reform_title: Education White Paper 5 on Early Childhood Education
reform_description: A national policy framework directed at expanding access and meeting the developmental needs of early childhood education.
A revision of the national curriculum that emphasized basic skills, content knowledge, logical grade progression, and constitutional values such as human rights and multilingualism.
A national multi-year campaign (2007–2011) designed to improve foundational learning outcomes and support curriculum implementation.

reform_title: White Paper on Education and Training
reform_description: A national policy framework for transforming post-apartheid education and training toward equity, access, quality, non-racism and non-sexism across the education system.

reform_title: South African Qualifications Authority Act and National Qualifications Framework
reform_description: Legislation establishing SAQA and the National Qualifications Framework to integrate education and training qualifications across levels and define outcomes-based standards.
A national higher education financing mechanism providing state-supported financial aid allocations to higher education institutions for students.
National legislation establishing the post-apartheid governance framework for education, with national policy responsibility and provincial implementation responsibilities.
National school legislation creating a unified public schooling system, devolving powers to elected school governing bodies and regulating school governance, admissions and fees.
A national curriculum reform introducing outcomes-based education, integrated learning areas and learner-centred pedagogy in schools.
National legislation for transforming higher education governance and establishing a single coordinated higher education system after apartheid.

reform_title: Education White Paper 3 on Higher Education
reform_description: A national higher education transformation policy setting goals for a planned, coordinated, equitable and development-oriented higher education system.
National legislation restructuring further education and training and providing the legal framework for public FET colleges and post-compulsory vocational provision.

Legislation establishing a national skills development system, including sector education and training authorities, the National Skills Fund and mechanisms to finance and coordinate workplace training.

reform_title: National Norms and Standards for School Funding
reform_description: A national school-financing reform allocating non-personnel and non-capital funds progressively toward poorer schools using poverty-based targeting.

reform_title: Adult Basic Education and Training Act
reform_description: National legislation establishing the legal framework for adult basic education and training provision and institutions.

reform_title: South African Council for Educators Act
reform_description: Legislation establishing the South African Council for Educators as a professional body for teacher registration and professional oversight.
A national teacher policy defining educator roles, competences and professional expectations for the teaching workforce.

reform_title: Education White Paper 5 on Early Childhood Development
reform_description: A national early childhood education policy expanding state responsibility for ECD and prioritising the development of Grade R and early learning provision.

reform_title: Education White Paper 6 on Inclusive Education
reform_description: A national inclusive education policy establishing an integrated system of mainstream schools, full-service schools, special schools and resource centres for learners requiring different levels of support.
A national higher education plan steering enrolments, institutional restructuring, equity, access and programme coordination across public higher education.

reform_title: Incorporation of Teacher Education Colleges into Higher Education
reform_description: A restructuring reform closing about 120 colleges of education and relocating teacher education into universities and technikons.

reform_title: Higher Education Institutional Mergers and Incorporations
reform_description: A national restructuring of higher education institutions through mergers and incorporations, reducing universities and technikons from 36 to 23 and creating new institutional types including comprehensive universities and universities of technology.

reform_title: Revised National Curriculum Statement Grades R-9
reform_description: A revised national curriculum for Grades R-9 that simplified Curriculum 2005 and placed greater emphasis on basic skills, content knowledge and grade progression.

reform_title: National Strategy for Mathematics Science and Technology Education
reform_description: A national strategy supporting mathematics, science and technology education, including Dinaledi centres of excellence and targeted implementation from 2005 to 2009.
A national teacher quality-management system used for educator assessment, salary progression, accelerated grade progression and future performance incentives.
A national senior secondary curriculum reform specifying subjects, learning programmes and assessment guidance for Grades 10-12.
A national qualification at Level 4 of the NQF replacing the old senior certificate structure and defining subject requirements for school-leaving certification.
A national skills strategy using skills levy income to support training, ABET completion, learnerships, internships, bursaries and scarce-skills development.
A national investment programme upgrading the 50 public FET colleges through recapitalisation funding and institutional strengthening.

reform_title: Updated Further Education and Training Act
reform_description: A legislative update to the FET framework, strengthening governance and restructuring of public further education and training colleges.
A pro-poor school-financing reform declaring the poorest two quintiles of schools no-fee schools and replacing household fee income with state support.
A school-financing reform requiring governing bodies to inform parents of exemption rights and applying a means-test formula for partial or full fee exemptions.

reform_title: National Policy on Assessment and Qualifications for Schools in the GET Band
reform_description: A national policy regulating assessment and qualifications in the General Education and Training band.

A new vocational qualification and curriculum in FET colleges, introduced across eleven economic sectors with compulsory language, mathematics or mathematical literacy, life orientation and vocational subjects.

reform_title: Mass Literacy Strategy and Kha Ri Gudi Campaign
reform_description: A national adult literacy campaign strategy with a dedicated secretariat, tutors, materials and targets to expand basic literacy provision for adults.
A negotiated teacher salary and career-structure reform increasing remuneration and linking wage progression to performance and assessment.

reform_title: National Policy Framework for Teacher Education and Development
reform_description: A national teacher education framework reorganising teacher development around initial professional education and continuing professional teacher development.
A national campaign focused on improving literacy and numeracy in Grades R-6, especially the foundation and intermediate phases.
A government programme providing meals to poor learners during the school day to support school attendance and learning among disadvantaged children.

reform_title: The National Institute for Education Evaluation (Instituto Nacional de Evaluación Educativa, INEE)
reform_description: The National Institute for Education Evaluation (Instituto Nacional de Evaluación Educativa, INEE) was established in 1990 as the central government body responsible for the evaluation of non-university education

reform_title: The 2001 Organic Act on Universities (Ley Orgánica de Universidades)
reform_description: Each public university has its own governing body, following the framework of the 2001 Organic Act on Universities (Ley Orgánica de Universidades).

reform_title: The Programmes for Reinforcement, Guidance and Support (Programas de Refuerzo, Orientación y Apoyo, 2005)
reform_description: Several national plans aim to improve equity in education and social inclusion: the Programmes for Reinforcement, Guidance and Support (Programas de Refuerzo, Orientación y Apoyo, 2005), which provide resources to education institutions to address inequalities and social exclusion.
The Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, 2013), reforms the 2006 Ley Orgánica de Educación (LOE).

reform_title: The Programme to reduce early dropout in education and training (Programa para la reducción del abandono temprano de la educación y la formación, 2008)
reform_description: The Programme to reduce early dropout in education and training (Programa para la reducción del abandono temprano de la educación y la formación, 2008) which provides funding for preventive measures

reform_title: The Programmes for Reinforcement, Guidance and Support (Programas de Refuerzo, Orientación y Apoyo, 2005, modified in 2010)
reform_description: Several national plans aim to improve equity in education and social inclusion: the Programmes for Reinforcement, Guidance and Support (Programas de Refuerzo, Orientación y Apoyo, 2005, modified in 2010), which provide resources to education institutions to address inequalities and social exclusion.

A dual VET system, developed in 2012, combines training with employment in companies. The aim is to provide a professional qualification by harmonising teaching and learning processes between training institutions and workplaces. Basic requirements for the dual system are regulated by the Ministry of Education, Culture and Sport (Ministerio de Educación, Cultura y Deporte), with implementation by regional governments.

reform_title: Annual National Reform Programme (Programa Nacional de Reformas, 2012)
reform_description: To set its priorities, an annual National Reform Programme (Programa Nacional de Reformas, 2012) presents objectives to meet the European Union 2020 strategy.

A measure to respond to the economic crisis (Real Decreto-ley 14/2012) addresses the rational use of resources in education, allowing for an increase in teaching hours per teacher, reviewing class size, adjusting education to demand and reviewing university fees. Some of these measures are temporary, and regional authorities can decide on their application.

reform_title: The Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, 2013),
reform_description: Spain aims to promote education improvement, mainly through a new law, the Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, 2013).  Among its main measures are greater autonomy for schools, new preventive diagnostic testing in primary education, more vocational pathways starting in the final years of lower secondary education, and exit exams in lower and upper secondary education.

reform_title: The Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, 2013),
reform_description: The Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, 2013), aims to increase flexibility in lower secondary education and improve the transition into upper secondary vocational programmes.

reform_title: The National Strategic Plan for Childhood and Adolescence 2013-2016 (Plan Estratégico Nacional de Infancia y Adolescencia)
reform_description: Several national plans aim to improve equity in education and social inclusion: the National Strategic Plan for Childhood and Adolescence 2013-2016 (Plan Estratégico Nacional de Infancia y Adolescencia)

reform_title: The National Action Plan for Social Inclusion 2013-2016 (Plan Nacional de Acción para la Inclusión Social)
reform_description: Several national plans aim to improve equity in education and social inclusion: the National Action Plan for Social Inclusion 2013-2016 (Plan Nacional de Acción para la Inclusión Social)
the National Reform Plan 2013, which lays out objectives to meet the European Union 2020 strategy

reform_title: The Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, 2013)
reform_description: A new reform in process of implementation, the Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, 2013), proposes to introduce greater flexibility in student pathways at age 15 instead of 16, ease the transition into upper secondary vocational education programmes, provide more autonomy to schools and school leaders, and strengthen external student assessments. To be implemented starting in September 2014, the reform is wide-ranging

reform_title: The Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, 2013)
reform_description: The Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, 2013) incorporates optional vocational pathways into the final year of lower secondary education from age 15 that aim to give access to high-level VET

The new education law (LOMCE, 2013, see Spotlight 1) establishes greater autonomy for schools and more decision-making capacities for school leaders in terms of curriculum and schedules, within a national framework.

reform_title: The Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, 2013)
reform_description: The Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, 2013), reforms the 2006 Ley Orgánica de Educación (LOE). One of the objectives is to increase the autonomy of education institutions while supporting greater accountability.

It introduces a new Diploma on Basic VET which lasts two years for students between 15 and 17, ends with a professional certificate and gives access to Intermediate Level VET (ciclos formativos de Formación Profesional).

Under this reform, students in the last year of lower secondary education can choose either general academic courses or more vocationally oriented courses that combine academics with specific training in one or more professional profiles
The new education law aims to give more autonomy to schools and school leaders

reform_title: Modified recruitment process for school leaders
reform_description: Recruitment processes for school leaders have been modified to introduce a course requirement and to give candidates from different schools the same opportunity in selection processes.

reform_title: The Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, 2013)
reform_description: The Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (Ley Orgánica para la Mejora de la Calidad Educativa, LOMCE, see Spotlight 1), introduces a formative and diagnostic internal assessment after primary school, which serves as an early detection system, and external high-stakes student assessments at the end of lower and upper secondary education.

Establishment of the central government body responsible for the evaluation of non-university education reporting annually to the Spanish Parliament using system evaluations including diagnostic evaluations and international assessments.

reform_title: Organic Act on Universities (Ley Orgánica de Universidades)
reform_description: A national law establishing the governance framework for public universities including internal quality assurance systems supervised by regional authorities and ANECA within the European Higher Education Area.

reform_title: National Agency for Quality Assessment and Accreditation (ANECA)
reform_description: Creation of a national agency to improve the quality of tertiary education through evaluation certification and accreditation of higher education institutions.

reform_title: Programmes for Reinforcement Guidance and Support (PROA)
reform_description: A national cooperative programme providing resources to education institutions to address inequalities and social exclusion by supporting students with learning difficulties.

reform_title: Programme to Reduce Early Dropout in Education and Training
reform_description: A national programme providing funding for preventive measures aimed at reducing early school leaving from education and training.

A new national dual vocational education and training system combining training with employment in companies to provide professional qualifications by harmonizing teaching and learning between training institutions and workplaces. Basic requirements regulated by the Ministry of Education Culture and Sport with implementation by regional governments.

reform_title: National Reform Programme (Programa Nacional de Reformas)
reform_description: An annual national programme setting priorities and objectives to meet the European Union 2020 strategy including education targets such as reducing dropout rates.

reform_title: Real Decreto-ley 14/2012 – Rational Use of Resources in Education
reform_description: A national decree addressing rational use of resources in education in response to the economic crisis allowing for increased teaching hours per teacher reviewing class size adjusting education to demand and reviewing university fees with regional authorities deciding on application.

reform_title: Organic Law for the Improvement of Educational Quality (LOMCE)
reform_description: A comprehensive national education law introducing greater flexibility in student pathways at age 15 instead of 16; a new two-year Basic VET diploma for students aged 15-17; greater school autonomy in schedule content and pedagogy; modified school leader selection requiring specialized training; external assessments at the end of each stage of education (diagnostic in primary; high-stakes in lower and upper secondary); and optional vocational pathways in the final year of lower secondary education.

reform_title: National Strategic Plan for Childhood and Adolescence 2013-2016
reform_description: A national plan aimed at improving equity in education and social inclusion for children and adolescents.

reform_title: National Action Plan for Social Inclusion 2013-2016
reform_description: A national plan aimed at improving equity in education and promoting social inclusion.

The 1960 Primary Education Act, as amended in 2019, includes in the statements that primary education is organised in such a way that the use of communication and information technology is promoted in schools. 
The term communication and information technology appears in various regulatory frameworks, including in the Primary Education Act of 1960, as amended in 2019, and in the National Strategic Policy Plan for ICT in Education of 2014. 
The 1960 Primary Education Act, as amended in 2019, mandates that if structural educational disadvantages exist as a result of external factors, the competent authority must develop a policy to eliminate them (art. 7).
The 1960 Primary Education Act, as amended in 2019, mandates that the school must take action and report incidents involving, among other things, child abuse, sexual harassment/abuse, bullying, and discrimination against students. The school administration must notify parents if outside authorities are involved. In addition, article 30 stipulates that school regulations must provide students with clearly defined processes for filing complaints regarding, among other things, bullying, treatment, and evaluation (art. 13). 
The 1960 Primary Education Act, as amended in 2019, dictates that the school regulations must include rules for students' conduct and dress at school, as well as clear agreements for the use of mobile phones and other forms of communication (art. 30).

reform_title: The Constitution of the Republic of Uganda (1995)
reform_description: During these years of compulsory education, which the Constitution of the Republic of Uganda (1995) clarifies in Article 34 (2) shall be the responsibility of the state, most education (59% of schools, 80% of total enrolment) is provided by the state. The Constitution of the Republic of Uganda (1995, as amended in 2005), refers to non-state actors operating within the country’s education system as individuals, religious bodies and other nongovernmental organizations. Teachers in state schools are considered public officers under the Constitution, the administration and conditions of service of which are regulated separately in the Constitution and Education Service Act 2002 (which do not apply to non-state school teachers). The language of instruction in all schools is English, which is the official language of Uganda as stated in Article 6 (1) of the Constitution of the Republic of Uganda (1995). According to Paragraph XVIII of the Constitution of the Republic of Uganda (1995), non-state actors are free to found and operate educational institutions if they comply with the general educational policy of the country and maintain national standards.

reform_title: The Education (pre-primary, primary and post-primary) Act of 2008
reform_description:
The Education (pre-primary, primary and post-primary) Act of 2008 distinguishes between public education institutions or government founded institutions, government grant-aided education institutions and private institutions which include profit and non-profit making, and international institutions. A private school is defined as a school which is not founded by government and which does not receive statutory grants from Government, while a government grant aided school is defined as a school not founded by the Government but which receives statutory grants in the form of aid from Government and is jointly managed by the foundation body and Government. The Act additionally defines an international institution as a private educational institution offering foreign curricula and examinations and certification. The main documents which govern the registration and regulation of nonstate actors in education include The Education (pre-primary, primary and post-primary) Act of 2008. If a teacher is found to be violating any provisions set out in the Education (pre-primary, primary and post-primary) Act of 2008 (including conviction of a criminal offence, professional misconduct, or failure to comply with conditions of registration), their certificate of registration may be cancelled by the Director of Education. According to the Education (pre-primary, primary and post-primary) Act of 2008, non-state schools are prohibited from refusing admission to any pupil based on discriminatory grounds (without specifying what these may be). The Act does state however that if any person or organization refuses to enrol a UPE or USE student (which has been offered a tuition-free place in the respective school), they will be liable to pay a fine up to 1,000,000 Shillings (273.69 USD). Certificates of registration may also be cancelled if the institution fails to comply with any requirements or contravenes any section of the Education (pre-primary, primary and post-primary) Act of 2008. 
The Education (pre-primary, primary and post-primary) Act of 2008, established a Directorate of Education Standards (Article 46) which, in addition to district or municipal Inspectors of Education, are responsible for ensuring that minimum standards in non-state schools are maintained. Inspectors may at any time (with or without notice) enter and inspect a school and provide an evaluation report to the Permanent Secretary at the Ministry responsible for education.
According to Article 10(3) (a) of the Education (pre-primary, primary and post-primary) Act of 2008, primary education shall be universal and compulsory for pupils aged 6 (six) years and above which shall last seven years.

reform_title: The 2014 National Strategic Policy Plan for ICT in Education
reform_description:
The 2014 National Strategic Policy Plan for ICT in Education aims to utilise ICT so that all Surinamese children have equal opportunities to develop their individuality. This goal is achieved through five intervention areas: the learning process and learning resources, teaching (pre-education and continuing education), a learning infrastructure that facilitates learning, school administration, and community engagement. 
The term communication and information technology appears in various regulatory frameworks, including in the National Strategic Policy Plan for ICT in Education of 2014. 
The 2014 National Strategic Policy Plan for ICT in Education identifies as a priority the establishment of a conducive learning environment, as well as the prioritisation of hardware selection (Internet access, networks, and devices) for schools, students, and teachers, as well as an emphasis on Internet access. 
The 2014 National Strategic Policy Plan for ICT in Education mentions the need to introduce Cloud computing with Bring Your Own Device (BYOD). 
The 2014 National Strategic Policy Plan for ICT in Education mentions the need to pay special attention to internet access, particularly the rollout of high-speed internet in the interior of the nation. 
The 2014 National Strategic Policy Plan for ICT in Education aims to integrate ICT, particularly video, as a tool for self-reflection and professionalisation in teacher training and refresher courses. In addition, it aimed to steer primary education toward a blended learning environment comprised of classroom education and e-learning. 
According to the 2014 National Strategic Policy Plan for ICT in Education, ICT is a formative component of the curriculum for all subjects. Additionally, it is an element of self- and world orientation (OJW). 
One of the main policy priorities of the 2014 National Strategic Policy Plan for ICT in Education is the implementation support of ICT integration in the learning process, including ICT tools in the current curriculum framework and supported pedagogical and didactic teaching methods. 
The 2014 National Strategic Policy Plan for ICT in Education proposes to make ICT related teacher training a top priority. It also proposes improving the quality of Pedagogical Institutes (PIs) so that ICT can be used effectively to teach learning strategies and so that students can integrate targeted pedagogical and didactic methods. It was proposed that integration begins with two themes: languages and math. 
Regarding continuous education, the 2014 National Strategic Policy Plan for ICT in Education states that it is the State's responsibility to ensure that the new curriculum framework serves as the starting point and that Pedagogical Institutes (PIs) offer training and refresher courses beginning with the use of ICT in language and mathematics. In addition, it proposes creating opportunities for teachers to share their knowledge through online communities (open source), blogs (inviting colleagues to comment on practical teaching questions), TED education, etc.

The 1974 Constitution stipulates that The personal, economic and cultural welfare of the private person shall be fundamental aims of public activity. In particular, it shall be incumbent upon the public institutions to secure the right to education, and to promote social care and social security (Art. 2). All children covered by compulsory schooling shall be entitled to a free basic education at a public school. The public institutions shall also be responsible for the provision of higher education (Art. 21) and A foreign national within the Realm is equated with a Swedish citizen in respect of the right to an education (Art. 21). However, the Constitution does not refer to the integration of technology in education.

The Agency has been in charge of the construction of Skoldatatek, a digital education activity since 2006. Skoldatatek's extensive tasks include offering support for the activities of all the municipal schools in terms of ICT and reading and writing difficulties. Specifically, it is made to assist school employees and pupils to test digital equipment and to develop their ICT skills. Furthermore, it helps teachers to borrow these digital materials and receive technical assistance for their utilisation.

The courses and training programs are offered by universities and colleges across Sweden and many are available online, funded by the Swedish government.

The 2010 Swedish Education Act (2010) defines distance learning as interactive teaching that is conducted with information and communication technology where students and teachers are separated in both space and time and distance education as interactive education conducted with information and communication technology where students and teachers are separated in space but not in time.

The 2010 Swedish Education Act (2010) defines distance learning as interactive teaching that is conducted with information and communication technology where students and teachers are separated in both space and time and distance education as interactive education conducted with information and communication technology where students and teachers are separated in space but not in time. 
The following features are part of the Education Act (SFS 2010:800), which went into effect on August 1, 2010: Textbooks, instructional aids, and equipment were replaced by "learning tools,. The "access to books and other learning tools required for a modern education" for students is to be provided without charge. The rapid advancements in information technology, which have considerably altered how education is carried out and the tools students use, were a contributing factor to the change. The definition of "learning tools" was given as "equipment and material, other than books, that students need to accomplish their educational objectives." These learning resources may also include alternative learning resources like speech synthesis. 
In 2018, the Swedish government revised the Curriculum for the compulsory school, preschool class and school-age educare to include digital competency, based on the Education Act (2010).

According to ICT for Everyone A Digital Agenda (2011) from the Swedish Ministry of Enterprise, Energy and Communications, Information and Communication Technology (ICT) is one of the teaching tools needed by schools to achieve their aims. In 2011, the Swedish Ministry of Enterprise, Energy and Communications realized a digital agenda for Sweden called ICT for Everyone. This document outlined a new objective for ICT policy and to promote access to ICT for all individuals. Also, this agenda is aimed at improving digital literacy skills, particularly among students and young people.

Regarding the Initial teacher education in using ICT to promote inclusive learning, the Higher Education Ordinance degree description (SFS 1993:100), valid since 1 January 2011, entails the following: Graduates of all four teacher degrees preschool teacher, compulsory school teacher, subject teacher, and vocational studies teacher should demonstrate through their "skills and capacities" "the ability to confidently and critically use digital tools in teaching and to be aware of the significance of the role of various media and digital environments in this area." And this replaced the previous phrase "show the ability to use information technology in teaching and comprehend the significance of the role of various media in this area.

In the Higher Education Ordinance, Annex 2 (2014), a teacher-to-be must show digital competence, using digital aids confidently and critically in educational processes as well as considering the significant roles of different media and a digital environment in education

The Swedish Electrical Safety Act (2016) sets out the basic rules and requirements for electrical safety in Sweden, including those that apply to schools. It requires that all electrical installations and equipment in schools meet certain safety standards.

reform_title: New responsibilities for the Swedish National Agency for Education
reform_description: Since 2017, the Swedish National Agency for Education has the responsibility to digitize the national tests, which among other things makes requirements for a stable internet connection mandatory.

reform_title: The National Digitalization Strategy for the School System
reform_description: In 2017, the Swedish Ministry of Education and Research, in collaboration with leaders from the academic and school sectors, implemented the National Digitalization Strategy for the School System as a key component of the National Digitalization Strategy.

reform_title: The national digitalisation strategy for the years 2017-2022
reform_description: To realize the national digitalisation strategy for the years 2017-2022, Sweden's Municipalities and County Councils published #skolDigiplan, in 2019.

According to one of Sweden's initiatives, Digitalization in Schools program (2018), more and more schools and principals also choose to provide students with a personal digital tool.

In 2018, the Swedish government revised the Curriculum for the compulsory school, preschool class and school-age educare to include digital competency, based on the Education Act (2010). In this curriculum, it is clarified that The school should contribute to pupils developing an understanding of how digitalization is affecting the individual and the development of society., that All pupils should be given the opportunity to develop their ability to use digital technology and that The educational program should thus provide pupils with conditions to develop digital competence and an attitude that promotes entrepreneurship. reform_title: The School at Home project (Skola Hemma) and its website
reform_description: On March 12, 2020, the School at Home project (Skola Hemma) started by the Swedish National Agency for Education. It is a project that provides school operations with collective support to meet challenges when parts of the teaching are conducted as remote or distance learning via websites as response to the pandemic. The website is maintained by the Research Institutes of Sweden (RISE) in collaboration with the Swedish National Agency for Education, Swedish Edtech Industry, Sweden's Municipalities and County Councils, etc. On March 16, 2020, the first version of the website, skolahemma.se, was launched.

In response to the COVID-19 pandemic, the Swedish government introduced several changes to the regulations for the school system; a revised Ordinance (2020) was adopted on March 3 of 2022. The ordinances included remote learning, social distancing, increased hygiene measures, changes to the grading and assessment, and modification of school schedules. 
At the beginning of 2021, according to the Ordinance (2020:115), teaching at upper secondary schools continues to be conducted remotely due to the COVID-19 pandemic. At the same time, it became possible by ordinance for upper secondary schools, under certain conditions, to conduct certain teaching remotely, in combination with teaching at the school. Based on this ordinance, principals could also, under certain conditions, make changes to the teaching hours. 
Based on Ordinance (2020), certain student groups of students were considered to be vulnerable to distance learning and were exempted during the pandemic. The reason for the exception was that these students needed to be at school for practical sessions, special support, and examinations. It could also be because they needed it for physical, mental, or other reasons.

Implementation of a government-funded professional development program offering online and in-person university courses to enhance teachers' skills and knowledge across various subjects.

The assignment of responsibility to the Swedish National Agency for Education to digitize the mandatory national tests.

reform_description:
the 1960s witnessed the beginning of a
formal school curriculum intended for nationwide implementation. A system

two of upper secondary education) was introduced at that time.

reform_title: Increased period of compulsory education from four to seven years
reform_description:
the 1960s witnessed the beginning of a
formal school curriculum intended for nationwide implementation. During that period, the government administered education through the Ministry of Interior, and
increased the period of compulsory education from four to seven years.

In 1978-79, the government undertook a significant review of the
curriculum. As a result, it adopted a system of 6 + 3 + 3. The primary
curriculum consisted of the following five subjects or learning areas:
1. basic skills for living (consisting of Thai language and
mathematics)
2. life experience (consisting of social studies and health)
3. character development (including physical education, art, music
and dance)
4. work education
5. extra content as determined by schools (but commonly
including English).
Responsibility for both basic and secondary education was moved from
the Ministry of Interior to the Ministry for Curriculum and Instruction.

The first phase of ICT use in Thailand’s education system began in

order to provide them with basic skills for operating and applying ICT

a major overhaul of the curriculum in the 1990s was conducted

Thai schools began offering computer courses to students in
order to provide them with basic skills for operating and applying ICT
(Meleiseia, 2008). The courses were compulsory within the mathematics

The 1997 constitution, as well as later legislative documents, made primary and lower secondary education compulsory.

In recent years, Thailand has made sweeping reforms to its
education system, notably with the 1999 National Education Act, in an
effort to adapt to domestic and global changes and to support sustained
economic growth.
the 1999 National Education Act
Since 1999, formal education has been divided into basic and higher (tertiary) education

Roughly 20% of Thai students attend private institutions (Office of the
Permanent Secretary, 2014). Under the authority of the 1999 National
Education Act, these institutions can be run by individuals, organisations or
companies; deliver general, vocational or special education on a formal or
non-formal basis (e.g. short or part-time courses, distance learning); and
receive public subsidies

At the local level, basic education is managed primarily by Educational
Service Areas (ESAs). These were established following the decentralisation
of education administration set out in the 1999 National Education Act.

A major reform was implemented in
1999 under the aegis of the National Education Act.

Thailand’s 1999 National Education Act (NEA) introduced sweeping
changes to improve the quality of the education system. Moving away from a
highly centralised structure of education governance, the NEA called for
education financing and administration to be decentralised to ESAs, LAOs and
schools, mirroring the government’s wider efforts to devolve administrative
responsibilities. It established equity and student-centred – rather than rote –
learning as guiding principles for the education system, calling for all segments
of society to be able to participate in education and for all learners to develop
themselves at their own pace and to the best of their potential. The legislation
also introduced policies to transform the curriculum, student assessment, the
role of teachers and school leaders, and, to a lesser extent, the use of
information and communication technology (ICT) in education. 
The NEA tasked the OBEC with developing a new
basic education curriculum at the national level, and stipulated that schools
would be responsible for developing their own curriculum content to address
the needs of their community and “Thai wisdom”. It established a quality
assurance framework, creating the Office for National Education Standards
and Quality Assessment (ONESQA) to inspect public and private schools, and
set the stage for the later establishment of a national student assessment body,
the National Institute of Educational Testing Service (NIETS). It introduced a
number of reforms to enhance the teaching profession and develop
educational personnel, including the creation of an independent organisation
responsible for establishing standards for teachers and school leaders, which
became the TCT, the introduction of a licensing system, and new legislation to
ensure educators were sufficiently remunerated. Finally, it listed technological
knowledge and skills as a subject to be covered in the formal, non-formal and
informal education systems, and encouraged the use of different types of
teaching and learning media in schools.
In 1999, the NEA expanded compulsory education from six to nine years to improve students’ knowledge and skills
In 2001, the government conducted a second major review of curriculum, resulting largely from the National Education Act (1999)

reform_title: New curriculum and system-wide education reform
reform_description:
One jurisdiction that has introduced professional development practices to support
capacity building in the face of a new curriculum and system-wide education reform is
Hong Kong, China. the reform began in 1999
the 1999 National Education Act B.E. 2542 (NEA)

Reforms stemming from the
1999 NEA have emphasised implementing assessments that gauge student
progress and achievement in a variety of ways.

In 1999 Hong Kong, China conducted a reform of its entire education system
using a “whole-system implementation” approach. The overarching goal was to
improve student learning; a significant component of the strategy consisted of
changes to student assessment. These included the introduction of school-based
assessment, and initiatives to help teachers identify how their students were
learning and how to change their pedagogy where necessary. All this was coupled
with the development of instruction and learning resources for teachers to help
them implement assessment changes; these resources included practical examples
that could help teachers’ approaches in the classroom. New teacher professional
development strategies and in-school support were also developed, enabling
teachers to undertake professional development through a continuing professional
development framework.

Thailand’s 1999 National Education Act introduced changes to the
teaching profession, raising standards and supporting the country’s shift
towards a more learner-centred education system.

Several ICT in-service training programmes have used the “training-the-
trainer model”, an educational model in which a group of teachers are trained in
ICT skills and then required to train other teachers in their schools. One of the largest programmes of this kind was the IPST’s Lead Teacher Programme,
which began in 1999 and focused on training secondary ICT teachers to become
lead teachers.

reform_title: Reforms to raise the quality of the teaching profession
reform_description:
Since the early 2000s, the country has implemented a number of reforms to
raise the quality of the teaching profession. Key changes have included a
longer pre-service teacher education programme, a teacher certification
system and, more recently, a new teacher induction programme.

With reforms in 2001 and 2008, Thailand shifted its
content-based curriculum to a modern standards-based approach describing
what students should know and be able to do in each subject. The new
curriculum is intended to support more learner-centred teaching strategies
rather than focus on information retention.

In 2001, Thailand replaced its content-based
curriculum, which focused on the retention and recall of information, with

know and be able to do in each subject.

reform_title: Shift in curriculum philosophy and structure
reform_description:
In Thailand, the shift in curriculum philosophy and structure first
undertaken in 2001, marked a very significant turning
point for the Thai education system. Thailand moved away from a
knowledge-based, centralised curriculum characterised by didactic teaching
and minimal opportunities to contextualise the curriculum to reflect local
needs, and towards a standards-based curriculum incorporating a high
degree of local curriculum decision making.
the 2001 curriculum

one. In making this change, Thailand moved to a flexible national
curriculum with, at least in theory, a high degree of local input and greater flexibility.

Thailand’s 2008 curriculum document is more comprehensive than the 2001

The programme included measures to improve the working and learning
environment of schools, such as:
• reducing working hours in teacher contracts and adding guaranteed
planning, preparation and assessment time
• reducing paperwork requirements
• adding support staff to conduct administrative work and support

reform_title: Revision to shift in curriculum philosophy and structure
reform_description:
In Thailand, the shift in curriculum philosophy and structure first
undertaken in 2001, and revised in 2008, marked a very significant turning

knowledge-based, centralised curriculum characterised by didactic teaching
and minimal opportunities to contextualise the curriculum to reflect local

As mentioned above, the 2008 curriculum purposefully devolves to schools a number of hours for the development of “school-based” curriculum.
the 2008 curriculum

Of the four policy objectives considered in this section, the 2008
curriculum addresses “Thai-ness” most comprehensively. The first principle
underpinning the curriculum states: “The ultimate aim is attainment of
national unity; learning standards and goals are therefore set with a view to
enabling the children and youths to acquire knowledge, skills, attitudes and
morality to serve as a foundation for Thai-ness and universal values”
(OBEC, 2008: p. 4). Similarly, Goal 5 in the curriculum is “Awareness of
the need to preserve all aspects of Thai culture and Thai wisdom”
the 2008 curriculum
the 2008 curriculum

The 2008 Basic Education Core Curriculum outlines the framework
principles behind the current student assessment system in Thailand,
building on the broad expectations for student assessment laid out in the
1999 National Education Act B.E. 2542 (NEA). 
The 2008 curriculum
identifies two overarching objectives for student assessment: helping
learners develop their capacity and measuring their achievements. It points
to four main levels of student assessment:
• classrooms, where teachers are to regularly and continuously
measure and evaluate learners’ performance
• schools, where annual - or semester-based assessment seeks to
determine whether the education programme has enabled learners to
reach learning goals, and to identify any gaps that need to be
addressed
• the educational service area (ESA) or local level, which monitors
student learning through instruments including standard
examination papers and data obtained from schools
• the national level, where assessment of students in Grades 3, 6, 9
and 12 (P3, P6, M3 and M6) provides data to compare educational
quality “at different levels”. The results of national tests are meant
to support planning efforts to raise education quality and inform
policy making more broadly.

Thailand’s current standards-based
curriculum (the 2008 curriculum) requires schools to determine their own
criteria for student learning assessment.

facilitate assessment through its identification of key competency levels to
be attained

In general terms, Thailand’s 2008 curriculum compares well with the
curricula in high-performing countries in the region, such as Singapore and
Korea. Thailand’s 2008 curriculum document is more comprehensive than the 2001
iteration, but issues remain regarding its structure, contents and alignment
with education reform priorities.

The 2008 curriculum document improves upon its 2001 equivalent by
providing more detail about the different interconnected components of the
curriculum (Figure 3.2). An introductory section provides statements of the
vision and goals and a range of information and advice regarding, among
other things, the rationale for changes to the 2001 curriculum, the
importance of learners’ key competencies and desired characteristics, time
allocations, and learning management. In each of the eight learning area
sections devoted to the subjects studied by students in grades P1 to M6
(primary to upper secondary), there are designated strands, clear
descriptions of the learning standards, and grade-level indicators.

The national curriculum first implemented in 2001 and revised in
2008 changed expectations of teachers from top-down lecturers in a culture

reform_title: Revisions to the basic education curriculum
reform_description:
Thailand’s 2008 revisions to its basic education curriculum added
“capacity for technological application” as one of five key competencies to
be taught across all subjects in the basic education system, and included ICT
as a topic of study in all grades

Thailand implemented a small-scale OLPC programme in 2008,
comprising approximately 500 XO laptops designed to be low cost and
durable machines for school use

reform_title: Pre-primary education made free of charge
reform_description:
Pre-primary education became part of basic education in 2004. It is not
compulsory, but has been made free of charge since 2009 in order to
facilitate access.

Free schooling was increased to 15 years in 2009, with the addition of 1 more year at the
pre-primary level

reform_title: The Second Decade of Educational Reform
reform_description:
The Second Decade of Educational Reform (OEC, 2009) and other
government policy documents set out Thailand’s education policy priorities.
These include: 1) further decentralising the education system; 2) supporting
school-to-work transitions; 3) integrating the country with the Association
of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) economic community; and
4) conserving Thai culture and traditions.

reform_description:

the Second Decade of Education Reform (2009-18), the government
proposed further decentralisation of the education system, and to support
this, the creation of a leadership development plan to set out qualifications
required for the school leadership role, and the recruitment process, career

ICT has been a central component of Thailand’s economic development
strategy for several decades, as evidenced by a series of national ICT policy
frameworks. These include Thailand’s ICT2010 (Ministry of ICT, 2009b,
2009a) and ICT2020 reports, which give a broad outline of the overall ICT development strategy, as well as a series of ICT Master Plans, which give
more specific implementation details and progress updates.

It has also introduced scholarship
programmes to attract high-achieving students to the profession and address

reform_title: New Breed of Teachers scholarship programme
reform_description:
In 2010, Thailand introduced a one-year New Breed of
Teachers scholarship programme for students who already had a degree as a
temporary measure to address shortage areas.

Thailand has traditionally used scholarship programmes to try to
increase the supply of high-quality teachers in shortage subjects and rural
areas of the country. The largest programme, and a flagship of Thailand’s
recent education reform, is the country’s New Breed of Teachers Project, a
THB 4 234 million (USD 150 million) programme to produce 30 000 high-
quality teachers, including teachers in shortage subjects, between 2010 and 2015.

reform_title: Implementation of a National Education Accounts project
reform_description:
Between 2011 and 2015, Thailand implemented a National Education
Accounts project with the aim of tracking and analysing education budget
flows comprehensively

At the local level, basic education is managed primarily by Educational
Service Areas (ESAs). These were established following the decentralisation
of education administration set out in the 1999 National Education Act. In 2011, Thailand was divided into 185 ESAs, 3 of which are situated in Bangkok

It is increasingly common for modern curricula to describe the knowledge,
skills, behaviours and dispositions that students need to live and work
successfully in the 21st century. These competencies, also called transversal skills,
generally bridge all subjects of the curriculum, pointing to the interdisciplinary
nature of learning in today’s world. The 2013 National Curriculum of Australia
describes these cross-curricular competencies in the form of seven “general
capabilities”:
1. literacy
2. numeracy
3. information and communication technology (ICT) capability
4. critical and creative thinking
5. personal and social capability
6. ethical understanding
7. intercultural understanding.

In a promising move, Thailand introduced an induction programme for
new teachers in 2013. Assistant teachers are evaluated by their principal, a
senior teacher and a member of the school board every three months
throughout their first two years on the job.

forms and tests be assessed at least once per academic term and that
assessment results be applied to individual teachers’ professional
development (ONESQA, 2013)

reform_title: Establishment of committees and boards to develop a new education reform agenda
reform_description:
Since the military took power in May 2014, the government has established several
committees and boards to develop a new education reform agenda.

reform_title: Changes to the teacher salary scale
reform_description:
In 2014, the government introduced changes to the salary scale to pay
teachers more for higher-level qualifications such as longer bachelor’s
degree programmes, master’s or doctoral degrees (OEC, 2014). The scale
makes no distinction between primary and secondary teachers.

The Ministry of Education has since produced two subsequent Master Plans

reform_description:

The country has also invested a comparatively large
