reform_title: Free schooling extended from pre-primary to upper secondary education
reform_description:
To promote enrolment among disadvantaged students, Thailand
expanded free education from 12 to 15 years in 2009, and free schooling now
extends from pre-primary to upper secondary education.

As Thailand became more open to international influences, foreign languages and
vocational subjects were added to the curriculum.

In 2001, the government conducted a second major review of
curriculum, resulting largely from the National Education Act (1999) and
forming an integral part of the National Education Plan (2001-2016). A very
significant shift in curriculum philosophy and structure occurred at this time.
While the previous content-based curriculum had focused primarily on the
retention and recall of knowledge and information, the 2001 curriculum
consisted of a set of standards for all subjects.

The government created 225 Education Service Areas (ESAs) at that time, and
delegated numerous functions to them under a newly prioritised
decentralisation strategy.

The most significant change incorporated into the 2008 curriculum was
the addition of very detailed “grade-level indicators” and “interval
indicators” (for Grades 10-12) to guide teachers in their selection of content
needed to achieve the standards. In essence, these provided a range of
instructions to teachers, each commencing with a verb, such as “record”,
“explain”, “present” or “express”.

For each of the eight learning areas studied by every student from grades P1 to
M6 in Thai schools, the 2008 curriculum provides the following guidance to
educators: a rationale (i.e. why it is necessary to learn the learning area), a
summary of the content (i.e. an overview of the learning strands), a statement of
“learners’ quality” (i.e. student outcomes at the end of grades P3, P6, M3 and M6,
the years of national assessment) and a description of the standards and grade-
level indicators for each strand.

The 2008 curriculum introduced the concept of competencies and
named five key competencies all students were expected to achieve:
1) communication capacity; 2) thinking capacity; 3) problem-solving
capacity; 4) capacity to apply life skills; and 5) capacity for technological
application. This is consistent with the approach taken in a number of
contemporary curricula (Box 3.4).

reform_description:

to serve as role models to develop other education personnel (QLF, 2015).

reform_description:

reform_description:

• strategic direction and policy environment

Between 2011 and 2014, the Thai government proposed seven priority

was the One Tablet Per Child (OTPC) policy

A more recent One Tablet Per Child policy was launched in Thailand by
the government of former Prime Minister Yingluck Shinawatra.

Thailand has invested in the development of digital learning resources.
The country’s One Tablet Per Child project involved the production of
e-books, learning objects, multimedia and songs to be installed on tablets
distributed to students.
In 2002, Thailand’s pre-service programmes expanded from four to five years to include an entire year of practicum time.

reform_title: Introduction of Computer Courses in Schools
reform_description: Thailand began offering compulsory computer courses to students within the mathematics subject cluster to provide basic skills for operating and applying ICT. The courses were subsequently revised in 1990 and 1997 to respond to technological developments.

A sweeping national education reform that decentralized education financing and administration to Educational Service Areas (ESAs) and local administration organizations; expanded compulsory education from six to nine years; established equity and student-centered learning as guiding principles; introduced curriculum reform; created a quality assurance framework including the Office for National Education Standards and Quality Assessment (ONESQA); set the stage for a national student assessment body; introduced teacher licensing and certification; listed ICT as a subject to be covered across formal and non-formal education; formally introduced and promoted portfolio-based assessment as part of classroom and school-level student assessment practices; and formed the legal basis for subsequent curriculum and assessment changes.

The Institute for the Promotion of Teaching Science and Technology launched a programme focused on training secondary ICT teachers to become lead teachers. These lead teachers reviewed digital materials, created educational resources and training content, and built ICT capacity among both ICT and non-ICT teachers. By 2007, 555 lead teachers were providing training to approximately 1,000 teachers per year at 20 ESA training centres.
A Thai National Teachers Union was established to support teachers and educational personnel with negotiations regarding their salary, benefits, employment, and licences.

reform_title: Establishment of the Office for National Education Standards and Quality Assessment (ONESQA)
reform_description: Creation of a national external quality assurance body responsible for reviewing all educational institutions in Thailand every five years against minimum quality standards.

Thailand replaced its content-based national curriculum with a learner-centered and standards-based curriculum that outlined predetermined standards for what students should know and be able to do in each subject across basic education. The curriculum included standards for what students in all 12 grades should know about ICT; technological education comprised ICT and content on design and technology.

A long-term national education planning instrument mandated by the 1999 National Education Act to promote human-centered development, the knowledge-based economy, continuous learning, and greater participation of stakeholders across the education system. Also referred to as the 15-Year National Education Plan (2002-2016).
The government established 225 Education Service Areas and delegated numerous administrative and support functions to them as part of a decentralisation strategy for the education system.

reform_title: Pre-service Teacher Education Programme Expansion to Five Years
reform_description: Thailand expanded its pre-service teacher education programmes from four to five years to include an entire year of practicum time, in part to prepare teacher candidates for 21st century Thai classrooms equipped with educational technology.
National legislation requiring all children between 7 and 15 years of age to attend school, thereby formalizing nine years of compulsory education.

A governance reform and national law that reorganized two ministries and two other bodies under the umbrella of the Ministry of Education to improve quality, democratic responsiveness, and efficiency of educational administration, establishing the institutional framework governing education administration in Thailand.

reform_title: Expansion of Free Schooling to 14 Years
reform_description: The government extended free basic education from 12 to 14 years, including two years of pre-primary education, making pre-primary schooling free of charge.

reform_title: Inclusion of Pre-Primary Education in Basic Education
reform_description: Pre-primary education was formally incorporated into the basic education system, expanding the scope of publicly provided schooling to include early childhood levels.

reform_title: Establishment of the Office of the Teacher Civil Service and Educational Personnel Commission (OTEPC)
reform_description: Creation of a national body established in 2004 to supervise all administrative matters concerning public school personnel under the jurisdiction of the Ministry of Education.

Thailand implemented a new teacher salary scheme that significantly increased teacher compensation. The scheme established salary ranges across career levels from assistant teacher through senior expert teacher.

reform_title: Establishment of the National Institute for Development of Teachers Faculty Staff and Educational Personnel (NIDTEP)
reform_description: Creation of a national institution in 2005 charged with formulating policies for teacher development, implementing support activities, and coordinating relevant agencies across the education system.

reform_title: Establishment of the National Institute of Educational Testing Service (NIETS)
reform_description: Creation of a dedicated national public institution to conduct the majority of the country's standardized student assessments, including the Ordinary National Education Test (O-NET) administered to students in Grades 6, 9, and 12, as well as the V-NET, N-NET, I-NET, and B-NET. Responsibilities include managing national testing systems, developing assessment tools, and measuring educational standards.

reform_title: TCT Standards of Knowledge Performance and Conduct for Teachers
reform_description: The Teachers' Council of Thailand established professional standards for teachers covering knowledge, performance, and conduct. These standards are used as part of the processes to accredit pre-service programmes and license teachers.

reform_title: Master Plan for ICT in Education 2007-2011
reform_description: The Ministry of ICT produced a strategy document proposing to teach students to use ICT to compete globally, integrate ICT into the classroom, further develop ICT infrastructure in the education sector, and use ICT to more effectively manage the school system.

Thailand revised its national basic education curriculum building on the 2001 reform to further develop a standards-based approach, adding detailed grade-level indicators and interval indicators for Grades 10-12 across eight learning areas, and introducing five key learner competencies—including capacity for technological application—and eight desired characteristics, with the intent of supporting learner-centered teaching strategies. ICT was included as a topic of study in all grades with special attention to ICT proficiency at the lower secondary level.

reform_title: 2008 Promotion of Non-Formal and Informal Education Act
reform_description: National legislation regulating access to non-formal and informal education in line with NEA principles of lifelong education for all, participation of all parts of society, and continuous development of the learning process.

reform_title: Establishment of the Office of Non-Formal and Informal Education (ONIE)
reform_description: Creation of a national office in 2008 within the Ministry of Education to support and coordinate all activities outside formal education, make policy recommendations, and manage recognition and equivalency issues between formal and non-formal education.

reform_title: Expansion of Free Schooling to 15 Years
reform_description: The government further extended free basic education from 14 to 15 years with the addition of one more year at the pre-primary level, making three years of pre-primary schooling free of charge.

A national education reform strategy and mid-term planning document setting principal goals of quality improvement, increasing learning opportunities for all, and strengthening stakeholder participation. Addressed evaluation and assessment, governance of small schools, decentralisation, disadvantaged students, qualifications frameworks, curricula, teacher quality, budget allocation, school-to-work transitions, ASEAN integration, conservation of Thai culture, and the creation of a leadership development plan to set out qualifications required for school leadership roles, recruitment processes, career paths, and incentives.

A THB 4,234 million (USD 150 million) government scholarship programme to produce 30,000 high-quality teachers including teachers in shortage subjects. The project consisted of a five-year programme for new students and a one-year programme for students with a bachelor's degree in a shortage subject area. Scholarship recipients were guaranteed employment and received a monthly bonus of THB 15,000 upon employment.

reform_title: Ministerial Regulations for Internal Quality Assurance of Educational Institutions
reform_description: Government regulations establishing systems, criteria, and procedures for internal quality assurance, requiring all affiliated agencies and schools to maintain an internal quality assurance system and submit annual reports to local education authorities.

The Thai government launched a national policy to distribute tablet computers to students at the basic education level. Approximately 1.2 million tablets were distributed to Grade 1 students. The programme included production of e-books, learning objects, multimedia, and songs installed on the tablets. The programme was suspended in June 2014 by the incoming government.

reform_title: Ministry of Education Four-Year Action Plan 2013-2016
reform_description: A short-term national education planning document providing vision, mission, and strategic targets aimed at developing education management, lifelong learning, education and teacher quality, and the use of ICT.

reform_title: Teacher Induction Programme for New Teachers
reform_description: Thailand introduced a formal induction programme for new teachers whereby assistant teachers are evaluated by their principal, a senior teacher, and a school board member every three months throughout their first two years on the job. Evaluators receive a manual and assistant teachers receive on-the-job training. Teachers who do not pass the induction are required to quit their position within five days.

Following suspension of the One Tablet Per Child programme, remaining funds were redirected to the Smart Classroom initiative developed by OBEC and Microsoft, aiming to integrate teaching with technology, digital content, cloud-based services and Microsoft Office 365, and technological devices into classrooms.

reform_title: Master Plan for ICT in Education 2014-2018
reform_description: The Ministry of Education produced a Master Plan for ICT in Education covering 2014-2018, setting out strategies for ICT integration including plans to upgrade Internet access in over 30,000 schools by leasing networks and integrating existing education networks.
The Thai government introduced changes to the teacher salary scale to pay teachers more for higher-level qualifications such as longer bachelor's degree programmes, master's or doctoral degrees.

reform_title: Reduced Classroom Hours Pilot in State Schools
reform_description: A government-mandated pilot project to cut classroom hours introduced in 4,100 state schools as part of the post-2014 education reform agenda.

reform_title: Alignment of English Teaching to CEFR
reform_description: The government mandated that English language teaching across schools be aligned to the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR) as part of the post-2014 education reform agenda.

OBEC and OVEC officials began drafting a new curriculum for general upper secondary education aimed at encouraging the study of vocational subjects and expanding opportunities for work-based learning.
A national committee was appointed to develop a national qualifications framework (NQF) with the ultimate goal of improving Thailand's global competitiveness.

A landmark comprehensive framework law that mandated 12 years of free basic education, decentralized education administration into Educational Service Area Offices (ESAOs), mandated student-centered learning, and established the Office for National Education Standards and Quality Assessment (ONESQA).
Introduction of a national curriculum replacing the previous segmented systems, aiming to standardize basic education across the country and promote localized curriculum development.

reform_title: Teacher and Educational Personnel Council Act
reform_description: Establishment of the Teachers Council of Thailand (Khurusapha) as an independent professional regulatory body responsible for issuing teacher licenses and setting professional standards.

Introduction of a mandatory national assessment system administered by the newly established National Institute of Educational Testing Service (NIETS) to evaluate student achievement at the end of Grades 6, 9, and 12.

A comprehensive national curriculum reform revising the 2001 framework by introducing clearer national standards, core indicators, and specific learning outcomes across eight core learning areas to guide instruction and assessment.

reform_title: 15 Years Free Education with Quality Policy
reform_description: A national policy extending the provision of free public education from 12 to 15 years, effectively expanding financial coverage to include pre-primary education and upper secondary education (both general and vocational tracks).

reform_title: Moderate Class, More Knowledge (Reduce Class Time, Increase Learning Time)
reform_description: A national government initiative applied to schools nationwide to reduce formal academic instructional hours in the afternoon, replacing them with experiential, vocational, and active learning extracurricular activities to reduce student stress and foster holistic skills.

Introduction of a formal school curriculum for nationwide implementation, organised around a 7+3+2 structure of seven years of primary education, three years of lower secondary education and two years of upper secondary education.

reform_title: Extension of Compulsory Education to Seven Years
reform_description: Increase in the period of compulsory education from four to seven years during the period when the government administered education through the Ministry of Interior.

Adoption of a 6+3+3 school structure and a revised primary curriculum organised around basic skills for living, life experience, character development, work education and school-determined extra content.

reform_title: Transfer of Basic and Secondary Education Responsibility
reform_description: Transfer of responsibility for basic and secondary education from the Ministry of Interior to the Ministry for Curriculum and Instruction.
The 1997 constitution and later legislative documents made primary and lower secondary education compulsory.

A national education law introducing sweeping reforms to improve education quality, decentralise education financing and administration, establish equity and student-centred learning principles, and transform curriculum, assessment, teachers, school leadership and ICT in education.

reform_title: Decentralisation to Education Service Areas, Local Administrations and Schools
reform_description: Decentralisation of education financing and administration to Educational Service Areas, Local Administration Organisations and schools under the 1999 National Education Act.

reform_title: Expansion of Compulsory Education to Nine Years
reform_description: Expansion of compulsory education from six to nine years under the 1999 National Education Act.
Creation of a national quality assurance framework and the Office for National Education Standards and Quality Assessment to inspect public and private schools.

reform_title: Teacher Profession Standards and Licensing Framework
reform_description: Introduction of reforms to enhance the teaching profession, including an independent organisation responsible for teacher and school leader standards, teacher licensing and legislation on educator remuneration.

reform_title: ICT in Education Mandate under the National Education Act
reform_description: Inclusion of technological knowledge and skills in formal, non-formal and informal education and encouragement of different teaching and learning media in schools.

Replacement of the content-based curriculum with a learner-centred, standards-based curriculum setting out standards for all subjects and giving educators greater responsibility for how and what students should be taught.
A 15-year national education plan promoting human-centred development, the knowledge-based economy, continuous learning and greater stakeholder participation.

reform_title: Twelve Years of Free Primary and Secondary Education
reform_description: Amendment to the National Education Act making 12 years of primary and secondary education free of charge, including for non-Thai children living in Thailand.
Extension of teacher pre-service education from four to five years to improve teaching quality.
National legislation requiring all children between 7 and 15 years of age to attend school.
Expansion of free schooling to 14 years, including two years of pre-primary education.

Legislation enacting teacher profession reforms and establishing or revising responsibilities for the Teachers’ Council of Thailand, the Committee for Promotion of the Benefits and Welfare of Teachers and Educational Personnel, and the Office of the Teacher Civil Service and Educational Personnel Commission.

reform_title: National Institute for the Development of Teachers, Faculty Staff and Educational Personnel
reform_description: Establishment of a national institute to oversee in-service training of education personnel, replacing earlier organisations such as the Institute for the Development of Educational Administrators.
Implementation of a national licensing system requiring teachers to obtain a licence from the Teachers’ Council of Thailand and renew it every five years.

reform_title: National Institute of Educational Testing Service
reform_description: Creation of a national assessment body responsible for managing testing systems and methods, developing tools to assess educational standards, and administering national education tests.

First national ICT education strategy document, setting goals to teach students to use ICT, integrate ICT into classrooms, develop education-sector ICT infrastructure and use ICT to manage the school system more effectively.
National standards-based basic education curriculum adding learner key competencies, desired characteristics, learning standards and indicators across eight learning areas.

reform_title: Promotion of Non-Formal and Informal Education Act
reform_description: Legislation regulating access to non-formal education in line with lifelong education for all, participation of all sectors of society and continuous development of the learning process.
Expansion of free schooling to 15 years by adding one more year at the pre-primary level.

reform_title: General Aptitude Test and Professional and Academic Aptitude Test
reform_description: Introduction of national aptitude and academic tests for secondary school graduates seeking admission to higher education within the national admissions system.

Mid-term Ministry of Education reform agenda with goals of quality improvement, expanded learning opportunities and stronger stakeholder participation, addressing assessment, small school governance, decentralisation, disadvantaged students, qualifications frameworks, curricula, teacher quality and budget allocation.
Teacher scholarship programme designed to attract high-achieving students into teaching and address subject-specific and region-specific teacher shortages.

reform_title: Information and Communication Master Plan for Education 2011-2013
reform_description: National ICT education plan intended to support continuous development in ICT in education through investments in infrastructure, digital learning materials, hardware and related implementation measures.

Government programme distributing tablet computers to students, including Grade 1 students, and producing digital learning materials such as e-books, learning objects, multimedia and songs for use on the tablets.

reform_title: Master Plan for ICT in Education 2014-2018
reform_description: National ICT education master plan providing proposed funding and direction for ICT infrastructure, Internet connectivity, digital learning materials and ICT integration in education.
Government initiative using remaining One Tablet Per Child funds to integrate teaching with technology, digital content, cloud-based services and technological devices into classrooms.

reform_title: Pilot Project to Cut Classroom Hours
reform_description: Government-led pilot project introduced in 4,100 state schools to reduce classroom hours.
Government mandate aligning English teaching with the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages.
The Council of Higher Education (YÖK, 1981) is an autonomous body responsible for all HEIs.

reform_description: In 2017, Turkey extended its Conditional Cash Transfer for Education programme (2003), previously limited to targeting disadvantaged families, to refugees. The programme is collaboratively run by the MoNE, Ministry of Family, Labour and Social Services, Turkish Red Crescent and UNICEF, and financially supported by the EU, Norway and the USA.

The project was initially a collaborative effort led by the Ministry of National Education with the support of several other ministries, the Treasury and the Scientific and Technological Research Council. FATİH has evolved into a longer-term programme that seeks to foster digital skills and improve access to Information and Communication Technologies (ICT) in schools.

reform_title: Increasing School Attendance Rates Especially for Girls (KEP-II, 2015-17)
reform_description:
Increasing School Attendance Rates Especially for Girls (KEP-II, 2015-17) aimed to raise participation,
particularly for girls, in vocational education. With EU financial support, KEP-II includes: school and community
events and family meetings to raise awareness; capacity building for gender mainstreaming and counselling; and
Provincial Visit Teams, which provide targeted support to early school-leavers.

The Turkish Qualifications Framework (2015) establishes an integrated standards framework for all education levels to promote lifelong learning, permeability between tracks and international mobility.

In Turkey, according to the Teacher Appointment and Relocation Regulation (2015), school leaders conduct annual teacher appraisals to measure teacher effectiveness and provide guidance. Teachers of all levels are assessed on how frequently they display criteria laid out in the national framework. These appraisals are low-stakes, not informing career progression or pay levels.

Since 2010, the MoNE has developed five-year strategic plans establishing medium-term goals to inform work at the central, provincial and district levels. The second (2015-19) emphasised quality and institutional capacity. To develop the latter, the MoNE consulted around 38 000 internal stakeholders (educators and administrators) and 35 000 external stakeholders (students, parents and academics).
The Higher Education Quality Board (2015) conducts quality assessments of HEIs and authorises private accreditation agencies.

Turkey has strengthened support for novice teachers through introducing a Teacher Induction Programme (2016) during the initial six months of a teacher’s first role. Participants are assigned a mentor and engage in various internal and external development activities, including school visits, training sessions, and planning and assessment support. The process includes formal probation appraisal, with evaluations conducted by the principal, the mentor and an inspector, as well as a written examination. Success leads to certification; unsuccessful candidates may repeat the induction process in another school.

Turkey reformed the system of upper secondary school placement (2018) aiming to address inequities created by early tracking. The new system sees most students transition to upper secondary based on personal preference and residence; around 10% of places are determined by an optional centralised examination.
The Literacy Campaign (2018) offers adult literacy courses through schools and public learning centres across the country.

reform_title: The Strengthening Democracy Culture in Basic Education project (2018-21)
reform_description: The Strengthening Democracy Culture in Basic Education project (2018-21) aims to promote democratic school cultures and strengthen democracy throughout society. Covering pre-school and compulsory education, and with financial support from the EU, the project develops relevant educational materials, builds teachers’ capacity to develop civic competencies, raises democratic awareness in schools and their communities, and develops policy recommendations for future system-level practices.
The Strategy’s objectives were rearranged for integration into the Education Vision 2023 (2018)
In 2018, the MoNE began publishing the Education, Analysis and Evaluation Series, offering regular analysis of policy work and challenges in education.

Education Vision 2023 (2018) promotes a holistic, human-centred approach to education. The 8 concrete targets address many of the challenges raised in this profile: 1) reduce gaps between schools; 2) improve school learning environments; 3) improve the attractiveness of VET; 4) reduce exam pressure; 5) develop 21st century skills; 6) improve educators’ job satisfaction; 7) expand ECEC; and 8) improve inclusive practices for students with special educational needs. The vision establishes 44 sub-goals, each with a rough policy timeline, often including piloting phases.

Coordinated by the Board of Education, the latest curricular revisions (2018) emphasise 21st century competences, reducing curriculum overload and enhancing labour market relevance. The revisions also took into account the knowledge and skills required for the information age, diversity in teacher competences and student needs in different types of schools across different regions, and alignment with the Turkish Qualifications Framework (see “Preparing students for the future”).

From 2018, with the introduction of the Education Vision 2023, the development of the Education Information Network (EBA) gained importance. EBA is the official national digital education platform, providing interactive and subject-specific digital content for students and teachers across Turkey from pre-school to upper secondary education. EBA aims to provide collaborative digital learning opportunities through the creation of alternative communication channels between teachers and learners and the development of personalised learning plans for individual learners. Features include a smart content recommendation system, gamified features such as points and badge collection, and the EBA Portfolio where students can display their achievements and their work.
The Education Vision 2023 school development model aims for a more equal and inclusive allocation, taking into account differences between students, schools and localities.

Since 2010, the MoNE has developed five-year strategic plans establishing medium-term goals to inform work at the central, provincial and district levels. The third plan (2019-23) aims to improve transitions to tertiary and employment, as well as support for special educational needs, and modernise structures for greater efficiency.
The comprehensive revision of VET curricula was completed in 2020 in collaboration with sector representatives.
Policies implemented over recent years, such as the expansion of open education, may have helped develop some of the digital skills, tools and infrastructure required for mass online learning

reform_description:
Turkey formed School
Administrative Boards of Vocational and Technical Education in all provinces and districts. These Boards
bring together representatives of municipal authorities, various professional and sectoral organisations and
other public and private institutions.

As part of efforts to strengthen quality assurance in VET, Turkey established a National Quality Assurance Centre for VET to coordinate and steer the various stakeholders involved in the sector and to develop a quality assurance framework aligned with the European Quality Assurance in Vocational Education and Training (EQAVET) Reference Framework.

A national programme providing financial payments to families of school-age children every two months on the condition that their child maintains a school attendance record of at least 80%, covering children from pre-primary to upper secondary level.

A national project addressing unemployment from skills mismatch by involving employers in skills development of the unemployed through vocational courses aligned with a Labour Force Market Needs Analysis, covering three months of in-school training and a three-month internship.

A five-year national strategic plan establishing medium-term goals focused on access to education, with quantitative progress indicators, responsible actors, and financial resources assigned to each goal.

reform_title: Movement to Enhance Opportunities and Improve Technology (FATİH)
reform_description: A national government project to extend and enhance the use of technology in teaching and learning by establishing digital infrastructure including broadband internet, tablets, and interactive whiteboards in schools, developing online educational content, and providing professional development to teachers.

reform_title: Establishment of the General Directorate for Lifelong Learning
reform_description: Creation of a dedicated directorate within MoNE to lead non-formal educational provision for those beyond the age of compulsory schooling.

reform_title: Improving the Quality of Vocational and Technical Education in Turkey (IQVET-I)
reform_description: A government project to improve the quality of vocational and technical education in Turkey through teacher training, student vocational and career guidance services, and partnerships between VET institutions and private enterprises.

reform_title: Private Education Institutions Law No. 5580 Amendment
reform_description: An amendment to the Private Education Institutions Law introducing financial support from the government for private VET institutions, initially for those located within Organised Industrial Zones and later extended to all private VET institutions.

reform_title: Extension of Compulsory Education to 12 Years
reform_description: A national policy increasing the length of compulsory education from ages 6 to 17, making it 12 years and one of the longest in the OECD.

reform_title: Co-operation Protocol for Teaching Entrepreneurship and Leadership Trainings
reform_description: A co-operation protocol between MoNE and the Scientific and Technical Research Council of Turkey aiming to carry out in-service training for VET teachers in real work environments.

A national strategy identifying six priority areas for lifelong learning: raising awareness, increasing opportunities, expanding access, developing a guidance counselling system, recognising prior learning, and establishing a monitoring and evaluation system.

reform_title: Improving the Quality of Vocational and Technical Education in Turkey (IQVET-II)
reform_description: A second round of the IQVET project resulting in training of more than 7,000 teachers, vocational and career guidance services for around 10,000 students, social partners,and over 200 partnerships between VET institutions and private enterprises.

reform_title: General Directorate of Assessment Evaluation and Examination Services
reform_description: Establishment of a dedicated national directorate to lead evaluation and assessment pilots and national projects, previously a unit within another directorate, with new provincial-level Assessment and Evaluation Centres to improve assessment capacity.

A government programme subsidising fees or parental contributions required by private schools to enable students from disadvantaged backgrounds to access these institutions, extended in 2015/16 to include children aged 48-66 months attending private ECEC settings.

reform_title: Increasing School Attendance Rates Especially for Girls (KEP-II)
reform_description: An EU-funded programme aimed at raising participation particularly for girls in vocational education through school and community events, family meetings, capacity building for gender mainstreaming and counselling, and Provincial Visit Teams providing targeted support to early school-leavers.

An integrated national standards framework for all education levels established to promote lifelong learning, permeability between tracks, and international mobility, overseen by the Vocational Qualifications Authority and aligned with the European Qualifications Framework.

A national regulation establishing that school leaders conduct annual teacher appraisals to measure teacher effectiveness and provide guidance, assessing teachers on how frequently they display criteria laid out in the national framework.
A five-year national strategic plan emphasising quality and institutional capacity, developed through consultation with approximately 38,000 internal and 35,000 external stakeholders.

reform_title: Mission Differentiation and Specialisation on the Basis of Regional Development
reform_description: A government initiative to promote higher education institutions' contribution to their region and encourage specialisation, with selected HEIs developing regional development action plans overseen by a multi-stakeholder expert body established by YÖK.

reform_title: Promoting Integration of Syrian Kids into the Turkish Education System
reform_description: A government project with EU support providing language programmes, catch-up and extra-support classes, transportation, educational materials, teacher training, and awareness raising in the 24 provinces with the highest concentration of Syrian refugees to integrate Syrian children into the Turkish education system.

A government initiative aiming to link all vocational and technical secondary education institutions with at least one sector organisation to strengthen collaboration between VET schools and industry.

reform_title: Vocational and Technical Education School Board of Directors
reform_description: A national body established to oversee and steer provincial School Administrative Boards of Vocational and Technical Education, which bring together representatives of municipal authorities, professional and sectoral organisations. and other public and private institutions.

A formal national induction programme for novice teachers during the initial six months of their first role, including mentoring, internal and external development activities, formal probation appraisal by the principal, mentor, and inspector, and a written examination leading to certification.

Based on the Universal Primary Education (UPE) program implemented by the Government of Uganda in 1997, tuition fees in primary state schools were abolished.

With the introduction of the UPE in 1997, many private schools in Uganda that were owned and managed by various non-state actors began being provided with statutory state grants, known as government-aided schools. UPE programs are prohibited from levying any kind of fees.UPE programs have been established by the Government of Uganda for state and non-state schools to offer tuition-free places primarily to students from lower socioeconomic backgrounds.

Based on the Local Government’s Act 243 adopted in 1997, the management and provision of education services in Uganda was devolved to local governments, where regional governments and district or urban councils are directly responsible for the supervision, management, and regulation of both state and non-state education actors within their jurisdiction.

The Universal Secondary Education (USE) program introduced in 2007 provides tuition-free places in secondary state and non-state schools to students from more disadvantaged backgrounds.

With the introduction of the USE in 2007, many private schools in Uganda that were owned and managed by various non-state actors began being provided with statutory state grants, known as government-aided schools. USE programs are prohibited from levying any kind of fees. USE programs have been established by the Government of Uganda for state and non-state schools to offer tuition-free places primarily to students from lower socioeconomic backgrounds. The USE policy (as well as all secondary schools) requires the students to have achieved a high score in the PLE in order to be offered a place in the program

reform_description: The main documents which govern the registration and regulation of nonstate actors in education include the Guidelines for Establishing, Licensing, Registering and Classification of Private Schools/Institutions in Uganda (2014).

reform_title: The Guidelines for Establishing, Licensing, Registering and Classification of Private Schools/Institutions in Uganda (2014)
reform_description: The Guidelines for Establishing, Licensing, Registering and Classification of Private Schools/Institutions in Uganda (2014) provide a detailed list of 20 different criteria for schools regarding water and sanitation, including student-latrine ratios (separated by sex), availability of latrine for people with special needs, availability of cleaning material, and amount of safe drinking water.

reform_title: The Higher Education Students Financing Act (2014)
reform_description: The Higher Education Students Financing Act (2014) established a Higher Education Students Financing Board (HESFB) to be responsible for providing loans and scholarships to eligible Ugandan students to pursue tertiary education in registered institutions (both state and non-state) in Uganda. These schemes cover costs such as tuition fees, functional fees, and research expenses. Students seeking financial assistance to pursue tertiary education can apply for either scheme, and their eligibility will be determined by the HESFB based on region, gender, socioeconomic needs, and equity.

According to the Circular No.2/2015, all acts of violence against children in schools, institutes and colleges has been banned. Paragraph 4 of the Circular further clarifies that this applies to all the educational institutions, whether government-aided, community or private.

reform_title: Uganda’s Education and Sports Sector Strategic Plan 2017/18 - 2019/20
reform_description: The term non-state actors is only used in Uganda’s Education and Sports Sector Strategic Plan 2017/18 - 2019/20 which refers to non-state education providers or non-state actors. Uganda’s Education and Sports Sector Strategic Plan 2017/18 2019/20 aims to enhance (the) contribution of non-state actors in education, recognizing the critical complementary role of private sector in achieving the policy objective of providing equitable access to education and sports by Government to all people living in Uganda.

reform_title: The Education Response Plan for Refugees and Host Communities in Uganda 2018
reform_description: The role of public-private partnerships is additionally highlighted in the Education Response Plan for Refugees and Host Communities in Uganda 2018, which states that the MoES and partners shall explore opportunities for public-private partnerships and work with the private sector.

reform_title: The Early Childhood Care and Education Policy 2018
reform_description: The Early Childhood Care and Education Policy 2018 also covers additional ECCE services for ages 0-8 (including lower primary).

reform_title: The Performance Management Guidelines for Schools and Tertiary Institutions 2020
reform_description: State school teachers are additionally subject to the Performance Management Guidelines for Schools and Tertiary Institutions 2020 (which do not apply to non-state schools).

reform_title: Establishment of the Private Schools and Institutions Department
reform_description: The Private Schools and Institutions Department was established to cater for the vibrant and ever-expanding private investment in education and foster Public Private Partnership in the provision of quality education and sports.

Promoting Equality in African Schools (PEAS) schools are low-fee secondary schools which are owned by the UK-based charity PEAS and established (since 2008) in rural and under-served communities in Uganda in partnership with the Ministry of Education and Sports as part of the USE program. These schools aim to enhance access to secondary education to students of lower socioeconomic status who did not manage to achieve the high scores required in the Primary Leaving Examination (PLE) to be offered a place in the USE program. PEAS schools are run and managed by local communities and funded mainly by PEAS, the Government of Uganda, and national or international donors.

According to the Early Childhood Care and Education Policy 2018, the MoES will be the leading agency in the delivery of ECCE, with services being established in accordance with three levels (day care, pre-primary, and lower primary services), covering ages 0-3, 3-6, and 6-8 respectively.

Teachers in state schools are considered public officers under the Constitution, the administration and conditions of service of which are regulated separately in the Constitution (which do not apply to non-state school teachers).

Government program implemented in 1997 that abolished tuition fees in primary state schools and extended statutory grants to privately owned primary schools that joined the program, making primary education tuition-free and expanding government funding to non-state schools.

reform_title: Local Government's Act 243 – Devolution of Education Services
reform_description: National legislation adopted in 1997 devolving the management and provision of education services to local governments, making regional governments and district or urban councils directly responsible for the supervision, management, and regulation of both state and non-state education actors within their jurisdictions.

reform_title: Universities and Other Tertiary Institutions Act 2001
reform_description: National legislation establishing the National Council for Higher Education (NCHE) as a statutory body corporate responsible for regulating the establishment, licensing, accreditation, and quality assurance of all state and non-state tertiary education institutions in Uganda.

Government program introduced in 2007 providing tuition-free places in secondary state and non-state schools to students from more disadvantaged backgrounds, with non-state schools receiving statutory grants covering tuition fees, staff salaries, and educational material upon joining the program.
A Ministry of Education and Sports circular issued in 2007 officially banning 'holiday coaching' (private tutoring during school holidays) in all schools, whether state, aided, or private.

reform_title: Education (Pre-Primary Primary and Post-Primary) Act of 2008
reform_description: National legislation establishing a legal framework for education governance, defining categories of public, government grant-aided, and private institutions, setting compulsory primary education for ages 6 and above, regulating registration and licensing of non-state schools from pre-primary to upper secondary level, and establishing the Directorate of Education Standards for school inspection.

reform_title: Establishment of the Private Schools and Institutions Department
reform_description: Creation in 2008 of a government department under the Ministry of Education and Sports directly responsible for the coordination, registration, and general administration of private educational institutions from pre-primary to secondary level, and for managing public-private partnership programs.

reform_title: Education Service (Teacher's Professional Code of Conduct) 2012
reform_description: National code of conduct applying to all teaching personnel in both state and non-state schools, regulating professional behavior including compliance with regulations on private tutoring and coaching, and prohibiting conducting business on school premises during official duty.

reform_title: Guidelines for Establishing Licensing Registering and Classification of Private Schools/Institutions in Uganda (2014)
reform_description: National guidelines issued in 2014 establishing detailed criteria and procedures for the registration, licensing, and classification of private schools and institutions from pre-primary to upper secondary level, including standards for water and sanitation, infrastructure, and school management.

National legislation establishing the Higher Education Students Financing Board (HESFB) to provide loans and scholarships to eligible Ugandan students pursuing tertiary education in registered state and non-state institutions, with eligibility determined by region, gender, socioeconomic need, and equity.

reform_title: Circular No. 2/2015 – Ban on Corporal Punishment in Schools
reform_description: A government circular banning all acts of violence against children in all educational institutions, whether government-aided, community, or private, applying across all schools and colleges nationwide.

reform_title: Education and Sports Sector Strategic Plan 2017/18–2019/20
reform_description: A national sector strategic plan aiming to enhance the contribution of non-state actors in education, recognizing the complementary role of the private sector in achieving equitable access to education, and guiding sector governance and public-private partnerships.

reform_title: Early Childhood Care and Education Policy 2018
reform_description: National policy designating the Ministry of Education and Sports as the leading agency for ECCE delivery, establishing a three-level framework for ECCE services covering ages 0–3 (day care), 3–6 (pre-primary), and 6–8 (lower primary), and setting registration and minimum standards requirements for ECCE providers.

reform_title: Guidelines for Reopening of Educational Institutions During COVID-19 (2020)
reform_description: Government guidelines issued in 2020 requiring all primary and secondary schools, including non-state schools, to comply with COVID-19 safety protocols upon reopening, including establishing school-level COVID-19 task forces, teacher training on health measures, and prohibition of fee increases.
Abolished tuition fees in primary state schools and began providing statutory state grants to non-state schools to expand access.
Devolved the management, provision, and regulation of education services to local governments such as regional, district, and urban councils.

reform_title: Universities and Other Tertiary Institutions Act
reform_description: Regulates the establishment and quality of all tertiary education institutions and created the National Council for Higher Education (NCHE) to oversee registration and accreditation.
Regulates the administration and conditions of service for public officers and teachers in state schools.
Provided tuition-free places in state and non-state secondary schools to disadvantaged students who achieve required primary examination scores.
An official ban issued by the Ministry of Education and Sports prohibiting all state, aided, and private schools from providing private tutoring lessons during school holidays.
Comprehensive legislation regulating public and private educational institutions, making primary education universal and compulsory, and setting registration and licensing requirements for schools.

reform_title: Establishment of the Private Schools and Institutions Department
reform_description: Created under the Ministry of Education and Sports to coordinate, register, and administer private educational institutions from pre-primary to secondary levels.

reform_title: Education Service (Teacher’s Professional Code of Conduct)
reform_description: Established a professional code of conduct applying to all teaching personnel, regulating professional behavior and prohibiting private business transactions while on official duty.

reform_title: Guidelines for Establishing, Licensing, Registering and Classification of Private Schools/Institutions
reform_description: Introduced detailed national criteria for private schools, encompassing infrastructure, sanitation, and licensing standards.
Established the Higher Education Students Financing Board (HESFB) to provide loans and scholarships to eligible students pursuing tertiary education in registered institutions.
Banned all acts of violence against children, outlawing corporal punishment across all government-aided, community, and private educational institutions.

reform_title: Early Childhood Care and Education Policy
reform_description: Designated the Ministry of Education and Sports as the lead agency for early childhood education delivery and categorized services into three levels covering ages zero to eight.

reform_title: Guidelines for Reopening of Educational Institutions During COVID-19
reform_description: Mandated the establishment of school COVID-19 task forces, teacher training on health protocols, and banned tuition fee increases without consultation during the pandemic reopening phase.

reform_title: Performance Management Guidelines for Schools and Tertiary Institutions
reform_description: Introduced new performance management regulations governing the administration and evaluation of state school teachers.

reform_title: Handbook on Teacher/Instructor/Tutor Education and Training Policies
reform_description: Mandated that teaching staff in all educational institutions be monitored and evaluated every term or semester.

reform_title: Constitutional provisions on education and non-state education providers
reform_description: The Constitution assigned responsibility for compulsory primary education to the state while allowing individuals, religious bodies and nongovernmental organizations to found and operate educational institutions subject to national education policy and standards.
Government program abolishing tuition fees in primary state schools and establishing universal primary education for pupils aged six and above.

The Local Governments Act devolved management and provision of education services to local governments, making regional governments and district or urban councils responsible for supervising, managing and regulating state and non-state education actors within their jurisdictions.

reform_title: Universities and Other Tertiary Institutions Act
reform_description: Higher education law establishing the National Council for Higher Education as the statutory body regulating the establishment, registration, monitoring and quality of state and private tertiary education institutions.
Law regulating the administration and conditions of service of teachers in state schools as public officers.
Ministry of Education and Sports circular prohibiting state, government-aided and private schools from providing private tutoring lessons during holidays.
Government program providing tuition-free places in secondary state and non-state schools, primarily for students from disadvantaged backgrounds.

reform_title: Education Act for pre-primary primary and post-primary education
reform_description: National law governing public, government-aided, private and international institutions from pre-primary through upper secondary education, including compulsory primary education, registration of non-state institutions, fee approval, admission rules, school governance bodies, teacher registration and inspection.

Creation of a Ministry of Education and Sports department responsible for coordinating, registering and administering private educational institutions from pre-primary to secondary level and managing public-private partnership programs.

reform_title: Education Service Teachers Professional Code of Conduct
reform_description: Professional code applying to teaching personnel in state and non-state schools, including rules on private instruction, coaching and teacher conduct while on official duty.

reform_title: Guidelines for private school licensing registration and classification
reform_description: National guidelines setting establishment, licensing, registration and classification requirements for private schools and institutions, including infrastructure, sanitation, land, classroom, documentation and development-plan standards.

Law establishing the Higher Education Students Financing Board to provide loans and scholarships to eligible Ugandan students pursuing tertiary education in registered state and non-state institutions.

reform_title: Circular No. 2/2015 ban on violence in educational institutions
reform_description: Ministry circular banning all acts of violence against children in schools, institutes and colleges, applying to government-aided, community and private educational institutions.

reform_title: Early Childhood Care and Education Policy
reform_description: Policy making the Ministry of Education and Sports the lead agency for ECCE delivery and structuring services into day care, pre-primary and lower primary levels covering children from ages zero to eight.

reform_title: Guidelines for reopening educational institutions during COVID-19
reform_description: National operational guidelines requiring educational institutions reopening after COVID-19 closures to establish school COVID-19 task forces, train teachers on control measures, prevent fee increases and implement standard operating procedures.

reform_title: Home-based learning continuity program during COVID-19
reform_description: Ministry of Education and Sports program to support continuity of learning during school closures through free self-study packages, radio lessons, television lessons and online uploads for students across Uganda.

reform_title: Performance Management Guidelines for Schools and Tertiary Institutions
reform_description: Guidelines applying performance management requirements to teachers in state schools and tertiary institutions.

reform_title: Teacher Instructor Tutor Education and Training Policies Handbook
reform_description: Policy handbook requiring teaching staff in all institutions to be monitored every term or semester.
